prologue:
Prologue

Welcome to the Complete User Manual for the Infinite Outcome Trade Simulator. This document is the deep-dive reference — a comprehensive guide to every setting, every statistic, and every concept that shapes how the indicator works.

Before You Begin

This manual is the deep dive. It assumes you've already read the Quick Start Guide (which covers first-time setup, asset class presets, and getting the Insight Monitor running) and the Walkthrough Guide (which gives a section-by-section overview of what each settings group controls).

If you haven't read those yet, start there. They'll give you the foundational understanding you need to get value from this document. This manual picks up where those left off — it's for when you're past the basics and want to dig deeper into the logic behind each setting, understand its impacts on trade setups and Insight Monitor results, and explore how it connects to other settings across the indicator.

Because almost everything in the indicator is customisable. Settings are stackable, chainable, and compoundable. A single change in one group can ripple through entry placement, stop loss behaviour, target calculation, partial profit levels, and ultimately every stat on the Insight Monitor. That's not a side effect — it's the entire design philosophy.

Every setting you touch opens a new branch of possibilities, and the Insight Monitor tells you whether that branch leads somewhere profitable.

Why Infinite Outcomes?

Five ICT trading models, each with configurable entry levels (25+ Fibonacci options plus manual input), seven target methods, eleven stop loss methods, four partial profit split strategies, target and stop loss modifiers that stack multiplicatively, trend filters, divergence filters, R:R filters, dollar and tick caps, killzone and macro time windows, and independent configuration for longs and shorts.

The number of unique setup combinations is, for all practical purposes, infinite. And every single one of them produces a different set of results on the Insight Monitor.

This manual documents every one of those settings and every one of those results.

How to Use This Manual

📖 AS A REFERENCE
Use the sidebar to jump directly to any setting group, Monitor chapter, workflow, or FAQ entry. Use the search box to find mentions of any term across every chapter. Every group cross-references its interacting groups and explains the ripple effects of its settings.
🎯 AS A WORKFLOW COMPANION
Part 3 (Workflows) walks you through complete tuning sessions — from first open of the indicator to a profitable baseline to regime-specific refinement. Start there if you want a guided path rather than a reference sprint.
🧭 AS A SOURCE OF TRUTH
When something on the chart doesn't behave the way you expect, the answer is in here. The Deep Dive drawers on every settings page cover the how and why — not just what each option does but what it does at the level of algorithm.

Two Modes: Quick and Full

Every settings chapter has a Quick / Full toggle in the top-right corner. Quick mode keeps deep-dive drawers collapsed — you see the setting table, callouts, and interactive widgets only. Full mode expands all drawers so the entire chapter reads as continuous prose.

Use Quick mode for scanning. Use Full mode for deep study. Your preference is remembered across the whole manual.

Interactive Content

Certain chapters include interactive demonstrations — flagged with a pulsing purple LIVE DEMO / TOGGLE / DROPDOWN / CALCULATOR badge. These are not decorative. They exist because some concepts are faster to understand through play than prose.

Examples: the Fibonacci entry-level visualiser in Group 6, the SL Method Explorer in Group 11, the Killzone and Macro timelines in Groups 29 and 30, and the Leverage/Margin Calculator in Group 4. Click, drag, select — the documentation responds to your input.

A Note on Scope

This manual documents the indicator. It does not teach you how to trade. ICT methodology, risk management principles, psychological discipline, and the thousand other skills that make a trader are beyond its scope.

What it can do is help you read the evidence the indicator produces clearly, change configurations deliberately, and understand exactly what each change will do before you make it. The rest is up to you.

♾️ THE THESIS
Trade with data. Trade with conviction. Trade your way.
intro:
FRONT MATTER

Foundations

This chapter exists so everything in the rest of the manual makes sense. It covers what the indicator is, what it isn't, the ICT terminology the rest of the manual uses freely, and the core concepts that shape how settings interact.

If you're an experienced ICT trader, you may already know most of what's covered here — feel free to skim or skip ahead. But if terms like displacement, fair value gap, or PD array are new or fuzzy, read carefully. The time you invest here will pay for itself many times over when you reach the settings reference.

What This Indicator Actually Does

The Infinite Outcome Trade Simulator scans historical price data on your TradingView chart, identifies trade setups based on five ICT entry models, calculates where entry, take profit, and stop loss would be for each setup based on your configuration, simulates what would have happened if every one of those trades had been taken, and presents the collective results as statistics on the Insight Monitor.

It does this automatically, across every qualifying setup in your scan range, instantly. What would take weeks of manual bar-replay backtesting happens in the time it takes TradingView to load the chart.

What It Doesn't Do

⚠️ BE CLEAR ABOUT LIMITATIONS
It does not predict the future. It does not guarantee profits. It does not place orders on your exchange. It does not tell you whether the next trade will win or lose. It cannot account for slippage, exchange fees, funding rates, or emotional decision-making. Simulated fills assume the limit order fills at the exact price — in reality, you might be filled worse during fast moves.

The Insight Monitor shows you how a specific configuration performed across a specific set of historical data. If the data says your setup had a 58% win rate and a 1.7 Profit Factor over 1,000 bars, that's a factual statement about the past — not a guarantee about the next 1,000.

A setup that has been consistently profitable across hundreds of trades, across multiple lookback windows, and across different timeframes is far more likely to have a genuine edge than one you've never tested. The indicator doesn't give you certainty — it gives you evidence. What you do with that evidence is your responsibility.

ICT Terminology — The Essentials

Deep dive · click any setting
▶DisplacementHOW & WHY

A candle where the close exceeds the prior candle's high (bullish) or undercuts the prior candle's low (bearish), with strong body-to-body momentum. The raw signal that institutional flow has entered the market.

Why it matters: Every other ICT pattern in the indicator is downstream of displacement. An FVG exists because a displacement created it. An OB is defined relative to the displacement that followed it. Without displacement, there is no structural move to trade.

▶Fair Value Gap (FVG)HOW & WHY

A three-candle pattern where a gap exists between candle 1's high and candle 3's low (bullish) or candle 1's low and candle 3's high (bearish), after a bullish or bearish displacement.

What it represents: Unfilled price delivery. Price moved so quickly from candle 1 to candle 3 that the middle zone was left behind as an imbalance. The market often returns to fill this gap before continuing.

How the indicator uses it: FVGs are one of the five entry models (Group 6). The gap itself becomes the entry zone; price retracing into the gap triggers a simulated fill.

▶Inversion FVG (i-FVG)HOW & WHY

An FVG that has been violated — price closed through the full gap in the opposite direction. The original thesis is invalidated. The indicator now treats the violated FVG as an inverted entry zone.

Classical ICT interpretation: The original bullish FVG failed, so buyers who entered there are now underwater. When price returns, they'll exit at cost, creating supply. The i-FVG becomes a short entry zone.

Expiry: i-FVGs don't live forever. Group 16 controls how many bars an unfilled i-FVG can wait before retiring.

▶Order Block (OB)HOW & WHY

The candle immediately before a displacement. Bullish OB = the last down-close before a bullish displacement. Bearish OB = the last up-close before a bearish displacement.

What it represents: The final institutional position-building candle before the real move began. Classical ICT theory: when price returns to this zone, institutional flow re-engages.

Range definition: The OB can be defined by its full wick range (wider, more retracement-tolerant) or its body range (tighter, more precise). Group 16 controls which.

▶Breaker BlockHOW & WHY

An Order Block that has been broken — price displaced through it in the opposite direction. A failed OB is no longer support/resistance; it flips to the opposite role.

Example: A bearish OB expected to act as resistance gets displaced through upward. It's now a Breaker Block — expected to act as support on the next retracement.

▶PD ArrayHOW & WHY

Premium and Discount Array — ICT's umbrella term for any structural feature where price 'delivers' from one state to another. FVGs, OBs, Breakers, swing highs/lows, wickless highs/lows, liquidity pools — all are PD arrays.

In the indicator: Groups 8, 10, and 12 let you choose which array types are considered for target placement, partial placement, and SL placement respectively. The full list: FVG, i-FVG, OB, Breaker, Wickless, Tailless, Swing H/L, BSL/SSL, HTF IPDA, HTF FVG, HTF OB.

▶BOS and MSSHOW & WHY

Break of Structure (BOS): Price breaks a swing high in an uptrend (or a swing low in a downtrend). Continuation signal.

Market Structure Shift (MSS): Price breaks a swing in the opposite direction of the prevailing trend. A bullish MSS occurs when an uptrend-series-ending swing high is broken after a sequence of lower highs — signalling potential reversal.

Detection: Both rely on what counts as a swing, which is set by Swing Strength in Group 5. A low Swing Strength detects small moves as BOS/MSS; a high Swing Strength only registers major ones.

▶BSL and SSLHOW & WHY

Buy-Side Liquidity (BSL): Equal highs — multiple swing highs at nearly the same price level. Resting stop orders from sellers sit just above. When price takes out the level, it's called a 'sweep' and often marks a reversal point.

Sell-Side Liquidity (SSL): The mirror. Equal lows with resting stop orders from buyers sitting just below.

As targets: Liquidity pools are some of the most reliable target magnets in ICT methodology. When enabled in Group 8, the indicator can place TPs at the next BSL/SSL level.

▶Killzones and MacrosHOW & WHY

Killzones: Multi-hour sessions where ICT methodology identifies concentrated institutional activity — London, NY AM, NY PM, etc. Group 29 lets you restrict detection to enabled killzones.

Macros: Narrower 20-to-30-minute windows within killzones where specific institutional events (rebalancing, options exercise, settlement) concentrate flow. Group 30 lets you restrict detection to enabled macros.

Time is more important than price. Filtering by killzone or macro often moves Profit Factor more than any price-based filter.

The Insight Monitor

Every configuration decision you make shows up on the Insight Monitor — the on-chart statistics table. The Monitor has six pages (Group 1 selects which is visible at any time):

PageWhat It Shows
Full StatsEvery metric the indicator tracks — Long, Short, and Total columns. Used for deep optimisation.
OverviewCompact summary. Win Rate, Net PnL, Peak Max DD, Profit Factor. Daily snapshot view.
Day AnalysisPerformance broken down by day of the week — which days carry your edge.
Hour AnalysisPerformance broken down by hour of the day — pair with killzones and macros.
Recent TradesChronological list of recent trade outcomes.
Model LeaderboardRanks all ten model/direction combos by Profit Factor. The pruning tool.

How Settings Interact

No setting lives in isolation. Entry Level (Group 6) affects the chart anchor for Target Method (Group 7) which feeds Target Array Depth (Group 7) which is influenced by the Target PD Arrays matrix (Group 8). Every change ripples. The indicator's settings pane ordering reflects this — core decisions are made first; modifiers and filters layer on top.

Every chapter in Part 1 lists its interactions — the other groups that affect or are affected by its settings. Read the interactions as carefully as the settings themselves.

♾️ LET'S BEGIN
Use the sidebar to jump to any chapter. Use the search box to find any term. Use the Quick/Full toggle to adjust depth. Every question about this indicator is answered somewhere in here.

GROUP 1

Insight Monitor & Output

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 1. INSIGHT MONITOR & OUTPUT ■

The first group in the settings pane. Controls the most visible feature of the indicator — the Insight Monitor dashboard. Despite containing only two settings, this group determines what you see and which view of the data you're looking at. Nothing in this group affects what the simulator detects or how trades behave.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Show Trade InsightsON / OFFMaster visibility toggle for the entire Insight Monitor dashboard. Simulation continues either way — this only controls whether the table is drawn.
Select MonitorFull Stats / Overview / Day Analysis / Hour Analysis / Recent Trades / Model LeaderboardWhich Monitor page is currently displayed. All pages share the same underlying dataset — switching pages just changes the report view.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Show Trade InsightsHOW & WHY

How it works: Master visibility toggle for the entire Insight Monitor dashboard. When ON, the stats table is drawn at the position and size you've configured in Group 31. When OFF, the table is completely removed from the chart.

Important — turning this OFF does NOT stop the simulation. The indicator continues to detect setups, track active trades, close trades at TP/SL/EOD, and compute every statistic in the background. The only thing that changes is whether the results table is drawn.

Workflow use: Keep it ON during analysis and setup refinement — you need the data visible to make informed decisions. Turn it OFF during live trading once your setup is tuned, to reclaim chart space and reduce visual noise while reading price action.

Pairs well with alerts: If you use alerts (Group 45), the Monitor becomes less necessary during active trading — alerts notify you of detections and entries while the chart stays clean.

▶Select Monitor — The Six PagesHOW & WHY

How it works: Selects which Monitor page is displayed. Only one page visible at a time. All pages draw from the same computed dataset — switching doesn't recalculate anything, it just chooses which report to render.

Full Stats — The deepest diagnostic view. Every trade metric split by Long / Short / Total. The go-to page during optimisation. Combined with Group 32's row toggles, you can build a compact personalised version of this page by hiding rows you don't need.

Overview — Compact summary card. Win Rate, Net PnL, Peak Max DD, Detection count, Profit Factor. Good for pre-session snapshots and live-trade reads when you don't need the whole stat suite.

Day Analysis — Win rate and performance broken down by day of week. Tells you which days carry your edge on the current asset.

Hour Analysis — Same breakdown but by hour of the day. Critical for session-based tuning — pair with Killzone filters (Group 29) to narrow trading to profitable hours.

Recent Trades — Chronological list of the most recent trades with their outcomes. Useful for eyeballing recent behaviour and spotting streaks.

Model Leaderboard — Ranks all ten model/direction combinations (5 models × Long/Short) by Profit Factor. Essential for pruning underperforming models from the detection engine.

Monitor Pages — Quick Reference

PageBest Used ForKey Metrics Shown
Full StatsOptimisation, deep diagnosticsEvery metric, Long / Short / Total columns
OverviewPre-session snapshot, live trade readsWin Rate, Net PnL, Peak DD, Detections, Profit Factor
Day AnalysisDay-of-week edge identificationPer-day Win Rate, Net PnL, trade count
Hour AnalysisIntraday session tuningPer-hour Win Rate, Net PnL, trade count

Model LeaderboardModel pruning decisionsPer-model Profit Factor, ranked
💡 KEY INSIGHT
Use Group 32's row toggles to build your own compact Full Stats view — hide every metric you don't actively monitor. It becomes a personalised mini-monitor, combining Full Stats' diagnostic depth with Overview's screen efficiency.
🎯 PRO TIP
Many traders cycle: Full Stats before the session to confirm the setup is healthy → Overview during active trading for minimal distraction → Model Leaderboard at week's end to decide whether to disable underperformers.

GROUP 2

Chart View — Quick Switches

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 2. CHART VIEW: QUICK SWITCHES ■

Quick Switches control which categories of trade visuals are drawn on the chart. Purely visual — nothing in this group affects what the simulator detects, how trades behave, or what the Insight Monitor calculates. They exist to let you control chart clutter and focus on what matters at any given moment.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Show DetectedON / OFFPending setups where entry price hasn't been reached. Drawn as limit-order-style lines with the 🎱 emoji.
Show ActiveON / OFFOpen trades being tracked against TP/SL/partials/trail. Entry label shows real-time PnL.
Show All ClosedON / OFFAll completed trades. High-volume — the most cluttered option. Typically used only during review.
Show MissedON / OFFSetups where TP was hit before entry filled. Marked ⛔️ Missed.
Show WinsON / OFFOnly trades closed in profit. Useful for pattern-study of winning setups.
Show LossesON / OFFOnly trades closed at SL. Useful for SL-placement analysis.
Show 2nd ChanceON / OFFSetups that were re-detected after an initial miss. Only meaningful if 2nd Chance is enabled in Group 5.
Show PartialsON / OFFTP1/TP2/TP3 partial-profit lines and labels on active trades.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶How the Quick Switches workHOW & WHY

All eight toggles are purely visual. Nothing here affects detection, trade behaviour, or the Insight Monitor. They exist so you can control chart clutter and focus on the information that matters to you at any given moment.

Colour and style customisation: Every colour, label size, ray width, and line style referenced in this group is the default. All are fully customisable in Group 42: Visuals — Trade Labels & Rays.

Trade visibility limits: The maximum number of trades drawn in each category is capped by Group 43: Visibility Limits. If you have more trades than the limit, only the most recent are shown.

▶Show DetectedHOW & WHY

What it shows: Pending trade setups where the entry price has not yet been reached. Each draws three lines (entry, TP, SL) with rays extending right and labels showing direction, model, Trade ID, and entry price. The 🎱 emoji (magic eight ball — asking the future) marks each one.

Critical note: Setups are detected whether you can see them or not. Turning this OFF doesn't prevent detection; it just hides pending setups from the chart. If price hits a hidden entry, the trade still transitions to Active.

Cap: Max Detected in Group 43 (default 30). Most-recent shown first.

▶Show ActiveHOW & WHY

What it shows: Open trades being actively managed. Draws entry/TP/SL lines with labels; the entry label's PnL figure updates in real time as price moves, showing total trade value including secured partials plus remaining unrealised PnL.

Lifecycle emoji change: When a Detected setup's entry fills, its emoji changes from 🎱 (prediction) to 🤞🏻 (fingers crossed — it's live). Trailing stops move the SL line in real time. When TP1 Secure triggers, the SL label updates to show 'B.E.'.

Typically always ON. The only reason to disable is when reviewing only historical trades and active visuals are in the way.

▶Show All ClosedHOW & WHY

What it shows: Every trade that has completed — wins, losses, missed, and EOD-closed. The most visually dense option. Labels are retained from the original setup with an outcome emoji prepended (✅ / ❌ / ⛔️ / ⏰).

When to enable: Post-session review or full-historical analysis. Turn OFF during live trading — the density obscures active and detected setups you actually need to see.

Don't pair with: Show Wins + Show Losses simultaneously. That produces duplicate visuals for every closed trade.

▶Show MissedHOW & WHY

What it shows: Setups where TP was reached before entry filled. Marked with ⛔️ Missed. Helps you see the cost of strict entry levels — high Missed count visible on chart is a signal to loosen entry requirements.

Use with: The Missed Entries and Missed PnL rows on Full Stats. The chart visualisation makes the abstract numbers concrete.

▶Show Wins / Show LossesHOW & WHY

Show Wins: Only trades closed in profit. Valuable for pattern-study — what do the winning setups look like? Which sessions? Which models? What structural conditions were present?

Show Losses: Only trades closed at SL. Valuable for SL-placement analysis — are your SLs being tagged by noise or by genuine reversals? Do losing setups cluster around specific HTF structure?

Combine for pattern recognition: Enable both simultaneously (but not alongside Show All Closed). Colour-code them differently in Group 42 and you'll see the visual separation between winning and losing areas of the chart.

▶Show 2nd ChanceHOW & WHY

What it shows: Setups that were originally missed and re-detected on a subsequent bar. Only meaningful when 2nd Chance Setups is enabled in Group 5.

Why view them separately: 2nd Chance trades often have different characteristics to first-touch trades. Isolating them visually helps confirm whether the 2nd Chance mechanic is finding genuine opportunities or just adding noise.

▶Show PartialsHOW & WHY

What it shows: TP1, TP2, and TP3 partial-profit lines and labels on active trades. Each partial draws at its calculated level with a distinct label showing the partial number and dollar value.

When to enable: Any time you're running partials and want to see where they're landing. Particularly valuable when experimenting with Partial Split Method (Group 9) to confirm the levels match your intent.

When to disable: When running four or more active trades simultaneously — the partials layer gets busy fast.

⚠️ WATCH OUT
Don't enable Show All Closed at the same time as Show Wins and Show Losses — you'll get duplicate labels and lines for every closed trade. Pick one category-set at a time.
🎯 PRO TIP
During live trading, keep only Show Detected and Show Active ON. During session review, turn those OFF and enable Show All Closed. Save two chart layouts — 'Live' and 'Review' — to switch between them in one click.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
None of these toggles affect statistics. Every outcome is counted and every stat is computed regardless of visibility. These are purely chart-noise controls.
g3:
GROUP 3

Live Detection & Backtest Engine

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 3. LIVE DETECTION & BACKTEST ENGINE ■

This group controls the scope and method of the simulation — how much data the simulator scans and how it calculates aggregate statistics. While Group 1 controls what you see, Group 3 controls what the simulator processes. Every trade detected, every stat calculated, and every number on the Monitor is bounded by the settings here.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Sim ModeLookback / Custom Date RangeHow the simulator selects which bars to scan. Lookback is a rolling window; Date Range is anchored to specific dates.
Avg / MedianAverage / MedianHow aggregate metrics are calculated. Average is sensitive to outliers; Median resists them.
Bars To Scan20 – 5,000Size of the rolling lookback window. The most impactful setting on the Monitor — every stat is calculated from trades within this window.
Trades To Track10 – 5,000Maximum number of trades held in memory. When full, oldest trades are discarded.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Sim ModeHOW & WHY

Lookback Period: Scans the most recent N bars from the current bar backwards, where N is set by Bars To Scan. Creates a rolling window that always includes the latest data and moves forward as new bars form.

Why rolling matters: Your stats always reflect current conditions. But this also means stats shift over time even if no settings change — because the underlying dataset is constantly updating.

Custom Date Range: Scans between two fixed dates set in Group 44. The window doesn't move — it's anchored to specific calendar dates regardless of how many bars are on the chart.

When Custom Date Range wins: Event-specific analysis. Set the range to cover the last three FOMC announcements, or a known trending week, or a consolidation phase — and you get a targeted read of how your setup performed during those exact conditions.

▶Average vs MedianHOW & WHY

Average (arithmetic mean): Sensitive to outliers. A single massive win or loss shifts the figure significantly. Gives you the mathematical expected value of your results.

Median (middle value): Resistant to outliers. Tells you what a 'typical' trade looks like.

Read both: If your Average PnL is significantly higher than your Median PnL, a small number of large winners are carrying performance. That's useful information — but also a risk flag if those large winners become rarer in current conditions.

▶Bars To Scan — the most powerful dial in the indicatorHOW & WHY

Why it matters so much: Price action cycles through distinct phases — contraction, expansion, trending, mean-reverting. A 5,000-bar lookback averages across ALL phases, telling you how a setup performs in general. A 200–500 bar lookback tells you how it performs RIGHT NOW.

The bracketing strategy: Start at maximum (5,000 bars) to establish a baseline. Then drop progressively — 2,500 → 1,000 → 500 → 300 — and watch Profit Factor. Where it stabilises or peaks is your setup's sweet spot for current conditions.

Minimum meaningful sample: About 100 trades. Profit Factor calculated from 15 trades is not statistically meaningful — a few lucky or unlucky outcomes disproportionately skew everything.

Chart impact: Changes how many trades are visible on chart. 200 on a 5-minute chart shows 20–30 trades; 5,000 shows hundreds.

▶Trades To TrackHOW & WHY

What it does: Caps how many trades can exist in the simulator's memory at once. When the cap is reached, the oldest trade is discarded as a new one is added.

Why it exists: Pine Script has finite memory. On very long lookbacks with high-detection setups, the sheer number of trades can exceed what the runtime can hold. Trades To Track prevents crashes.

How to set it: Default 5,000 is almost always enough. If you're seeing strange stat behaviour on a huge lookback and suspect old trades are being lost, check whether the total detected count is approaching the Trades To Track limit.

The Lookback Period is arguably your single most powerful tuning variable. 5,000 bars = all regimes. 300 bars = right now. Use both strategically. Start high for baseline, lower for current adaptation.
🧪 EXPERIMENT
Systematic bracketing: 5,000 → 2,500 → 1,000 → 500 → 300 → 200. Record Profit Factor at each stop. Plot mentally. The curve tells you whether your setup is benefiting from or being hurt by the current regime.
💡 GOOD TO KNOW
If Average PnL is significantly higher than Median PnL, a small number of large winners carry your performance. That's fine — but means a few missed winners in current conditions could tip the setup negative. Watch the median.
g4:
GROUP 4

Account & Risk Profiling

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 4. ACCOUNT & RISK PROFILING ■

This group forms the financial bridge between raw price movements on your chart and the real-world monetary values on your Insight Monitor. Previous groups dictate when and where a trade happens; Group 4 dictates how much it's worth. Misconfigure this and the simulator still detects trades perfectly, but PnL, Drawdown, and Net Return statistics become completely detached from your actual exchange account.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Global ValueDollars / TicksPrimary unit for all financial output across the Monitor and chart. Dollars = fiat currency; Ticks = pure chart distance.
Auto-Detect Point ValueON / OFFWhen ON, reads the exchange's native point value. Essential ON for CME futures (ES/NQ/CL/GC); essential OFF for crypto and equities.
Contracts / Qty0.01+Number of units traded. For futures, the contract count. For crypto with Auto-Detect OFF, leave at 1.0 — the simulator derives coin quantity automatically.
Margin ($)0.01+Your collateral. For futures, leave at 1.0 (point value handles sizing). For crypto/equities, enter the actual dollar amount allocated per trade.
Leverage (×)1.0+Exchange leverage multiplier. 1.0 for futures and spot. Match your exchange for crypto margin. All PnL flows from Buying Power = Margin × Leverage.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Global Value — Dollars vs TicksHOW & WHY

Dollars: Computes distances and PnL in fiat currency based on Margin × Leverage. The figures you see on the Monitor are what you'd actually win or lose on your real account.

Ticks: Bypasses margin and leverage entirely. Calculates pure physical chart distance (price movement divided by the asset's minimum tick size). Useful when you want to evaluate setups in direction-agnostic terms — comparing NQ to CL to ES on equal footing.

Key exception: Peak Max Realised and Peak Max DD always track in standard USD regardless of this setting. No matter what you trade, your account balance is measured in fiat — the Monitor respects that.

The power-user setup: Run two IOTSIM instances on the same chart — one in Dollars, one in Ticks. You get both perspectives simultaneously. See the Life Hacks appendix for the full workflow.

▶Auto-Detect Point ValueHOW & WHY

When ON: The simulator reads the exchange's native point value for the asset. Works perfectly for traditional futures where 1 contract has a fixed, universal multiplier. ES is $50/point, NQ is $20/point, CL is $1,000/point — Auto-Detect finds these from the ticker metadata.

When OFF: The simulator uses your Margin × Leverage inputs to calculate position sizing internally. Essential for cryptocurrency trading, where token prices vary wildly and '1 contract' means completely different things on different assets.

Quick decision: CME futures (ES/NQ/CL/GC/HG/ZB/ZN) → ON. Crypto → OFF. FX → OFF (broker metadata inconsistent). Equities → OFF. Commodities on CME (standardised contracts) → ON.

▶Margin, Leverage, and Contracts — the three financial inputsHOW & WHY

How they combine: Buying Power = Margin × Leverage. All PnL calculations flow from this buying power value. Contracts multiplies the final result — setting Contracts to 2 doubles every profit and loss figure.

Futures: Margin 1.0, Leverage 1.0, Contracts = number of contracts you trade. The point value from Auto-Detect handles everything else.

Crypto spot: Margin = actual dollar amount invested (e.g. 500 for $500 into ADA). Leverage 1.0. Contracts 1.0. The simulator uses Buying Power / Entry Price × Contracts to work out coin quantity.

Crypto isolated margin: Margin = collateral locked for the trade. Leverage = your exchange's leverage setting. Contracts 1.0. Example: $300 margin at 10× on ETH gives $3,000 notional position.

Equities: Margin = per-trade allocation. Leverage 1.0 for cash accounts, match broker's margin multiplier for margin accounts. Contracts 1.0.

Universal shortcut for crypto: If you don't want to think about margin and leverage separately, put your total intended position size in Margin and set Leverage to 1. The math is identical — $200 × 10× = $2,000 is the same as $2,000 × 1×.

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Trade DirectionHOW & WHY

How it works: The simulator has two parallel detection engines — one for longs, one for shorts — each pulling from its own configuration block (Groups 6–16 for longs, Groups 17–27 for shorts). Trade Direction gates which engine actually runs. Long Only silences the short engine entirely; Short Only silences the long engine.

Why you'd change it: Focused optimisation. When you're tuning a long-side configuration and you want a clean Full Stats read without short trades noising up the Total column, set it to Long Only. Switch to Short Only to do the same for shorts. Return to Both once each side is stable.

When to leave it on Both: All live-trading scenarios, and any time you want to see how the system behaves as a whole. The Long and Short columns on Full Stats give you direction-specific reads without needing to toggle this.

▶Setup MirroringHOW & WHY

How it works: When enabled, one direction's settings (Entry Level, Target Method, SL Method, modifiers, filters — everything in Groups 6–16 or 17–27) are continuously copied to the other direction in real time. Changes to the mirrored side silently take no effect — only the source side has control.

Why it exists: Configuring 22 setting groups from scratch for a new asset is slow. Most asymmetries between long and short performance are small enough that a mirrored configuration is a respectable baseline. Once you have a profitable mirrored setup, you can isolate the weaker direction and fine-tune it independently.

When to turn it off: When Full Stats shows a Profit Factor gap above about 0.5 between Long and Short columns, or when the columns have opposite-sign Net PnL. That's statistical evidence the asset behaves asymmetrically and deserves two separate configurations.

▶2nd Chance SetupsHOW & WHY

How it works: Normally, when a setup's TP is reached before its entry has filled, that setup is marked Missed (⛔️) and retired. With 2nd Chance ON, the simulator continues watching for the same structural pattern — a new displacement/FVG/OB forms near the missed entry zone — and re-registers it as a fresh setup eligible for a fill.

Why it helps on some assets: On volatile instruments (crypto, commodities), initial retracements often fail but deeper pullbacks succeed. The 2nd Chance mechanic captures these deeper-entry opportunities that would otherwise be lost. The 2nd Chance Win Rate row on Full Stats tells you whether those re-detections are profitable.

Why it can hurt on others: On disciplined trending instruments (index futures during NY AM), first-touch entries are typically highest-probability. Enabling 2nd Chance here adds marginal setups that dilute the edge. If 2nd Chance Win Rate is lower than the base Win Rate, the setting is costing you money.

Double TP Sweep: If a setup misses its fill a second time, it's permanently retired — the simulator doesn't keep looking forever.

▶EOD Auto-CloseHOW & WHY

How it works: On instruments with a defined session close, every active trade is force-closed at the final bar. The label shows the ⏰ EOD emoji and the trade is counted as a third outcome category — not a Win, not a Loss, just an EOD Close. Realised PnL at close still feeds Net PnL normally.

Why enable it for futures/equities: Overnight gaps can dramatically skew stats. A position left open through an overnight session might open the next day at a price far from where it closed — that's a realistic day-trading risk and closing at session end matches realistic day-trading behaviour.

Why disable for crypto: 24/7 markets have no meaningful EOD concept. If you leave it on, the simulator picks an arbitrary cutoff (typically 23:59 UTC) and force-closes every active trade — creating a wave of artificial EOD closures that suppress your real TP/SL stats.

▶HTF IPDA Levels & TimeframeHOW & WHY

How it works: IPDA (Interbank Price Delivery Algorithm) is an ICT concept describing the theoretical algorithm by which institutional flow delivers price between premium and discount regions. In practice, the indicator draws the 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100-period highs and lows from your selected HTF as horizontal projections — macro reference points that price is often drawn toward.

Why these specific periods: They're the standard ICT IPDA lookbacks. 20 is the short-term range, 40 is intermediate, 60/80/100 define the broader distribution. Together they map the structural premium/discount zones on the HTF.

Pairing guide: The HTF should be significantly larger than the chart's timeframe — otherwise the IPDA levels recompute too frequently to be meaningful. 5m chart → Daily IPDA, 15m chart → Daily IPDA, 1h chart → Weekly IPDA, Daily chart → Monthly IPDA.

▶Swing StrengthHOW & WHY

How it works: A swing high (or low) is identified when a pivot candle has N bars to its left AND N bars to its right where no bar exceeds the pivot's high (or undercuts its low). Swing Strength sets N. At N=5, every swing must have 5 confirmed bars on each side.

What it affects downstream: Every structural feature that references swings — BOS/MSS detection, BSL/SSL liquidity levels, the Swing H/L target method, the Swing H/L SL method, and the HTF IPDA range calculations. One setting, broad impact.

Low values (1–3): Many small swings detected. Tight stops, frequent signals, more noise in structural reads. Good for scalping.

Default (5): The ICT canonical value. Balanced for most intraday trading.

High values (10+): Only major pivots qualify as swings. Wider stops, fewer structural levels, more reliable reads. Good for swing trading and structural analysis.

▶HTF Scout (Filter + FVG + OB + OB Range)HOW & WHY

What the Scout is: A sandboxed higher-timeframe scanner that tracks the most recent unmitigated bullish and bearish FVG and OB on a configurable HTF. Separate from the main detection engine — it runs independently and produces visual overlays and optional filter data.

Filter Trades By HTF: When ON, only setups aligned with the Scout's most recent unmitigated PD array are detected. Longs require price to be above (or just touching) the most recent unmitigated bullish HTF FVG/OB; shorts need the mirror. Produces fewer but higher-quality detections.

Enable HTF FVG / Enable HTF OB: Independent visual toggles. Draw the HTF arrays on your chart as reference zones even if you don't use them for filtering. Useful context for reading whether price is inside a structurally important area.

HTF OB Range: Determines whether the HTF OB box covers the candle's full wick range (wider zone, more permissive) or just the body range (tighter zone, stricter entries). Bodies is more conservative; Wicks catches deeper retracements.

Long Setup — Core Entry

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 6. LONG SETUP: CORE ENTRY ■

Where you define what the simulator looks for and where it places the entry. Group 6 controls the long side; Group 17 controls the short side (or mirrors this group if Setup Mirroring is active in Group 5). Two decisions are being made here: which ICT model(s) to detect, and where within each detected model's range to place the simulated limit order.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Model SelectionDisplacement / FVG / i-FVG / OB / BreakerIndependent toggles for each of the 5 ICT entry models. Any combination allowed. All ON by default.
Entry Level0.0 – 1.0 / Manual / F.RunWhere within the PD array the entry sits, like a Fibonacci retracement inside the array. Default 0.34 — see deep dive for why this differs from the textbook 0.618 OTE.
Manual EntryAny floatCustom entry value when Entry Level is set to Manual Input. Lets you trial non-standard levels.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Model SelectionHOW & WHY

Five independent checkboxes: Displacement, FVG, Inversion FVG, Order Block, Breaker Block. You can enable any combination — one model, all five, or any mix. When all five are enabled this is functionally equivalent to 'All Models'.

Displacement — Detects bullish displacement candles (close exceeds prior candle's high with strong body-to-body momentum). The entry range is the displacement candle's body (open to close). This is the raw momentum signal. Displacements tend to have the widest entry ranges and the most aggressive deviation multipliers when using Auto Calc targets.

FVG (Fair Value Gap) — Detects bullish three-candle patterns where a gap exists between candle 1's high and candle 3's low after a bullish displacement. The entry range is the gap itself — the unfilled space between the two non-overlapping candles. FVGs represent undelivered price action the market is expected to return to. Tighter entry ranges than Displacements, often more precise entries.

Inversion FVG (i-FVG) — Detects FVGs that have been violated (price closed through the original FVG) and then inverted. A bearish FVG violated becomes a bullish i-FVG entry zone. Represents areas where the original narrative was invalidated and the market is now using the same zone in the opposite direction. Subject to i-FVG Expiry in Group 16.

Order Block — Detects the candle immediately before a displacement. The OB range (wicks or bodies, controlled by Group 16) becomes the entry zone. OBs represent the last point of institutional activity before the displacement move. Optional strict colour matching and liquidity sweep requirements available in Group 16.

Breaker Block — Detects Order Blocks that have been 'broken' — price displaced through the OB in the opposite direction. A bearish OB broken to the upside becomes a bullish Breaker entry zone. Represents failed institutional positions now expected to act as support. Subject to OB/BRK Expiry in Group 16.

▶Entry LevelHOW & WHY

How it works: Every detected setup creates a box on the chart (the model range). Entry Level determines where inside that box your entry sits. Expressed as a decimal between 0.0 and 1.0 (plus Front Run options outside that range), functioning like a Fibonacci retracement level within the PD array.

The shallow-vs-deep trade-off: Lower values place the entry near the top of the range (for longs) where price touches first on a retracement. Higher values place it deeper into the range where price must retrace further to reach. Shallower entries fill more often but give worse prices; deeper entries give better prices but fill less often.

What ICT actually defines as the OTE: The Optimal Trade Entry in ICT methodology is the 0.618 retracement — that's the textbook level. It's available here as a one-click preset, and it's the right choice for traders who want strict ICT methodology applied as written.

Why this indicator defaults to 0.34 instead: The five entry models (Displacement, FVG, i-FVG, OB, Breaker) all target a specific candle's range rather than the broader price-leg ranges that classical ICT OTE analysis is applied to. Inside an FVG or an OB, the 0.5 mid-point has structural meaning — it's the Consequent Encroachment of a gap and the Mean Threshold of an Order Block. Both 0.5 mid-points are key levels institutional flow is drawn to. By placing the default at 0.34, the indicator front-runs the 0.5 mid-point — your entry fills before price reaches the level the algo is most likely to react from. This trades a small amount of theoretical R:R for a meaningful improvement in fill rate.

It's a trader's choice, not a rule. The default is a starting point chosen for fill-rate balance. If you want strict ICT — set 0.618. If you want maximum fill rate — set 0.25 or use Front Run. If you want the institutional reaction price — set 0.5. The Insight Monitor will tell you which level your asset and timeframe actually reward; let the data decide rather than the default.

Front Run (±0.1425): Places the entry OUTSIDE the PD array (for longs, above the near edge; for shorts, below). Fills BEFORE price even enters the array. Maximum fill rate at the cost of theoretical R:R — you're chasing price rather than waiting for it.

The full preset list: The Entry Level dropdown contains 22 preset Fibonacci levels covering every classical retracement and extension plus several useful in-between values. Outside the array: F. Run (+0.1425). Inside the array, shallow → deep: 0.0 (Near Edge), 0.1, 0.1425, 0.214, 0.236, 0.25, 0.298, 0.34 (default), 0.361, 0.382, 0.44, 0.5 (Equilibrium), 0.56, 0.618 (ICT OTE), 0.639, 0.66, 0.702, 0.75, 0.786, 0.8575, 0.9, 1.0 (Far Edge). Beyond the array: F. Run (−0.1425). The dense spacing around the structurally significant points (0.34, 0.5, 0.618) lets you fine-tune around the critical zones where detection-vs-fill-rate trade-offs are most sensitive.

Manual Input: Use any decimal value not in the preset list. Useful for experimenting with non-standard retracement levels, or when Insight Monitor analysis suggests a specific in-between level optimises results.

▶How Model × Entry Level interactHOW & WHY

These two settings compound. The model determines the range boundaries; the Entry Level determines where within those boundaries you sit. Changing models changes the meaning of a given Entry Level.

Example: Entry Level 0.34 on a Displacement setup places the entry 34% down from the displacement candle's high. Entry Level 0.34 on an FVG setup places the entry 34% down from the FVG's top edge. Because the FVG is typically a smaller range than the Displacement body, the 0.34 FVG entry is an absolutely shallower retracement than the 0.34 Displacement entry.

Implication for optimisation: When switching models, expect Monitor stats to shift even if Entry Level is unchanged. The same Fib level produces different entry prices depending on which model's range is being sampled.

Long Setup — Core Targets

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 7. LONG SETUP: CORE TARGETS ■

How take-profit levels are calculated for long trades. The target method determines where the TP line is drawn, which directly affects potential reward, R:R ratio, and every profit-related stat on the Monitor. Group 18 is the short equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Target MethodAuto Calc / Fixed Devs / Dollar $ / Ticks / Wickless / Swing H/L / HTF / PD Array MatrixHow TP is calculated. Auto Calc uses displacement-based deviation multiplier. Structural methods find actual chart features. Fixed methods use user-specified distances.
Fixed Dev Multiplier0.5 – 10.0Manual deviation multiplier when Target Method is Fixed Devs. TP distance = multiplier × displacement candle body size.
Dollar TPAny $Fixed-dollar TP distance when Target Method is Dollar $.
Tick TPAny tick countFixed-tick TP distance when Target Method is Ticks.
Tgt Array Depth1 – 10For structural methods: which array to target. Depth 1 = nearest, depth 3 = skip first two and target the third. Higher = deeper, further-out targets.

Max Tgt DevsOff / Filter / ReducerMaximum deviation threshold. Filter rejects setups above it; Reducer caps TP down to it.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Target Method — the seven optionsHOW & WHY

Auto Calc: Default. TP is calculated as entry + (displacement candle body × deviation multiplier). The deviation is derived from the candle's momentum profile. The most adaptive method — targets scale naturally with volatility.

Fixed Devs: You specify the multiplier manually (e.g. 2.5×). TP = entry + (displacement body × your multiplier). Use when you have a specific deviation that works on your asset and you don't want the auto-calc varying it.

Dollar $ / Ticks: Fixed distances in absolute units. TP always lands the same dollar or tick amount from entry regardless of setup size. Best for scalping where you want consistent profit targets.

Wickless: TP lands at the nearest wickless candle high above entry (for longs). Wickless candles represent unfilled liquidity that price often revisits. Dynamic — the target adapts to chart structure.

Swing H/L: TP lands at the nearest prior swing high above entry (for longs). Swing Strength in Group 5 determines what counts as a swing.

HTF: TP lands at the nearest HTF IPDA level above entry. Requires HTF IPDA Levels ON in Group 5.

PD Array Matrix: TP lands at the nearest unmitigated PD array above entry. Which arrays count is controlled by Group 8. Tgt Array Depth picks which one (1st, 2nd, 3rd…).

▶Fixed Dev Multiplier — when to use itHOW & WHY

Why manual devs: Auto Calc's multiplier varies with candle momentum. For high-variance assets, this can produce wildly inconsistent targets. Fixed Devs gives you a single consistent multiplier across every setup.

Typical values: 1.5–2.0 for conservative targets with high hit rates. 2.5–3.5 for balanced. 4.0+ for ambitious, low-hit-rate targets.

How to find yours: Enable Auto Calc and check the deviation multiplier shown on your TP labels across 100+ trades. Average them. That average is your Fixed Dev starting point.

▶Target Array DepthHOW & WHY

What depth means: For structural target methods (Wickless, Swing H/L, HTF, PD Array Matrix), depth selects which instance of the structural feature to target. Depth 1 = nearest. Depth 2 = skip the nearest, target the next. Depth 5 = skip four, target the fifth.

Shallow vs deep targets: Depth 1 targets are close — high hit rate but limited R:R. Depth 5+ targets are far — low hit rate, substantial R:R when reached.

The Reducer modifier: If Max Tgt Devs is set to Reducer, a deep-depth target that exceeds Max Dev will be pulled back to the closest array that meets the threshold. You get the benefits of a deep scan with a safety net against unrealistic targets.

▶Min / Max Dev thresholdsHOW & WHY

Min Tgt Devs — Filter: Any setup whose natural target is below the minimum dev multiplier is rejected. The Missed Entries row stays clean; Detection count drops. Use when you only want setups with meaningful profit potential.

Min Tgt Devs — Increaser: Same as Filter, but instead of rejecting the setup, the TP is bumped up to meet the minimum. The label shows 🧢 to indicate a modifier has been applied.

Max Tgt Devs — Filter: Rejects setups whose natural target exceeds the maximum. Filters out unrealistically ambitious trades.

Max Tgt Devs — Reducer: Caps an oversized target down to the maximum. Gives you a ceiling on R:R without sacrificing the setup entirely.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Auto Calc is the right default for most configurations because it scales with momentum — quiet candles produce conservative targets, explosive candles produce ambitious ones. Switch to Fixed Devs only when you have a specific multiplier identified on your asset.

Target: HTF IPDAON / OFFInclude HTF IPDA levels (20/40/60/80/100-period highs and lows from the configured HTF).
Target: HTF FVGON / OFFInclude the most recent unmitigated HTF FVG (from the HTF Scout in Group 5).
Target: HTF OBON / OFFInclude the most recent unmitigated HTF OB (from the HTF Scout in Group 5).
Deep dive · click any setting
▶How the Target Array Matrix worksHOW & WHY

Only active when Target Method = PD Array Matrix (Group 7). If you're using any other Target Method, these toggles are inert — the simulator doesn't consult them.

The matrix logic: For each detected setup, the simulator scans all enabled array types in the direction of the trade, finds every unmitigated instance, sorts them by distance from entry, and picks the one at the configured Tgt Array Depth (Group 7). Depth 1 = nearest, Depth 5 = skip first four.

What enabling more arrays does: Expands the pool of possible targets. More arrays = denser target-scan = shorter typical target distances (because there are more candidates to choose from). Disabling array types spreads target candidates further apart — Targets land deeper but less often.

What disabling does: Forces the simulator to ignore that structural feature entirely. If you believe FVGs don't act as reliable targets on your asset, disable them — the simulator will find its Depth-1 target in a non-FVG structure instead.

▶Choosing which arrays to enableHOW & WHY

Start with all ON (default). Run 500+ trades. Check Profit Factor and Final TPs Hit on the Monitor.

If Final TPs Hit is low (under 30%): Your targets are too far. Try disabling the larger-scale arrays (HTF IPDA, Swing H/L, BSL/SSL) — this forces closer targets on average.

If Final TPs Hit is very high (over 70%) with low R:R: Your targets are too close. Enable more structural arrays (HTF IPDA, HTF FVG, HTF OB) to pull targets further out on average.

Liquidity-specific configuration: If you trade with the liquidity-sweep thesis, enable only Wickless + Tailless + BSL/SSL. The simulator targets only liquidity pools; nothing else. Compact, powerful structural setup for liquidity-sweep traders.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Enabling or disabling array types changes the shape of the target distribution, not just target counts. Before you tune array depth (Group 7) or modifiers (Group 13), get this matrix right — it's the foundation the depth selector operates on.
🧪 EXPERIMENT
Build two instances on the same chart. Instance A has all arrays ON. Instance B has only FVG + OB ON. Compare the Monitors. The difference reveals how much of your edge comes from the broader structural-target pool vs the core FVG/OB pair.

GROUP 9

Long Setup — Partial Profits

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 9. LONG SETUP: PARTIAL PROFITS ■

Partial profits let you close portions of your position at intermediate levels between entry and the final take profit. Rather than waiting for the full TP — which may not happen — you secure profit at structural or mathematical levels along the way. Group 20 is the short equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Enable PartialsON / OFFMaster toggle for partial-profit closures. When OFF, each trade runs as all-in / all-out between entry and final TP.
Partial Count1 – 3How many partial levels to place between entry and final TP. TP1 is always the nearest; TP3 is the deepest.
Split MethodEqual Distance / Early Offset / PD Arrays (Near) / PD Arrays (Grid)How the partial levels are spaced between entry and final TP. See drawer for each.
Early Offset %0 – 50%Shifts partials closer to entry when Split Method is Early Offset. 30% pulls TP1 30% closer to entry.
Size MethodEqual Size / Risk BackHow the position is split across partials. Risk Back weights TP1 to recover a multiple of the trade's risk.
Risk Back %100%+Proportion of trade risk recovered at TP1 when Size Method is Risk Back. 100% = breakeven on risk; 200% = recover 2R. Capped at 75% of position.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Enable Partials — why turn it on or offHOW & WHY

When ON: Every trade closes a portion of its position at TP1, TP2, and/or TP3 before the final TP. Each partial triggers its own emoji and PnL contribution to the Monitor.

When OFF: Pure all-in / all-out. Each trade either hits final TP (win), SL (loss), or EOD close. Cleaner diagnostic view but no intermediate profit-taking.

Diagnostic use of OFF: Temporarily disable partials when you want to see the raw performance of your Target Method, Entry Level, and SL placement. If the configuration is unprofitable without partials, partials are masking a deeper problem — fix entry/target/SL first.

▶Partial Count — 1, 2, or 3HOW & WHY

Partial Count 1: Single partial (TP1). Binary: either TP1 then final TP, or TP1 then SL/EOD. Simplest to analyse.

Partial Count 2: TP1 + TP2 + final. Middle ground.

Partial Count 3: TP1 + TP2 + TP3 + final. Maximum profit-averaging. Useful when target distances are large — gives multiple chances to secure gains along the way. Downside: the position size remaining at final TP is smaller, so the 'big TP' reward is less dramatic.

▶Split Method — the four algorithmsHOW & WHY

Equal Distance: Partials are evenly spaced between entry and final TP. Partial Count 3 with final TP at 3× means TP1 at 0.75×, TP2 at 1.5×, TP3 at 2.25×. Predictable; purely mathematical.

Early Offset: Same as Equal Distance but all partials are shifted closer to entry by the Early Offset percentage. At 30% offset, TP1 lands 30% closer to entry than Equal Distance would place it. Captures profit faster at the cost of leaving more upside on the table.

PD Arrays (Near): Partials land on the nearest structural PD arrays (from Group 10) between entry and final TP. Natural profit-taking zones — where liquidity is likely to react regardless of your plan.

PD Arrays (Grid): The simulator divides the entry-to-TP distance by Partial Count, then finds the nearest PD array at each division point. Evenly distributed like Equal Distance but snapped to real structure. The most adaptive option — different from Near on assets where structural features cluster near entry.

▶Size Method — how the position is splitHOW & WHY

Equal Size: Each partial closes an equal fraction of the position. Partial Count 3 → 25% at TP1, 25% at TP2, 25% at TP3, 25% at final TP.

Risk Back: TP1 is sized to close whatever fraction of position is needed to recover the specified multiple of risk. Risk Back 100% means TP1 closes enough position to break even on the trade's risk. Risk Back 200% closes enough to realise 2R at TP1 alone.

Why Risk Back is powerful: Your worst-case trade outcome changes dramatically once TP1 hits. If TP1 Secure is also enabled (Group 13), the SL moves to breakeven after TP1 — so a Risk Back trade that hits TP1 is effectively guaranteed-positive regardless of what happens next.

The 75% cap: Risk Back is capped at closing 75% of position. Prevents a situation where TP1 closes the entire position and there's nothing left to run to final TP.

Split Method Quick Reference

MethodWhere Partials LandBest For
Equal DistanceMathematically even between entry and TPPredictability; benchmark runs
Early OffsetShifted toward entry by Offset %Profit-banking strategies
PD Arrays (Near)On nearest structural featuresLiquidity-aware trade management
PD Arrays (Grid)On structural features at even fractionsHybrid — structural and predictable
💡 KEY INSIGHT
Partials + TP1 Secure (Group 13) is the most common risk-management combo in the indicator. Once TP1 hits, SL moves to BE and partial gain is locked in — every subsequent outcome is a win, breakeven, or better. Transforms your trade-outcome distribution.
🔥 TRY ALL FOUR SPLIT METHODS
Compare Profit Factor across Equal Distance, Early Offset, PD Arrays Near, and PD Arrays Grid on the same setup. If they all produce similar Profit Factor, your core setup is robust. If they differ dramatically, your profitability is highly sensitive to partial placement — pay close attention to TP1 Hits and TP1 Total on the Monitor.

▶How it worksHOW & WHY

Active only when Split Method (Group 9) is PD Arrays (Near) or PD Arrays (Grid). If Split Method is Equal Distance or Early Offset, these toggles are inert — partial placement is purely mathematical in those modes.

Identical behaviour to Group 8: This array matrix works exactly the same way as the Target Array Matrix — it's just applied to partial placement rather than final-target placement. The simulator scans all enabled arrays between entry and final TP, and places partials on them.

Why separate from Group 8: You may want different arrays for partials than for final targets. For example: include Wickless + BSL/SSL + HTF FVG for partials (small intermediate liquidity) but only HTF IPDA for final targets (macro destinations). Independent matrices give you that flexibility.

▶Tuning adviceHOW & WHY

Default — all ON. Produces the densest partial-candidate pool and the most adaptive placement.

For tight scalping: Enable only Wickless + Tailless + FVG. These are the smallest-distance features; partials land very close to entry.

For swing-style profit banking: Enable only Swing H/L + BSL/SSL + HTF IPDA. Partials only fire on structurally meaningful levels — fewer partials trigger per trade, but each one represents a genuine liquidity event.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
The TP1 Hits row on the Monitor directly responds to this group. Enable more arrays → more partial-candidate density → higher TP1 hit rate. Disable arrays → fewer candidates → lower hit rate but each hit is on a more significant level.

Long Setup — Core Stop Loss

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 11. LONG SETUP: CORE STOP LOSS ■

How the stop loss is placed and managed for long trades. The SL method determines where the SL line is drawn, which directly affects risk per trade, R:R ratio, and all drawdown calculations. Group 22 is the short equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
SL MethodSetup Auto Calc / Adjustable R / Dollar $ / Ticks / Wickless / Swing H/L / HTF / PD Array Matrix / Standard Trail / B.E. Trail / Advanced TrailHow the stop loss is placed and managed. 11 options from fixed to dynamic to trailing.
R:R (Adjustable R)0.1 – 2.0When SL Method is Adjustable R: SL distance as a fraction of target distance. 0.33 = SL at 33% of TP distance (giving 3R).
Fixed Dollar SLAny $Fixed-dollar SL distance when SL Method is Dollar $.
Fixed Tick SLAny tick countFixed-tick SL distance when SL Method is Ticks.

SL Method ExplorerLive demo

Eleven SL methods, each with different behaviour. Click any to see how it works, why you'd choose it, and when to avoid.

SL Method Explorer
How it works
The simulator places SL based on the detected model's structural boundary — for an FVG entry, the SL sits beyond the FVG's opposite boundary; for an OB, beyond the OB's low (longs) or high (shorts).
Why you'd choose it
The most structurally defensible SL method. If the SL gets hit, the setup's foundational structure has been invalidated — by definition, the trade thesis is dead.
When to avoid
Scalping environments where you want the absolute tightest possible stop. The structural boundary may be close to entry on some models, making the R:R unfavourable.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶The fundamental SL trade-offHOW & WHY

Tight SL: Low dollar risk per trade, high R:R when winning, but stops get hit by noise. Low Win Rate, potentially low Profit Factor if the SL hits outpace the winners.

Wide SL: High dollar risk per trade, lower R:R, but stops survive noise. Higher Win Rate, but bigger losses on the trades that do hit SL.

The balancing point varies by asset. NQ likes moderately wide stops. CL tolerates tight stops better due to its cleaner displacement behaviour. There's no universal answer — the Insight Monitor's Max/Avg SL Unrealised row tells you whether your stops are too tight (trades that went profitable before being stopped) or OK.

▶Trailing stops — when each type winsHOW & WHY

Standard Trail: For setups that run fast and far. Works when your win trades tend to be one-directional. Fails on choppy, back-and-fill moves.

B.E. Trail: A risk-neutralisation tool, not a profit-maximisation tool. Use when you want trades to 'go to zero or full TP' — no middle ground of small wins or small losses.

Advanced Trail: The gold standard for trending instruments. The early-trade grace period (Trail R Trigger) prevents noise-outs; once triggered, the tight trail captures continuation moves efficiently.

Which is right? If average winning bars is long (>30), use Advanced Trail to capture the extension. If it's short (<10), trailing adds noise — use a static SL method instead.

🧪 EXPERIMENT
Cycle SL Method through all 11 options on a fixed setup. Record Max Avg SL Unrealised, Win Rate, Profit Factor at each. The method that minimises 'trades that went positive before being stopped' while maintaining high Profit Factor is your asset's optimal SL method.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
The Max / Avg SL Unrealised row on the Monitor is the smoking gun for SL tuning. If it's high, your stops are being tagged by noise — either widen them, add a buffer (Group 14), or switch to a structural method.

▶How it worksHOW & WHY

Active only when SL Method (Group 11) is PD Array Matrix. Otherwise these toggles are inert.

Identical logic to Group 8 but inverted: The simulator scans behind entry (below for longs, above for shorts), finds every unmitigated instance of each enabled array type, sorts by distance, and uses the one at SL Array Depth (Group 11).

Tight vs wide: More arrays enabled = denser candidate pool = typically closer SL placement. Fewer arrays = sparser pool = deeper, safer stops.

▶Tuning for safety vs R:RHOW & WHY

For safer, deeper stops: Enable only the big structural arrays — Swing H/L + HTF IPDA + BSL/SSL. The nearest such array behind entry is typically a meaningful level. Stops get wider on average but survive noise.

For tight stops: Enable only FVG + i-FVG + OB + Breaker. These are generally tighter to entry, producing stops with better R:R but higher hit rates.

Balanced default: All ON. Usually produces mid-range stops that balance R:R and survival.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Use Groups 8 and 12 together. A common configuration: all arrays ON in Group 8 (dense target candidates, gives Tgt Array Depth flexibility), but only Swing H/L + HTF IPDA + BSL/SSL ON in Group 12 (protects stops behind major structure only). Asymmetric matrices for asymmetric purposes.
g13:
GROUP 13

Long Setup — Target Modifiers

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 13. LONG SETUP: TARGET MODIFIERS ■

Target modifiers are applied AFTER the base target is calculated by Target Method (Group 7). They adjust the final TP position up or down. Multiple modifiers can be stacked — they apply multiplicatively. Labels show 🎛️ when any modifier is active. Group 24 is the short equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Round Down 0.25ON / OFFRounds the deviation multiplier down to the nearest 0.25 (e.g. 2.73× → 2.5×). Labels marked 🧢.
Round Down 1.0ON / OFFRounds the dev down to the nearest whole number (2.73× → 2.0×).
Front Run (Slight)ON / OFFReduces TP distance by 14.25% of projection. Fills TPs slightly earlier.
Front Run (Deep)ON / OFFReduces TP distance by 20%. Fills TPs meaningfully earlier.
1/2 TargetsON / OFFHalves the TP distance. TP1/TP2/TP3 scale proportionally.
3/4 TargetsON / OFFReduces TP distance to 75% of original.
TP ExpansionOFF / 1.5× / 2× / 3× / 4× / 5×Multiplies TP distance by the chosen factor. Ambitious setups.
TP1 Secure (B.E. SL)ON / OFFWhen TP1 hits, the SL moves to breakeven. Label shows 'B.E.'. The most impactful modifier in Group 13.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶How modifiers stack — multiplicativelyHOW & WHY

The key rule: Every Target Modifier applies multiplicatively to the base TP distance. 1/2 Targets + TP Expansion 2× = net 1.0× (back to original distance). 3/4 Targets + TP Expansion 2× = 1.5×. Front Run Deep + 1/2 Targets = 0.4× (20% reduction of 50% = 40% of original).

Verifying the final result: The deviation multiplier printed on each TP label always shows the FINAL number after all modifiers have stacked. If you configure 1/2 Targets + TP Expansion 2× and the label shows '1.00×' — that confirms the stack is net-zero.

Strategic use of stacking: Stack a Front Run with a Round Down for very conservative targets. Stack TP Expansion 3× with Min Tgt Devs Increaser for aggressive but filtered targets.

▶Round Down 0.25 / 1.0 — the precision signalHOW & WHY

What they do: Truncate the Auto Calc deviation multiplier to the nearest 0.25 (or 1.0) below it. 2.73× becomes 2.5× (or 2.0×). The resulting TP is closer to entry than the raw Auto Calc would have placed it.

Why they exist: Setups whose Auto Calc deviation naturally lands on clean numbers (2.0×, 2.5×, 3.0×, 1.75×) tend to be higher-quality than ones with messy numbers (2.73×, 3.41×). Rounding Down normalises everything and typically improves Profit Factor by trimming the tails.

Advanced use: Disable both rounding filters and scan your detected setups for labels with clean deviation values. These are the highest-probability trades. See the Life Hacks appendix for the Deviation Precision technique.

▶Front Run (Slight / Deep)HOW & WHY

Slight (−14.25%): Pulls the TP back by 14.25% of the projected distance. Small, conservative reduction.

Deep (−20%): Pulls the TP back by 20%. More aggressive reduction; noticeably closer TPs.

Why 14.25% and 20%: 14.25% (0.1425) is the 1/7 Fibonacci ratio; 20% (0.2) is the standard 'conservative fill' offset from ICT methodology. Both values are calibrated to get you filled just before price does whatever liquidity-sweep manoeuvre might have invalidated the trade.

▶1/2 and 3/4 TargetsHOW & WHY

What they do: Scale all TP distances (including partial TPs) by the factor. 1/2 = half distance across the board; 3/4 = 75% distance.

Stacking: Enable both at once? The result is 3/4 × 1/2 = 3/8 = 37.5% of the original distance. Rare combination but mathematically valid.

Diagnostic use: Temporarily enable 1/2 Targets on a setup that's got great Detections but low Final TPs Hit. If it pushes Final TPs Hit past 50% with a Profit Factor above 1, the setup works at reduced ambition.

▶TP ExpansionHOW & WHY

What it does: Multiplies TP distance by 1.5×, 2×, 3×, 4×, or 5×. Expansive targets for ambitious setups.

Why it can work: Some assets (trending futures during earnings, crypto during news) systematically over-deliver on momentum. Auto Calc may underestimate target in these environments; TP Expansion gives you back the upside.

Why it can hurt: Final TPs Hit will drop. If the expanded target doesn't systematically get reached, you're now relying on partials to bank most of the profit — which means TP1 Secure becomes essential.

▶TP1 Secure (B.E. SL) — the most important modifier in Group 13HOW & WHY

What it does: When TP1 is hit, the SL is immediately moved to breakeven. The SL label updates to 'B.E.'. From that point on, the trade cannot lose — it will either run to TP2/TP3/final, or close at breakeven if price reverses to entry.

Why it transforms outcomes: Without TP1 Secure, a TP1-hit-then-reversal produces a small profit + a larger loss. With TP1 Secure, it produces a small profit + a breakeven close. On trades that would have been -1R without TP1 Secure, you're now at +0.3R to +0.5R (the secured TP1).

Diagnostic side-effect: Win Rate on the Monitor changes its meaning. 'Wins' now includes BE-closes after TP1. This makes raw Win Rate less comparable between TP1 Secure ON and OFF states — always look at Net PnL and Profit Factor in parallel.

Disable for clean diagnostics: When tuning a setup's raw entry/target/SL behaviour, disable TP1 Secure temporarily. You'll see the unprotected profitability of the core setup. See the Life Hacks appendix for the 'Disable Partials' workflow.

⚠️ STACKING TRAP
Modifiers stack multiplicatively, not additively. 1/2 Targets + TP Expansion 2× = net 1.0× (zero change). If you intend the two to combine, work out the product first — enabling two seemingly-aggressive modifiers can accidentally produce a net-zero setting.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
TP1 Secure + Risk Back Partial Sizing (Group 9) is the most powerful pair in the indicator for changing outcome distributions. Trades that previously had three states (win / break-even / loss) now have four states (full-TP win / TP1-then-BE / BE / loss), with the loss state now much rarer.
