GROUP 14

Long Setup — SL Modifiers

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 14. LONG SETUP: SL MODIFIERS ■

SL modifiers are applied AFTER the base stop loss is calculated by SL Method (Group 11). They adjust the final SL position. Multiple modifiers can be stacked multiplicatively. Labels show 🎚️ when any modifier is active. Group 25 is the short equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
SL BufferON / OFFAdds a buffer of 50% of base risk beyond the SL level. The SL is wider than the structural level alone would place it.
Extra SL BufferON / OFFAdds an additional 100% buffer (so with both enabled, SL is 150% wider than base).
1/2 SLON / OFFHalves the SL distance. Tighter stops; higher win rate hit.
3/4 SLON / OFFReduces SL distance to 75%. Moderate tightening.
SL ExpansionOFF / 1.5× / 2× / 3× / 4× / 5×Multiplies SL distance by the chosen factor. Much wider stops; structural-survival configurations.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶SL Buffer and Extra SL BufferHOW & WHY

SL Buffer (+50% of base risk): Adds a cushion between the structural SL level and the actual stop-out price. Protects against candle wicks that tag the structure but don't invalidate it.

Extra SL Buffer (+100% more): Stacks on top of SL Buffer. With both enabled, the total buffer is 150% of base risk — the SL sits well beyond the structural level.

Why buffers matter: Price often wicks through FVGs, OBs, and swing lows by a small amount before reversing. A buffer turns these wicks into non-events rather than stop-outs. The Monitor's Max Avg SL Unrealised drops dramatically when a buffer is added.

Cost: Wider stops mean lower R:R on winning trades. Worth it only when Max Avg SL Unrealised is genuinely high — evidence that noise is tagging stops.

▶1/2 and 3/4 SL — tighteningHOW & WHY

1/2 SL: Halves the SL distance. Stops become very tight. Win Rate will drop significantly, but the losses that do occur are half-size.

3/4 SL: Stops are 75% of original distance. Moderate tightening.

Stacking with buffers cancels out: SL Buffer (+50%) + 1/2 SL (−50%) = back to base. The result isn't always what you'd expect — check the actual SL distance on the chart to confirm.

Use case: Breakeven-positive setup with too much drawdown exposure. 1/2 SL reduces per-trade dollar risk while accepting that more stops will trigger.

▶SL Expansion — aggressive wideningHOW & WHY

What it does: Multiplies the base SL distance by the selected factor. SL Expansion 3× = stop 3× as wide as the base method would place it.

When to use: Volatile instruments where normal stops chop you out. Crypto during weekend, commodities during news, equities around earnings. A wider stop survives the volatility event.

Drawback: R:R collapses. A 3× wider stop on an unchanged target method means every win now has 1/3 the R:R it did before. The math only works if the widened stop produces enough additional winners to offset the R:R loss.

💡 DIAGNOSTIC TELLTALE
Max Avg SL Unrealised on the Monitor is the smoking gun. If this number is high (a trade went to 60% of target profit before reversing to hit SL), your stops aren't too tight in principle — they're being tagged at the worst possible moment. Activate Advanced Trail (Group 11) or add SL Buffer to preserve the gains.
⚠️ STACKING WARNING
SL modifiers stack multiplicatively like target modifiers. SL Buffer (+50%) + 1/2 SL produces a net widening of only 25% — probably not what you intended. Always check the resulting SL distance on a chart label.
g15:
GROUP 15

Long Setup — Trend & Filters

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 15. LONG SETUP: TREND & FILTERS ■

Optional quality filters that must pass before a detected setup is accepted as valid. These reduce the quantity of setups but aim to improve quality by requiring additional confirmation. Every filter is OFF by default — the recommended approach is to add them one at a time, comparing the Insight Monitor before and after. Group 26 is the short equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
SMA FilterON / OFFRequire price to be above the SMA (for longs) before accepting a setup.
SMA Length2 – 500SMA period. Default 200. Common alternatives: 50, 100, 21.
Invert SMAON / OFFFlips the logic: longs require price BELOW the SMA. Mean-reversion setup.
MSS/BOS FilterON / OFFRequire a recent Market Structure Shift or Break of Structure in the trade's direction.
Bullish DivergenceON / OFFRequire RSI bullish divergence within the lookback window before accepting a long.
RSI Lookback5 – 200How many bars the divergence scan searches backwards.
RSI Length2 – 50RSI calculation period. Default 14.
Min R:R FilterOFF / 0.5 – 5.0Rejects any setup whose R:R falls below the threshold. Cleanest edge-preservation filter in the group.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Filter philosophy — the trade-offHOW & WHY

Every filter costs detections. Each filter you add reduces the pool of setups that pass through to the Monitor. Fewer setups = smaller sample = less reliable stats. There's a minimum-detections threshold beneath which Profit Factor becomes statistically meaningless (about 100 setups).

The goal: The minimum number of filters that maximise Profit Factor with a trade count you're comfortable with. More filters is not inherently better.

The method: Enable one filter at a time. Check the Monitor. Record Profit Factor and detection count. Disable. Try a different filter. Compare independently. Only combine filters after individual testing.

▶SMA Filter — trend alignmentHOW & WHY

What it does: Requires price to be above the configured SMA before a long setup is accepted. 'Only trade with the trend' expressed as a quantitative rule.

SMA Length: Default 200 — the classic institutional trend line. 50 is more responsive (weekly swing structure). 21 is intraday. Longer SMA = stricter trend requirement; shorter SMA = more permissive.

Invert SMA: Flips the rule. Longs require price BELOW the SMA. This creates a mean-reversion filter — only take longs when price is extended below trend and likely to snap back.

When it helps: Strong trending markets. A simple trend filter dramatically cleans up counter-trend losers.

When it hurts: Range-bound markets. Your setups will be stuck waiting for trend confirmation that never comes; detection count drops to near-zero.

▶MSS/BOS Filter — structural confirmationHOW & WHY

Market Structure Shift (MSS): When price breaks the most recent opposing-direction swing. A bullish MSS occurs when price breaks above the most recent confirmed swing high after a series of lower highs.

Break of Structure (BOS): When price breaks a swing high in an uptrend (continuation) or a swing low in a downtrend.

What the filter requires: A recent MSS or BOS in the trade's direction before a setup is accepted. Swing Strength (Group 5) determines what qualifies as a swing — and therefore how strict this filter is.

Why it works: Structural breaks confirm institutional intent. Taking setups only after structure has broken in your direction filters out early-reversal attempts that will fail.

▶RSI Divergence FilterHOW & WHY

What it detects: Classic RSI divergence — price making a new low while RSI makes a higher low (bullish), or vice versa for bearish.

RSI Lookback: How many bars back the scan searches. Short (20–50) = only recent divergences count. Long (100–200) = older divergences are still valid. Shorter = stricter filter.

RSI Length: The RSI calculation period. Default 14 is canonical. Shorter (7, 10) produces more divergences; longer (21, 28) produces fewer but cleaner ones.

When it helps: Reversal-trading setups. Divergence is a classical reversal signal; pairing it with a fresh FVG or OB dramatically filters to high-quality reversals.

When it hurts: Strong trending markets. Divergences fire constantly in trends and most fail. Disable in trend.

▶Min R:R Filter — the edge-preservation classicHOW & WHY

What it does: Rejects any setup whose natural R:R (target distance / SL distance) is below the threshold. Min R:R 1.0 means target must be at least 1× as far as the stop; Min R:R 2.0 means 2×.

Why it's the cleanest filter: Directly protects expected value. Even at 40% Win Rate, a consistent 2:1 R:R setup has a positive expectancy. Min R:R Filter guarantees the math stays favourable.

Trade-off: Higher thresholds reject more setups. Min R:R 1.0 typically removes 10–20% of detections; Min R:R 2.0 removes 30–50%; Min R:R 3.0 can remove 70%+.

Recommended default: Start at 1.0 (never take a trade with worse-than-even R:R). Tighten only if the resulting Profit Factor is below 1.5.

🎯 PRO TIP
Start with NO filters. Study the Insight Monitor. Add filters one at a time. Compare Profit Factor before and after. If a filter improves PF but leaves very few trades (under 50), it may be overfitting — reject it even if the PF looks beautiful.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
The Min R:R Filter is the safest filter to leave on permanently. Every other filter can produce configuration-specific edge cases; Min R:R preserves expected value as a universal rule.
⚠️ OVERFITTING TRAP
Combining four filters that each improve Profit Factor doesn't mean their combination is better. Each filter removes setups; the remaining sample may be too small to be reliable. Always verify the combined detection count is at least 100.

GROUP 16

Long Setup — Model Specific

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 16. LONG SETUP: MODEL SPECIFIC ■

Fine-tuning settings that apply to specific ICT models. These let you customise how individual model types behave beyond the core entry, target, and SL configuration. Most have no effect on the Displacement model. Group 27 is the short equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
OB Candle RangeWicks / BodiesApplies to OB and Breaker. Whether the OB range uses the candle's full wick or just its body. Wicks = wider, more fills; Bodies = tighter, better R:R.
Strict OB ColourON / OFFOnly accept OB setups where the OB candle's colour matches the expected bias (red for short OBs, green for long OBs).
OB Entry MethodOB Range / Disp. Candle RangeWhether to enter within the OB candle's range or within the displacement candle's range.
OB Liquidity SweepON / OFFRequire the OB candle to have swept a prior swing before accepting the setup.
FVG (Displacement)ON / OFFOnly applies to Displacement model. Requires an FVG to form alongside the displacement candle.
i-FVG Expiry0 – 1000 barsBars before an unfilled i-FVG expires. 0 = never expires.
OB / Breaker Expiry0 – 1000 barsBars before an unfilled OB or Breaker expires.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶OB Candle Range — Wicks or Bodies?HOW & WHY

Wicks: The OB range is defined by the OB candle's full range — from wick top to wick bottom. The entry zone is wider.

Bodies: The OB range is defined by just the candle body — open to close, ignoring wicks. The entry zone is tighter.

Bodies wins on R:R. A tighter entry zone means entries land deeper into the OB candle's structure on average, which gives better R:R on winners.

Wicks wins on fill rate. A wider entry zone catches more retracements that don't quite reach the body. Detection counts are higher; Missed Entries lower.

▶Strict OB ColourHOW & WHY

What it does: Enforces the classical ICT OB definition — a bullish OB must be a red candle (the last down-close before a bullish displacement) and a bearish OB must be a green candle. Disabling this allows any OB candle colour.

Why keep it ON: The colour rule is part of what makes an OB an OB. A green candle before a bullish displacement is just a continuation candle, not a structural imbalance.

Why it might be OFF: On very low-timeframe charts where candle colours are noisy. You may find the non-strict version produces more reliable setups because the structural concept matters more than the colour.

▶OB Entry MethodHOW & WHY

OB Range: The entry level (Group 6) is computed within the OB candle itself. Standard ICT OB entry.

Disp. Candle Range: The entry level is computed within the displacement candle (the candle immediately following the OB). This is often a different, typically wider range.

When Disp. Candle Range helps: On assets where the OB is very compact (tight-bodied candles) but the displacement is large. Using the displacement range gives you a more useful entry zone — otherwise your entries are extremely tight to the OB close.

▶OB Liquidity Sweep requirementHOW & WHY

What it does: Only accepts an OB setup if the OB candle itself swept a prior swing low (for bullish OBs) or prior swing high (for bearish OBs). The OB is effectively also a liquidity grab.

Why it matters: An OB that swept liquidity before the displacement represents institutional 'clearing' of retail stops before the real move. These OBs have a higher fill-and-follow-through rate than bare OBs.

Cost: Dramatically reduces OB detections. On a typical 5-minute chart, maybe 30–50% of raw OBs also swept liquidity. Enabling this filter trims the OB pool significantly but tightens its quality.

▶FVG (Displacement) requirementHOW & WHY

What it does: Only accepts a Displacement-model setup if the displacement candle also formed an FVG (three-candle pattern with a gap). Effectively turns the Displacement model into 'Displacement AND FVG confluence'.

Why it's powerful: Pure Displacement detections produce many setups, but some are just large single candles with no structural gap. The FVG requirement ensures each Displacement detection also carries an unfilled gap — a structural pull point.

Alternative: Leave Displacement untouched and enable the FVG model separately (Group 6). You'll get both types of setup. The FVG (Displacement) requirement creates a hybrid model that only detects when both conditions are present.

▶Expiry rules — when stale setups dieHOW & WHY

i-FVG Expiry: An unfilled Inversion FVG waits this many bars before retiring itself. 0 = never expires. Typical setting: 100–200 bars. Older i-FVGs lose reliability because the structural context has shifted.

OB / Breaker Expiry: Same concept for OBs and Breakers. OBs are often considered fresher and more reliable; many users set OB Expiry to 50–100 bars.

Why expiry exists: An unmitigated OB from 500 bars ago has a much lower chance of being honoured than one from 50 bars ago. Expiry prevents the simulator from tracking zombie setups indefinitely.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
OB Candle Range = Wicks paired with Strict OB Colour = OFF produces the loosest OB detection — highest count, lowest quality. Bodies + Strict Colour + Liquidity Sweep produces the strictest — lowest count, highest quality. Both extremes have their uses; neither is universally right.
🧪 EXPERIMENT
Enable only the OB model. Run the simulator with OB Candle Range = Wicks, then rerun with Bodies. Compare Monitor. The difference in Profit Factor tells you which the asset respects more. Repeat separately for Strict OB Colour and Liquidity Sweep — each is a quality knob worth tuning once, then locking in.

GROUP 17

Short Setup — Core Entry

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 17. SHORT SETUP: CORE ENTRY ■
🪞 SHORT MIRROR — FULL CONTENT
This chapter is the complete short-side equivalent of Group 6. All settings, logic, and guidance apply identically with directional language reversed. If Setup Mirroring (Group 5) is active with "Shorts Mirror Longs", these settings are ignored and Group 6 governs both directions.

Where you define what the simulator looks for and where it places the entry. Group 6 controls the short side; Group 6 controls the short side (or mirrors this group if Setup Mirroring is active in Group 5). Two decisions are being made here: which ICT model(s) to detect, and where within each detected model's range to place the simulated limit order.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Model SelectionDisplacement / FVG / i-FVG / OB / BreakerIndependent toggles for each of the 5 ICT entry models. Any combination allowed. All ON by default.
Entry Level0.0 – 1.0 / Manual / F.RunWhere within the PD array the entry sits, like a Fibonacci retracement inside the array. Default 0.34 — see deep dive for why this differs from the textbook 0.618 OTE.
Manual EntryAny floatCustom entry value when Entry Level is set to Manual Input. Lets you trial non-standard levels.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Model SelectionHOW & WHY

Five independent checkboxes: Displacement, FVG, Inversion FVG, Order Block, Breaker Block. You can enable any combination — one model, all five, or any mix. When all five are enabled this is functionally equivalent to 'All Models'.

Displacement — Detects bullish displacement candles (close exceeds prior candle's high with strong body-to-body momentum). The entry range is the displacement candle's body (open to close). This is the raw momentum signal. Displacements tend to have the widest entry ranges and the most aggressive deviation multipliers when using Auto Calc targets.

FVG (Fair Value Gap) — Detects bullish three-candle patterns where a gap exists between candle 1's high and candle 3's low after a bullish displacement. The entry range is the gap itself — the unfilled space between the two non-overlapping candles. FVGs represent undelivered price action the market is expected to return to. Tighter entry ranges than Displacements, often more precise entries.

Inversion FVG (i-FVG) — Detects FVGs that have been violated (price closed through the original FVG) and then inverted. A bearish FVG violated becomes a bullish i-FVG entry zone. Represents areas where the original narrative was invalidated and the market is now using the same zone in the opposite direction. Subject to i-FVG Expiry in Group 16.

Order Block — Detects the candle immediately before a displacement. The OB range (wicks or bodies, controlled by Group 16) becomes the entry zone. OBs represent the last point of institutional activity before the displacement move. Optional strict colour matching and liquidity sweep requirements available in Group 16.

Breaker Block — Detects Order Blocks that have been 'broken' — price displaced through the OB in the opposite direction. A bearish OB broken to the upside becomes a bullish Breaker entry zone. Represents failed institutional positions now expected to act as support. Subject to OB/BRK Expiry in Group 16.

▶Entry LevelHOW & WHY

How it works: Every detected setup creates a box on the chart (the model range). Entry Level determines where inside that box your entry sits. Expressed as a decimal between 0.0 and 1.0 (plus Front Run options outside that range), functioning like a Fibonacci retracement level within the PD array.

The shallow-vs-deep trade-off: Lower values place the entry near the top of the range (for shorts) where price touches first on a retracement. Higher values place it deeper into the range where price must retrace further to reach. Shallower entries fill more often but give worse prices; deeper entries give better prices but fill less often.

What ICT actually defines as the OTE: The Optimal Trade Entry in ICT methodology is the 0.618 retracement — that's the textbook level. It's available here as a one-click preset, and it's the right choice for traders who want strict ICT methodology applied as written.

Why this indicator defaults to 0.34 instead: The five entry models (Displacement, FVG, i-FVG, OB, Breaker) all target a specific candle's range rather than the broader price-leg ranges that classical ICT OTE analysis is applied to. Inside an FVG or an OB, the 0.5 mid-point has structural meaning — it's the Consequent Encroachment of a gap and the Mean Threshold of an Order Block. Both 0.5 mid-points are key levels institutional flow is drawn to. By placing the default at 0.34, the indicator front-runs the 0.5 mid-point — your entry fills before price reaches the level the algo is most likely to react from. This trades a small amount of theoretical R:R for a meaningful improvement in fill rate.

It's a trader's choice, not a rule. The default is a starting point chosen for fill-rate balance. If you want strict ICT — set 0.618. If you want maximum fill rate — set 0.25 or use Front Run. If you want the institutional reaction price — set 0.5. The Insight Monitor will tell you which level your asset and timeframe actually reward; let the data decide rather than the default.

Front Run (±0.1425): Places the entry OUTSIDE the PD array (for shorts, above the near edge; for shorts, below). Fills BEFORE price even enters the array. Maximum fill rate at the cost of theoretical R:R — you're chasing price rather than waiting for it.

The full preset list: The Entry Level dropdown contains 22 preset Fibonacci levels covering every classical retracement and extension plus several useful in-between values. Outside the array: F. Run (+0.1425). Inside the array, shallow → deep: 0.0 (Near Edge), 0.1, 0.1425, 0.214, 0.236, 0.25, 0.298, 0.34 (default), 0.361, 0.382, 0.44, 0.5 (Equilibrium), 0.56, 0.618 (ICT OTE), 0.639, 0.66, 0.702, 0.75, 0.786, 0.8575, 0.9, 1.0 (Far Edge). Beyond the array: F. Run (−0.1425). The dense spacing around the structurally significant points (0.34, 0.5, 0.618) lets you fine-tune around the critical zones where detection-vs-fill-rate trade-offs are most sensitive.

Manual Input: Use any decimal value not in the preset list. Useful for experimenting with non-standard retracement levels, or when Insight Monitor analysis suggests a specific in-between level optimises results.

▶How Model × Entry Level interactHOW & WHY

These two settings compound. The model determines the range boundaries; the Entry Level determines where within those boundaries you sit. Changing models changes the meaning of a given Entry Level.

Example: Entry Level 0.34 on a Displacement setup places the entry 34% down from the displacement candle's high. Entry Level 0.34 on an FVG setup places the entry 34% down from the FVG's top edge. Because the FVG is typically a smaller range than the Displacement body, the 0.34 FVG entry is an absolutely shallower retracement than the 0.34 Displacement entry.

Implication for optimisation: When switching models, expect Monitor stats to shift even if Entry Level is unchanged. The same Fib level produces different entry prices depending on which model's range is being sampled.

🧪 EXPERIMENT
Hold everything else constant and sweep Entry Level from 0.0 → 0.25 → 0.34 → 0.5 → 0.618 → 0.786 on a 2,000-bar lookback. Record Win Rate, Profit Factor, Detections, and Missed Entries at each level. The sweet spot typically shows a clear peak in Profit Factor, often at a non-default value.
⚠️ WATCH OUT
The Missed Entries stat climbs dramatically as Entry Level increases. At 0.786, 40–60% of detected setups may fail to fill. If your Missed PnL grows faster than your Net PnL, you're passing up more opportunity than you're gaining in R:R.

GROUP 18

Short Setup — Core Targets

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 18. SHORT SETUP: CORE TARGETS ■
🪞 SHORT MIRROR — FULL CONTENT
This chapter is the complete short-side equivalent of Group 7. All settings, logic, and guidance apply identically with directional language reversed. If Setup Mirroring (Group 5) is active with "Shorts Mirror Longs", these settings are ignored and Group 7 governs both directions.

How take-profit levels are calculated for short trades. The target method determines where the TP line is drawn, which directly affects potential reward, R:R ratio, and every profit-related stat on the Monitor. Group 7 is the long equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Target MethodAuto Calc / Fixed Devs / Dollar $ / Ticks / Wickless / Swing H/L / HTF / PD Array MatrixHow TP is calculated. Auto Calc uses displacement-based deviation multiplier. Structural methods find actual chart features. Fixed methods use user-specified distances.
Fixed Dev Multiplier0.5 – 10.0Manual deviation multiplier when Target Method is Fixed Devs. TP distance = multiplier × displacement candle body size.
Dollar TPAny $Fixed-dollar TP distance when Target Method is Dollar $.
Tick TPAny tick countFixed-tick TP distance when Target Method is Ticks.
Tgt Array Depth1 – 10For structural methods: which array to target. Depth 1 = nearest, depth 3 = skip first two and target the third. Higher = deeper, further-out targets.
Min Tgt DevsOff / Filter / IncreaserMinimum deviation threshold. Filter rejects setups below it; Increaser bumps TP up to meet it.
Max Tgt DevsOff / Filter / ReducerMaximum deviation threshold. Filter rejects setups above it; Reducer caps TP down to it.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Target Method — the seven optionsHOW & WHY

Auto Calc: Default. TP is calculated as entry + (displacement candle body × deviation multiplier). The deviation is derived from the candle's momentum profile. The most adaptive method — targets scale naturally with volatility.

Fixed Devs: You specify the multiplier manually (e.g. 2.5×). TP = entry + (displacement body × your multiplier). Use when you have a specific deviation that works on your asset and you don't want the auto-calc varying it.

Dollar $ / Ticks: Fixed distances in absolute units. TP always lands the same dollar or tick amount from entry regardless of setup size. Best for scalping where you want consistent profit targets.

Wickless: TP lands at the nearest wickless candle high above entry (for shorts). Wickless candles represent unfilled liquidity that price often revisits. Dynamic — the target adapts to chart structure.

Swing H/L: TP lands at the nearest prior swing high above entry (for shorts). Swing Strength in Group 5 determines what counts as a swing.

HTF: TP lands at the nearest HTF IPDA level below entry. Requires HTF IPDA Levels ON in Group 5.

PD Array Matrix: TP lands at the nearest unmitigated PD array below entry. Which arrays count is controlled by Group 8. Tgt Array Depth picks which one (1st, 2nd, 3rd…).

▶Fixed Dev Multiplier — when to use itHOW & WHY

Why manual devs: Auto Calc's multiplier varies with candle momentum. For high-variance assets, this can produce wildly inconsistent targets. Fixed Devs gives you a single consistent multiplier across every setup.

Typical values: 1.5–2.0 for conservative targets with high hit rates. 2.5–3.5 for balanced. 4.0+ for ambitious, low-hit-rate targets.

How to find yours: Enable Auto Calc and check the deviation multiplier shown on your TP labels across 100+ trades. Average them. That average is your Fixed Dev starting point.

▶Target Array DepthHOW & WHY

What depth means: For structural target methods (Wickless, Swing H/L, HTF, PD Array Matrix), depth selects which instance of the structural feature to target. Depth 1 = nearest. Depth 2 = skip the nearest, target the next. Depth 5 = skip four, target the fifth.

Shallow vs deep targets: Depth 1 targets are close — high hit rate but limited R:R. Depth 5+ targets are far — low hit rate, substantial R:R when reached.

The Reducer modifier: If Max Tgt Devs is set to Reducer, a deep-depth target that exceeds Max Dev will be pulled back to the closest array that meets the threshold. You get the benefits of a deep scan with a safety net against unrealistic targets.

▶Min / Max Dev thresholdsHOW & WHY

Min Tgt Devs — Filter: Any setup whose natural target is below the minimum dev multiplier is rejected. The Missed Entries row stays clean; Detection count drops. Use when you only want setups with meaningful profit potential.

Min Tgt Devs — Increaser: Same as Filter, but instead of rejecting the setup, the TP is bumped up to meet the minimum. The label shows 🧢 to indicate a modifier has been applied.

Max Tgt Devs — Filter: Rejects setups whose natural target exceeds the maximum. Filters out unrealistically ambitious trades.

Max Tgt Devs — Reducer: Caps an oversized target down to the maximum. Gives you a ceiling on R:R without sacrificing the setup entirely.

Target Method Quick Reference

MethodBehaviourBest For
Auto CalcMomentum-adaptive TP, widest scalingDefault. All-round use.
Fixed DevsUser-defined multiplier, consistentWhen Auto Calc's variance is too volatile for your plan
Dollar $ / TicksFixed absolute distanceScalping; consistent targets
WicklessNearest wickless candle H/LLiquidity-seeking trades
Swing H/LNearest prior swing extremeStructural trading
HTFNearest HTF IPDA levelMacro-aware trading
PD Array MatrixNearest unmitigated PD arrayICT-purist structural targeting
🧪 EXPERIMENT
Hold Entry Level and SL Method constant. Cycle Target Method through all seven options on a 2,000-bar lookback. Record Profit Factor, Avg R:R, and Win Rate for each. The Target Method that maximises Profit Factor on your specific asset is rarely the default.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
Auto Calc is the right default for most configurations because it scales with momentum — quiet candles produce conservative targets, explosive candles produce ambitious ones. Switch to Fixed Devs only when you have a specific multiplier identified on your asset.

GROUP 19

Short Setup — Target PD Arrays

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 19. SHORT SETUP: TARGET PD ARRAYS ■
🪞 SHORT MIRROR — FULL CONTENT
This chapter is the complete short-side equivalent of Group 8. All settings, logic, and guidance apply identically with directional language reversed. If Setup Mirroring (Group 5) is active with "Shorts Mirror Longs", these settings are ignored and Group 8 governs both directions.

Controls which PD Array types are included when the Target Method (Group 7) is set to PD Array Matrix. Provides granular control over the structural features the simulator scans when building its sorted proximity list for dynamic targeting. Group 8 is the long equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Target: FVGON / OFFInclude Fair Value Gaps in the target-scan array.
Target: i-FVGON / OFFInclude Inversion Fair Value Gaps (violated and re-entered).
Target: OBON / OFFInclude Order Blocks (last candle before displacement).
Target: BreakerON / OFFInclude Breaker Blocks (failed and re-entered Order Blocks).
Target: WicklessON / OFFInclude wickless-candle highs and lows (undelivered liquidity).
Target: TaillessON / OFFInclude tailless-candle highs and lows (the mirror of wickless).
Target: Swing H/LON / OFFInclude confirmed swing highs and lows as targetable levels.
Target: BSL/SSLON / OFFInclude Buy-side and Sell-side Liquidity levels — equal highs/lows that sit as liquidity pools.
Target: HTF IPDAON / OFFInclude HTF IPDA levels (20/40/60/80/100-period highs and lows from the configured HTF).
Target: HTF FVGON / OFFInclude the most recent unmitigated HTF FVG (from the HTF Scout in Group 5).
Target: HTF OBON / OFFInclude the most recent unmitigated HTF OB (from the HTF Scout in Group 5).
Deep dive · click any setting
▶How the Target Array Matrix worksHOW & WHY

Only active when Target Method = PD Array Matrix (Group 7). If you're using any other Target Method, these toggles are inert — the simulator doesn't consult them.

The matrix logic: For each detected setup, the simulator scans all enabled array types in the direction of the trade, finds every unmitigated instance, sorts them by distance from entry, and picks the one at the configured Tgt Array Depth (Group 7). Depth 1 = nearest, Depth 5 = skip first four.

What enabling more arrays does: Expands the pool of possible targets. More arrays = denser target-scan = shorter typical target distances (because there are more candidates to choose from). Disabling array types spreads target candidates further apart — Targets land deeper but less often.

What disabling does: Forces the simulator to ignore that structural feature entirely. If you believe FVGs don't act as reliable targets on your asset, disable them — the simulator will find its Depth-1 target in a non-FVG structure instead.

▶Choosing which arrays to enableHOW & WHY

Start with all ON (default). Run 500+ trades. Check Profit Factor and Final TPs Hit on the Monitor.

If Final TPs Hit is low (under 30%): Your targets are too far. Try disabling the larger-scale arrays (HTF IPDA, Swing H/L, BSL/SSL) — this forces closer targets on average.

If Final TPs Hit is very high (over 70%) with low R:R: Your targets are too close. Enable more structural arrays (HTF IPDA, HTF FVG, HTF OB) to pull targets further out on average.

Liquidity-specific configuration: If you trade with the liquidity-sweep thesis, enable only Wickless + Tailless + BSL/SSL. The simulator targets only liquidity pools; nothing else. Compact, powerful structural setup for liquidity-sweep traders.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Enabling or disabling array types changes the shape of the target distribution, not just target counts. Before you tune array depth (Group 7) or modifiers (Group 13), get this matrix right — it's the foundation the depth selector operates on.
🧪 EXPERIMENT
Build two instances on the same chart. Instance A has all arrays ON. Instance B has only FVG + OB ON. Compare the Monitors. The difference reveals how much of your edge comes from the broader structural-target pool vs the core FVG/OB pair.

GROUP 20

Short Setup — Partial Profits

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 20. SHORT SETUP: PARTIAL PROFITS ■
🪞 SHORT MIRROR — FULL CONTENT
This chapter is the complete short-side equivalent of Group 9. All settings, logic, and guidance apply identically with directional language reversed. If Setup Mirroring (Group 5) is active with "Shorts Mirror Longs", these settings are ignored and Group 9 governs both directions.

Partial profits let you close portions of your position at intermediate levels between entry and the final take profit. Rather than waiting for the full TP — which may not happen — you secure profit at structural or mathematical levels along the way. Group 9 is the long equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Enable PartialsON / OFFMaster toggle for partial-profit closures. When OFF, each trade runs as all-in / all-out between entry and final TP.
Partial Count1 – 3How many partial levels to place between entry and final TP. TP1 is always the nearest; TP3 is the deepest.
Split MethodEqual Distance / Early Offset / PD Arrays (Near) / PD Arrays (Grid)How the partial levels are spaced between entry and final TP. See drawer for each.
Early Offset %0 – 50%Shifts partials closer to entry when Split Method is Early Offset. 30% pulls TP1 30% closer to entry.
Size MethodEqual Size / Risk BackHow the position is split across partials. Risk Back weights TP1 to recover a multiple of the trade's risk.
Risk Back %100%+Proportion of trade risk recovered at TP1 when Size Method is Risk Back. 100% = breakeven on risk; 200% = recover 2R. Capped at 75% of position.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Enable Partials — why turn it on or offHOW & WHY

When ON: Every trade closes a portion of its position at TP1, TP2, and/or TP3 before the final TP. Each partial triggers its own emoji and PnL contribution to the Monitor.

When OFF: Pure all-in / all-out. Each trade either hits final TP (win), SL (loss), or EOD close. Cleaner diagnostic view but no intermediate profit-taking.

Diagnostic use of OFF: Temporarily disable partials when you want to see the raw performance of your Target Method, Entry Level, and SL placement. If the configuration is unprofitable without partials, partials are masking a deeper problem — fix entry/target/SL first.

▶Partial Count — 1, 2, or 3HOW & WHY

Partial Count 1: Single partial (TP1). Binary: either TP1 then final TP, or TP1 then SL/EOD. Simplest to analyse.

Partial Count 2: TP1 + TP2 + final. Middle ground.

Partial Count 3: TP1 + TP2 + TP3 + final. Maximum profit-averaging. Useful when target distances are large — gives multiple chances to secure gains along the way. Downside: the position size remaining at final TP is smaller, so the 'big TP' reward is less dramatic.

▶Split Method — the four algorithmsHOW & WHY

Equal Distance: Partials are evenly spaced between entry and final TP. Partial Count 3 with final TP at 3× means TP1 at 0.75×, TP2 at 1.5×, TP3 at 2.25×. Predictable; purely mathematical.

Early Offset: Same as Equal Distance but all partials are shifted closer to entry by the Early Offset percentage. At 30% offset, TP1 lands 30% closer to entry than Equal Distance would place it. Captures profit faster at the cost of leaving more upside on the table.

PD Arrays (Near): Partials land on the nearest structural PD arrays (from Group 10) between entry and final TP. Natural profit-taking zones — where liquidity is likely to react regardless of your plan.

PD Arrays (Grid): The simulator divides the entry-to-TP distance by Partial Count, then finds the nearest PD array at each division point. Evenly distributed like Equal Distance but snapped to real structure. The most adaptive option — different from Near on assets where structural features cluster near entry.

▶Size Method — how the position is splitHOW & WHY

Equal Size: Each partial closes an equal fraction of the position. Partial Count 3 → 25% at TP1, 25% at TP2, 25% at TP3, 25% at final TP.

Risk Back: TP1 is sized to close whatever fraction of position is needed to recover the specified multiple of risk. Risk Back 100% means TP1 closes enough position to break even on the trade's risk. Risk Back 200% closes enough to realise 2R at TP1 alone.

Why Risk Back is powerful: Your worst-case trade outcome changes dramatically once TP1 hits. If TP1 Secure is also enabled (Group 13), the SL moves to breakeven after TP1 — so a Risk Back trade that hits TP1 is effectively guaranteed-positive regardless of what happens next.

The 75% cap: Risk Back is capped at closing 75% of position. Prevents a situation where TP1 closes the entire position and there's nothing left to run to final TP.

Split Method Quick Reference

MethodWhere Partials LandBest For
Equal DistanceMathematically even between entry and TPPredictability; benchmark runs
Early OffsetShifted toward entry by Offset %Profit-banking strategies
PD Arrays (Near)On nearest structural featuresLiquidity-aware trade management
PD Arrays (Grid)On structural features at even fractionsHybrid — structural and predictable
💡 KEY INSIGHT
Partials + TP1 Secure (Group 13) is the most common risk-management combo in the indicator. Once TP1 hits, SL moves to BE and partial gain is locked in — every subsequent outcome is a win, breakeven, or better. Transforms your trade-outcome distribution.
🔥 TRY ALL FOUR SPLIT METHODS
Compare Profit Factor across Equal Distance, Early Offset, PD Arrays Near, and PD Arrays Grid on the same setup. If they all produce similar Profit Factor, your core setup is robust. If they differ dramatically, your profitability is highly sensitive to partial placement — pay close attention to TP1 Hits and TP1 Total on the Monitor.

SL Method ExplorerLive demo

Eleven SL methods, each with different behaviour. Click any to see how it works, why you'd choose it, and when to avoid.

SL Method Explorer
How it works
The simulator places SL based on the detected model's structural boundary — for an FVG entry, the SL sits beyond the FVG's opposite boundary; for an OB, beyond the OB's low (longs) or high (shorts).
Why you'd choose it
The most structurally defensible SL method. If the SL gets hit, the setup's foundational structure has been invalidated — by definition, the trade thesis is dead.
When to avoid
Scalping environments where you want the absolute tightest possible stop. The structural boundary may be close to entry on some models, making the R:R unfavourable.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶The fundamental SL trade-offHOW & WHY

Tight SL: Low dollar risk per trade, high R:R when winning, but stops get hit by noise. Low Win Rate, potentially low Profit Factor if the SL hits outpace the winners.

Wide SL: High dollar risk per trade, lower R:R, but stops survive noise. Higher Win Rate, but bigger losses on the trades that do hit SL.

The balancing point varies by asset. NQ likes moderately wide stops. CL tolerates tight stops better due to its cleaner displacement behaviour. There's no universal answer — the Insight Monitor's Max/Avg SL Unrealised row tells you whether your stops are too tight (trades that went profitable before being stopped) or OK.

▶Trailing stops — when each type winsHOW & WHY

Standard Trail: For setups that run fast and far. Works when your win trades tend to be one-directional. Fails on choppy, back-and-fill moves.

B.E. Trail: A risk-neutralisation tool, not a profit-maximisation tool. Use when you want trades to 'go to zero or full TP' — no middle ground of small wins or small losses.

Advanced Trail: The gold standard for trending instruments. The early-trade grace period (Trail R Trigger) prevents noise-outs; once triggered, the tight trail captures continuation moves efficiently.

Which is right? If average winning bars is long (>30), use Advanced Trail to capture the extension. If it's short (<10), trailing adds noise — use a static SL method instead.

🧪 EXPERIMENT
Cycle SL Method through all 11 options on a fixed setup. Record Max Avg SL Unrealised, Win Rate, Profit Factor at each. The method that minimises 'trades that went positive before being stopped' while maintaining high Profit Factor is your asset's optimal SL method.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
The Max / Avg SL Unrealised row on the Monitor is the smoking gun for SL tuning. If it's high, your stops are being tagged by noise — either widen them, add a buffer (Group 14), or switch to a structural method.

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 24. SHORT SETUP: TARGET MODIFIERS ■
🪞 SHORT MIRROR — FULL CONTENT
This chapter is the complete short-side equivalent of Group 13. All settings, logic, and guidance apply identically with directional language reversed. If Setup Mirroring (Group 5) is active with "Shorts Mirror Longs", these settings are ignored and Group 13 governs both directions.

Target modifiers are applied AFTER the base target is calculated by Target Method (Group 7). They adjust the final TP position up or down. Multiple modifiers can be stacked — they apply multiplicatively. Labels show 🎛️ when any modifier is active. Group 13 is the long equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Round Down 0.25ON / OFFRounds the deviation multiplier down to the nearest 0.25 (e.g. 2.73× → 2.5×). Labels marked 🧢.
Round Down 1.0ON / OFFRounds the dev down to the nearest whole number (2.73× → 2.0×).
Front Run (Slight)ON / OFFReduces TP distance by 14.25% of projection. Fills TPs slightly earlier.
Front Run (Deep)ON / OFFReduces TP distance by 20%. Fills TPs meaningfully earlier.
1/2 TargetsON / OFFHalves the TP distance. TP1/TP2/TP3 scale proportionally.
3/4 TargetsON / OFFReduces TP distance to 75% of original.
TP ExpansionOFF / 1.5× / 2× / 3× / 4× / 5×Multiplies TP distance by the chosen factor. Ambitious setups.
TP1 Secure (B.E. SL)ON / OFFWhen TP1 hits, the SL moves to breakeven. Label shows 'B.E.'. The most impactful modifier in Group 13.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶How modifiers stack — multiplicativelyHOW & WHY

The key rule: Every Target Modifier applies multiplicatively to the base TP distance. 1/2 Targets + TP Expansion 2× = net 1.0× (back to original distance). 3/4 Targets + TP Expansion 2× = 1.5×. Front Run Deep + 1/2 Targets = 0.4× (20% reduction of 50% = 40% of original).

Verifying the final result: The deviation multiplier printed on each TP label always shows the FINAL number after all modifiers have stacked. If you configure 1/2 Targets + TP Expansion 2× and the label shows '1.00×' — that confirms the stack is net-zero.

Strategic use of stacking: Stack a Front Run with a Round Down for very conservative targets. Stack TP Expansion 3× with Min Tgt Devs Increaser for aggressive but filtered targets.

▶Round Down 0.25 / 1.0 — the precision signalHOW & WHY

What they do: Truncate the Auto Calc deviation multiplier to the nearest 0.25 (or 1.0) below it. 2.73× becomes 2.5× (or 2.0×). The resulting TP is closer to entry than the raw Auto Calc would have placed it.

Why they exist: Setups whose Auto Calc deviation naturally lands on clean numbers (2.0×, 2.5×, 3.0×, 1.75×) tend to be higher-quality than ones with messy numbers (2.73×, 3.41×). Rounding Down normalises everything and typically improves Profit Factor by trimming the tails.

Advanced use: Disable both rounding filters and scan your detected setups for labels with clean deviation values. These are the highest-probability trades. See the Life Hacks appendix for the Deviation Precision technique.

▶Front Run (Slight / Deep)HOW & WHY

Slight (−14.25%): Pulls the TP back by 14.25% of the projected distance. Small, conservative reduction.

Deep (−20%): Pulls the TP back by 20%. More aggressive reduction; noticeably closer TPs.

Why 14.25% and 20%: 14.25% (0.1425) is the 1/7 Fibonacci ratio; 20% (0.2) is the standard 'conservative fill' offset from ICT methodology. Both values are calibrated to get you filled just before price does whatever liquidity-sweep manoeuvre might have invalidated the trade.

▶1/2 and 3/4 TargetsHOW & WHY

What they do: Scale all TP distances (including partial TPs) by the factor. 1/2 = half distance across the board; 3/4 = 75% distance.

Stacking: Enable both at once? The result is 3/4 × 1/2 = 3/8 = 37.5% of the original distance. Rare combination but mathematically valid.

Diagnostic use: Temporarily enable 1/2 Targets on a setup that's got great Detections but low Final TPs Hit. If it pushes Final TPs Hit past 50% with a Profit Factor above 1, the setup works at reduced ambition.

▶TP ExpansionHOW & WHY

What it does: Multiplies TP distance by 1.5×, 2×, 3×, 4×, or 5×. Expansive targets for ambitious setups.

Why it can work: Some assets (trending futures during earnings, crypto during news) systematically over-deliver on momentum. Auto Calc may underestimate target in these environments; TP Expansion gives you back the upside.

Why it can hurt: Final TPs Hit will drop. If the expanded target doesn't systematically get reached, you're now relying on partials to bank most of the profit — which means TP1 Secure becomes essential.

▶TP1 Secure (B.E. SL) — the most important modifier in Group 13HOW & WHY

What it does: When TP1 is hit, the SL is immediately moved to breakeven. The SL label updates to 'B.E.'. From that point on, the trade cannot lose — it will either run to TP2/TP3/final, or close at breakeven if price reverses to entry.

Why it transforms outcomes: Without TP1 Secure, a TP1-hit-then-reversal produces a small profit + a larger loss. With TP1 Secure, it produces a small profit + a breakeven close. On trades that would have been -1R without TP1 Secure, you're now at +0.3R to +0.5R (the secured TP1).

Diagnostic side-effect: Win Rate on the Monitor changes its meaning. 'Wins' now includes BE-closes after TP1. This makes raw Win Rate less comparable between TP1 Secure ON and OFF states — always look at Net PnL and Profit Factor in parallel.

Disable for clean diagnostics: When tuning a setup's raw entry/target/SL behaviour, disable TP1 Secure temporarily. You'll see the unprotected profitability of the core setup. See the Life Hacks appendix for the 'Disable Partials' workflow.

⚠️ STACKING TRAP
Modifiers stack multiplicatively, not additively. 1/2 Targets + TP Expansion 2× = net 1.0× (zero change). If you intend the two to combine, work out the product first — enabling two seemingly-aggressive modifiers can accidentally produce a net-zero setting.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
TP1 Secure + Risk Back Partial Sizing (Group 9) is the most powerful pair in the indicator for changing outcome distributions. Trades that previously had three states (win / break-even / loss) now have four states (full-TP win / TP1-then-BE / BE / loss), with the loss state now much rarer.

🪞 SHORT MIRROR — FULL CONTENT
This chapter is the complete short-side equivalent of Group 14. All settings, logic, and guidance apply identically with directional language reversed. If Setup Mirroring (Group 5) is active with "Shorts Mirror Longs", these settings are ignored and Group 14 governs both directions.

SL modifiers are applied AFTER the base stop loss is calculated by SL Method (Group 11). They adjust the final SL position. Multiple modifiers can be stacked multiplicatively. Labels show 🎚️ when any modifier is active. Group 14 is the long equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
SL BufferON / OFFAdds a buffer of 50% of base risk beyond the SL level. The SL is wider than the structural level alone would place it.
Extra SL BufferON / OFFAdds an additional 100% buffer (so with both enabled, SL is 150% wider than base).
1/2 SLON / OFFHalves the SL distance. Tighter stops; higher win rate hit.
3/4 SLON / OFFReduces SL distance to 75%. Moderate tightening.
SL ExpansionOFF / 1.5× / 2× / 3× / 4× / 5×Multiplies SL distance by the chosen factor. Much wider stops; structural-survival configurations.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶SL Buffer and Extra SL BufferHOW & WHY

SL Buffer (+50% of base risk): Adds a cushion between the structural SL level and the actual stop-out price. Protects against candle wicks that tag the structure but don't invalidate it.

Extra SL Buffer (+100% more): Stacks on top of SL Buffer. With both enabled, the total buffer is 150% of base risk — the SL sits well beyond the structural level.

Why buffers matter: Price often wicks through FVGs, OBs, and swing lows by a small amount before reversing. A buffer turns these wicks into non-events rather than stop-outs. The Monitor's Max Avg SL Unrealised drops dramatically when a buffer is added.

Cost: Wider stops mean lower R:R on winning trades. Worth it only when Max Avg SL Unrealised is genuinely high — evidence that noise is tagging stops.

▶1/2 and 3/4 SL — tighteningHOW & WHY

1/2 SL: Halves the SL distance. Stops become very tight. Win Rate will drop significantly, but the losses that do occur are half-size.

3/4 SL: Stops are 75% of original distance. Moderate tightening.

Stacking with buffers cancels out: SL Buffer (+50%) + 1/2 SL (−50%) = back to base. The result isn't always what you'd expect — check the actual SL distance on the chart to confirm.

Use case: Breakeven-positive setup with too much drawdown exposure. 1/2 SL reduces per-trade dollar risk while accepting that more stops will trigger.

▶SL Expansion — aggressive wideningHOW & WHY

What it does: Multiplies the base SL distance by the selected factor. SL Expansion 3× = stop 3× as wide as the base method would place it.

When to use: Volatile instruments where normal stops chop you out. Crypto during weekend, commodities during news, equities around earnings. A wider stop survives the volatility event.

Drawback: R:R collapses. A 3× wider stop on an unchanged target method means every win now has 1/3 the R:R it did before. The math only works if the widened stop produces enough additional winners to offset the R:R loss.

💡 DIAGNOSTIC TELLTALE
Max Avg SL Unrealised on the Monitor is the smoking gun. If this number is high (a trade went to 60% of target profit before reversing to hit SL), your stops aren't too tight in principle — they're being tagged at the worst possible moment. Activate Advanced Trail (Group 11) or add SL Buffer to preserve the gains.
⚠️ STACKING WARNING
SL modifiers stack multiplicatively like target modifiers. SL Buffer (+50%) + 1/2 SL produces a net widening of only 25% — probably not what you intended. Always check the resulting SL distance on a chart label.

Short Setup — Trend & Filters

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 26. SHORT SETUP: TREND & FILTERS ■
🪞 SHORT MIRROR — FULL CONTENT
This chapter is the complete short-side equivalent of Group 15. All settings, logic, and guidance apply identically with directional language reversed. If Setup Mirroring (Group 5) is active with "Shorts Mirror Longs", these settings are ignored and Group 15 governs both directions.

Optional quality filters that must pass before a detected setup is accepted as valid. These reduce the quantity of setups but aim to improve quality by requiring additional confirmation. Every filter is OFF by default — the recommended approach is to add them one at a time, comparing the Insight Monitor before and after. Group 15 is the long equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
SMA FilterON / OFFRequire price to be below the SMA (for shorts) before accepting a setup.
SMA Length2 – 500SMA period. Default 200. Common alternatives: 50, 100, 21.
Invert SMAON / OFFFlips the logic: shorts require price ABOVE the SMA. Mean-reversion setup.
MSS/BOS FilterON / OFFRequire a recent Market Structure Shift or Break of Structure in the trade's direction.
Bullish DivergenceON / OFFRequire RSI bullish divergence within the lookback window before accepting a short.
RSI Lookback5 – 200How many bars the divergence scan searches backwards.
RSI Length2 – 50RSI calculation period. Default 14.
Min R:R FilterOFF / 0.5 – 5.0Rejects any setup whose R:R falls below the threshold. Cleanest edge-preservation filter in the group.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Filter philosophy — the trade-offHOW & WHY

Every filter costs detections. Each filter you add reduces the pool of setups that pass through to the Monitor. Fewer setups = smaller sample = less reliable stats. There's a minimum-detections threshold beneath which Profit Factor becomes statistically meaningless (about 100 setups).

The goal: The minimum number of filters that maximise Profit Factor with a trade count you're comfortable with. More filters is not inherently better.

The method: Enable one filter at a time. Check the Monitor. Record Profit Factor and detection count. Disable. Try a different filter. Compare independently. Only combine filters after individual testing.

▶SMA Filter — trend alignmentHOW & WHY

What it does: Requires price to be below the configured SMA before a long setup is accepted. 'Only trade with the trend' expressed as a quantitative rule.

SMA Length: Default 200 — the classic institutional trend line. 50 is more responsive (weekly swing structure). 21 is intraday. Longer SMA = stricter trend requirement; shorter SMA = more permissive.

Invert SMA: Flips the rule. Shorts require price ABOVE the SMA. This creates a mean-reversion filter — only take longs when price is extended below trend and likely to snap back.

When it helps: Strong trending markets. A simple trend filter dramatically cleans up counter-trend losers.

When it hurts: Range-bound markets. Your setups will be stuck waiting for trend confirmation that never comes; detection count drops to near-zero.

▶MSS/BOS Filter — structural confirmationHOW & WHY

Market Structure Shift (MSS): When price breaks the most recent opposing-direction swing. A bullish MSS occurs when price breaks above the most recent confirmed swing high after a series of lower highs.

Break of Structure (BOS): When price breaks a swing high in an uptrend (continuation) or a swing low in a downtrend.

What the filter requires: A recent MSS or BOS in the trade's direction before a setup is accepted. Swing Strength (Group 5) determines what qualifies as a swing — and therefore how strict this filter is.

Why it works: Structural breaks confirm institutional intent. Taking setups only after structure has broken in your direction filters out early-reversal attempts that will fail.

▶RSI Divergence FilterHOW & WHY

What it detects: Classic RSI divergence — price making a new low while RSI makes a higher low (bullish), or vice versa for bearish.

RSI Lookback: How many bars back the scan searches. Short (20–50) = only recent divergences count. Long (100–200) = older divergences are still valid. Shorter = stricter filter.

RSI Length: The RSI calculation period. Default 14 is canonical. Shorter (7, 10) produces more divergences; longer (21, 28) produces fewer but cleaner ones.

When it helps: Reversal-trading setups. Divergence is a classical reversal signal; pairing it with a fresh FVG or OB dramatically filters to high-quality reversals.

When it hurts: Strong trending markets. Divergences fire constantly in trends and most fail. Disable in trend.

▶Min R:R Filter — the edge-preservation classicHOW & WHY

What it does: Rejects any setup whose natural R:R (target distance / SL distance) is below the threshold. Min R:R 1.0 means target must be at least 1× as far as the stop; Min R:R 2.0 means 2×.

Why it's the cleanest filter: Directly protects expected value. Even at 40% Win Rate, a consistent 2:1 R:R setup has a positive expectancy. Min R:R Filter guarantees the math stays favourable.

Trade-off: Higher thresholds reject more setups. Min R:R 1.0 typically removes 10–20% of detections; Min R:R 2.0 removes 30–50%; Min R:R 3.0 can remove 70%+.

Recommended default: Start at 1.0 (never take a trade with worse-than-even R:R). Tighten only if the resulting Profit Factor is below 1.5.

🎯 PRO TIP
Start with NO filters. Study the Insight Monitor. Add filters one at a time. Compare Profit Factor before and after. If a filter improves PF but leaves very few trades (under 50), it may be overfitting — reject it even if the PF looks beautiful.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
The Min R:R Filter is the safest filter to leave on permanently. Every other filter can produce configuration-specific edge cases; Min R:R preserves expected value as a universal rule.
⚠️ OVERFITTING TRAP
Combining four filters that each improve Profit Factor doesn't mean their combination is better. Each filter removes setups; the remaining sample may be too small to be reliable. Always verify the combined detection count is at least 100.

🪞 SHORT MIRROR — FULL CONTENT

Fine-tuning settings that apply to specific ICT models. These let you customise how individual model types behave beyond the core entry, target, and SL configuration. Most have no effect on the Displacement model. Group 16 is the long equivalent.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
OB Candle RangeWicks / BodiesApplies to OB and Breaker. Whether the OB range uses the candle's full wick or just its body. Wicks = wider, more fills; Bodies = tighter, better R:R.
Strict OB ColourON / OFFOnly accept OB setups where the OB candle's colour matches the expected bias (red for short OBs, green for long OBs).
OB Entry MethodOB Range / Disp. Candle RangeWhether to enter within the OB candle's range or within the displacement candle's range.
OB Liquidity SweepON / OFFRequire the OB candle to have swept a prior swing before accepting the setup.

i-FVG Expiry0 – 1000 barsBars before an unfilled i-FVG expires. 0 = never expires.
OB / Breaker Expiry0 – 1000 barsBars before an unfilled OB or Breaker expires.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶OB Candle Range — Wicks or Bodies?HOW & WHY

Wicks: The OB range is defined by the OB candle's full range — from wick top to wick bottom. The entry zone is wider.

Bodies: The OB range is defined by just the candle body — open to close, ignoring wicks. The entry zone is tighter.

Bodies wins on R:R. A tighter entry zone means entries land deeper into the OB candle's structure on average, which gives better R:R on winners.

Wicks wins on fill rate. A wider entry zone catches more retracements that don't quite reach the body. Detection counts are higher; Missed Entries lower.

▶Strict OB ColourHOW & WHY

What it does: Enforces the classical ICT OB definition — a bullish OB must be a red candle (the last down-close before a bullish displacement) and a bearish OB must be a green candle. Disabling this allows any OB candle colour.

Why keep it ON: The colour rule is part of what makes an OB an OB. A green candle before a bullish displacement is just a continuation candle, not a structural imbalance.

Why it might be OFF: On very low-timeframe charts where candle colours are noisy. You may find the non-strict version produces more reliable setups because the structural concept matters more than the colour.

▶OB Entry MethodHOW & WHY

OB Range: The entry level (Group 6) is computed within the OB candle itself. Standard ICT OB entry.

Disp. Candle Range: The entry level is computed within the displacement candle (the candle immediately following the OB). This is often a different, typically wider range.

When Disp. Candle Range helps: On assets where the OB is very compact (tight-bodied candles) but the displacement is large. Using the displacement range gives you a more useful entry zone — otherwise your entries are extremely tight to the OB close.

▶OB Liquidity Sweep requirementHOW & WHY

What it does: Only accepts an OB setup if the OB candle itself swept a prior swing low (for bullish OBs) or prior swing high (for bearish OBs). The OB is effectively also a liquidity grab.

Why it matters: An OB that swept liquidity before the displacement represents institutional 'clearing' of retail stops before the real move. These OBs have a higher fill-and-follow-through rate than bare OBs.

Cost: Dramatically reduces OB detections. On a typical 5-minute chart, maybe 30–50% of raw OBs also swept liquidity. Enabling this filter trims the OB pool significantly but tightens its quality.

▶FVG (Displacement) requirementHOW & WHY

What it does: Only accepts a Displacement-model setup if the displacement candle also formed an FVG (three-candle pattern with a gap). Effectively turns the Displacement model into 'Displacement AND FVG confluence'.

Why it's powerful: Pure Displacement detections produce many setups, but some are just large single candles with no structural gap. The FVG requirement ensures each Displacement detection also carries an unfilled gap — a structural pull point.

Alternative: Leave Displacement untouched and enable the FVG model separately (Group 6). You'll get both types of setup. The FVG (Displacement) requirement creates a hybrid model that only detects when both conditions are present.

▶Expiry rules — when stale setups dieHOW & WHY

i-FVG Expiry: An unfilled Inversion FVG waits this many bars before retiring itself. 0 = never expires. Typical setting: 100–200 bars. Older i-FVGs lose reliability because the structural context has shifted.

OB / Breaker Expiry: Same concept for OBs and Breakers. OBs are often considered fresher and more reliable; many users set OB Expiry to 50–100 bars.

Why expiry exists: An unmitigated OB from 500 bars ago has a much lower chance of being honoured than one from 50 bars ago. Expiry prevents the simulator from tracking zombie setups indefinitely.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
OB Candle Range = Wicks paired with Strict OB Colour = OFF produces the loosest OB detection — highest count, lowest quality. Bodies + Strict Colour + Liquidity Sweep produces the strictest — lowest count, highest quality. Both extremes have their uses; neither is universally right.
🧪 EXPERIMENT
Enable only the OB model. Run the simulator with OB Candle Range = Wicks, then rerun with Bodies. Compare Monitor. The difference in Profit Factor tells you which the asset respects more. Repeat separately for Strict OB Colour and Liquidity Sweep — each is a quality knob worth tuning once, then locking in.

GROUP 28

Dollar & Tick Filters

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 28. DOLLAR & TICK FILTERS ■

Absolute floor and ceiling controls expressed in real dollar or tick values. These filters operate on every setup regardless of model or direction, providing account-level risk management distinct from the per-setup filters in Groups 15/26.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Min ProfitOFF / Filter / IncreaserMinimum projected profit floor in $ or ticks. Filter rejects setups below it; Increaser extends TP to meet it.
Max DD (Risk)OFF / Filter / ReducerMaximum projected risk ceiling. Filter rejects setups above it; Reducer tightens SL to meet it.
TP Hard CapON + limit valueAbsolute $/tick ceiling on every TP. Any setup whose TP exceeds this cap is truncated to the cap. Capped labels show 💰.
Daily Circuit BreakerON + limit valueHalts new detections after the day's cumulative loss reaches the limit. Open trades continue. Resets at 18:00 EST. Halted setups marked 🛑.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Min Profit — Filter vs IncreaserHOW & WHY

Filter mode: Any setup whose projected profit is below your threshold is rejected entirely. The detection count drops; no chart label appears for rejected setups.

Increaser mode: Same threshold, but instead of rejecting, the TP is extended outward until it meets the threshold. The setup survives; its R:R improves; the Missed Entries rate may climb because the now-more-distant TP is harder to reach.

Why it matters: On volatile instruments, Auto Calc can produce legitimately tiny targets on low-momentum candles. A $5 profit target on ES may not be worth the fees. Min Profit Filter trims these out; Increaser reshapes them.

▶Max DD — Filter vs ReducerHOW & WHY

Filter mode: Rejects setups whose projected SL risk exceeds the ceiling. Protects against accidentally taking a trade with uncapped downside.

Reducer mode: Tightens the SL inward to meet the ceiling. Preserves the setup but changes its structural profile. May produce stops that are inside important levels — use with care.

Pairing with Group 4: Max DD in dollars only makes sense if Group 4's Margin/Leverage/Contracts are configured correctly. Otherwise the $-threshold refers to nominal chart distance, not your actual risk.

▶TP Hard Cap — absolute ceilingHOW & WHY

What it does: After all other target calculations and modifiers, if the projected TP distance in dollars or ticks exceeds the cap, the TP is truncated to the cap. The trade label shows the 💰 emoji.

Why it exists: On volatile assets, Auto Calc + TP Expansion can occasionally produce wildly-optimistic targets (hundreds of points on NQ). A Hard Cap ensures you don't sit holding a trade waiting for a target that statistically won't hit.

Tuning: Set the cap to roughly 2–3× your historical Average Realised PnL from a similar setup. Far enough to capture most wins, close enough to prevent runaway over-targets.

▶Daily Circuit Breaker — live-trading safety netHOW & WHY

What it does: Tracks cumulative realised loss per trading day. If the loss reaches the limit, new setups stop being registered. Open trades continue to run — the breaker prevents new exposure, not existing positions.

Reset: 18:00 EST (the end of the traditional US trading day). Next day, detections resume.

Chart visual: Setups that would have been detected during the halt still appear on the chart but are marked 🛑 HALTED and excluded from all active-trade calculations.

Important caveat: Circuit Breaker should never trigger on clean asset data during normal market conditions. If it's firing frequently on your backtest, investigate — you're either risking too much per trade (Group 4) or the underlying data has anomalies. It's a safety net, not a tuning target.

⚠️ CIRCUIT BREAKER WARNING
Circuit Breaker should never trigger on a clean, well-configured setup during normal market conditions. Frequent triggers indicate either excessive per-trade risk (fix Group 4) or outlier data in your backtest window (check the chart). The breaker exists as insurance, not as a tuning tool.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
Min Profit and Max DD in Filter mode are defensive; in Increaser/Reducer mode they're shape-changing. Filter preserves the setup's structure but culls; Increaser/Reducer preserves the setup but distorts it. When in doubt, use Filter.

Deep dive · click any setting
▶How killzone filters workHOW & WHY

When Enable Killzones is OFF: The simulator scans every bar in the lookback window for setups. No time-based filtering.

When Enable Killzones is ON: Only bars that fall within at least one enabled killzone window are scanned for setups. A bar outside all enabled zones is silently skipped — no detection event occurs at all.

Overlap handling: Some zones overlap (London Close extends into NY AM on some interpretations). Enabling multiple overlapping zones extends the detection window; it doesn't double-count anything.

Timezone note: All times in EST. TradingView's chart timezone is independent — a 5-minute bar timestamped 08:30 GMT is analysed as 04:30 EST for killzone purposes.

▶London — 02:00–05:00 ESTHOW & WHY

Character: First major session. Strong directional moves that often set the range for the rest of the trading day. High-quality displacement setups; institutional position-building.

Works well for: FVG and OB models. The displacement during London Open frequently creates clean, tradeable ICT structures.

Asset sweet spot: FX majors, European index futures (DAX, FTSE). Also productive for US index futures as they react to European flow.

▶NY AM — 07:00–10:00 ESTHOW & WHY

Character: The highest-volume window of the day. Most setups detected. Most explosive moves. Most noise.

Works well for: All five ICT models. This is where ICT methodology was originally calibrated — it works best here.

Asset sweet spot: US index futures (ES, NQ, RTY), US large-cap equities, crude oil (CL), gold (GC). The default session for most US-market traders.

▶London Close — 10:00–12:00 ESTHOW & WHY

Character: Post-NY-Open reversal zone. The initial NY momentum often exhausts around 10:00 and price reverses or consolidates.

Works well for: Reversal models — i-FVG, Breaker Block. Counter-trend setups during this window have a higher hit rate than during NY AM.

▶NY Lunch — 12:00–13:00 ESTHOW & WHY

Character: Low volume, choppy. Frequently produces failed setups as price drifts in a narrow range.

Default recommendation: OFF. Unless the Monitor shows genuine NY Lunch edge on your asset (unusual), disable this killzone.

▶NY PM — 13:00–16:00 ESTHOW & WHY

Character: Second major US session. Volume picks up into the close. Afternoon trends and session-end positioning.

Works well for: Continuation setups (Displacement, OB) and NY AM-confirmation reversals. Many traders find NY PM produces quality trades at lower detection volume.

▶Asian — 19:00 EST to 02:00 EST (next day)HOW & WHY

Character: Range-building session. Price often establishes the day's structural boundaries during this window. Lower volatility than London/NY but cleaner levels.

Works well for: Liquidity-sweep setups — Asian-session highs and lows become targets for London Open sweeps. Trading during Asian is slow but productive on JPY pairs, AUD pairs, Nikkei futures.

Use the Hour Analysis page on the Insight Monitor (Group 1) to verify which of these sessions actually produce edge on YOUR asset. If Killzone Win Rate (Full Stats) is 65% and global Win Rate is 30%, turn on Killzone Filter and leave only the profitable sessions enabled.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
Time is more important than price. Enabling the right killzones typically moves Profit Factor more than any price-based filter. Check Killzone Win Rate vs global Win Rate — the gap is your time edge.

Time-based filters using the 8 canonical ICT Macro windows — narrower than killzones, aligned to known institutional rebalancing events. When Enable Macros is ON, setups are only detected within enabled macro windows. All times EST.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does

24-Hour Macro TimelineLive session view

Live Session Timeline
Deep dive · click any setting
▶What is a Macro?HOW & WHY

ICT definition: A Macro is a recurring time window during which ICT methodology observes higher-than-baseline rates of algorithmic displacement. Macros are tighter than killzones — typically 20–30 minutes — and are calibrated to specific known institutional rebalancing events (margin calls, options expiries, fund-redemption windows).

Why they work: Institutional algorithms execute on schedule. The 09:50 NY AM 2 macro, for instance, corresponds to the typical post-open rebalancing window where institutional flow re-enters the market after the 09:30 open settles. These schedules are remarkably stable across years.

Relationship to killzones: Most macros sit inside killzones. A trade during NY AM 1 is also during the NY AM killzone. Enabling both filters gives you the tightest possible detection window.

▶London Macros (London 1, London 2)HOW & WHY

London 1 (02:33–03:00): Early London 'manipulation' window — institutions often build positions in the opposite direction of the true intended move, creating liquidity pools that they later sweep.

London 2 (04:03–04:30): Continuation / reversal window. By this point the true session bias has emerged. Clean structural setups often form here.

Best on: FX majors, DAX, FTSE. Less productive on US futures (their overnight session is quieter).

▶NY AM Macros (NY AM 1, NY AM 2)HOW & WHY

NY AM 1 (08:50–09:10): Straddles the 09:30 NYSE open. Pre-open manipulation and opening positioning. Explosive but noisy.

NY AM 2 (09:50–10:10): The most studied macro. Post-open algorithms rebalance after the initial rush settles. Cleaner, more tradeable setups than NY AM 1.

Best on: ES, NQ, RTY, US large-cap equities, SPY/QQQ. The canonical NY AM environment.

▶Mid-Morning + London CloseHOW & WHY

Mid-Morning (10:50–11:10): Late-morning exhaustion window. Initial NY momentum has peaked; reversals and profit-taking dominate.

London Close (11:50–12:10): European session wraps up; US traders establish positions for the afternoon. Transitional volatility.

▶NY PM Macros (NY PM 1, NY PM 2)HOW & WHY

NY PM 1 (13:50–14:10): Afternoon momentum window. Fresh institutional flow into the second session. Good for continuation setups.

NY PM 2 (15:15–15:45): Final-hour positioning. The longest of the macros (30 minutes). Heavy institutional rebalancing ahead of the 16:00 close — frequently produces the most explosive displacement of the day.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Macros work best as an ADD-ON to Killzones, not a replacement. Enable the killzone for your session, then layer macros on top. If global Profit Factor is 1.2 and Macro Profit Factor is 2.0, your edge is concentrated in those narrow windows.
🎯 PRO TIP
For aggressive scalpers: disable killzones entirely and enable only macros. This gives you the smallest detection window possible — typically 3–4 hours of trading a day spread across 8 precise windows, with detection density concentrated where algorithms predictably execute.
🧪 EXPERIMENT
Use the Hour Analysis page on the Insight Monitor to verify macros produce edge on YOUR asset before committing. Some assets respond to killzones but not macros; others (particularly ES/NQ) respond to both.

▶PositioningHOW & WHY

Nine anchor points: The Monitor snaps to one of 9 fixed positions. Pick based on where your chart's empty space typically is — if you trade on the right side of the chart, put the Monitor top-left. If you use the drawing toolbar on the left, put it top-right.

Why position matters: A well-placed Monitor is read at a glance without covering price action. A badly-placed one is constantly closed and reopened. Worth tuning once for each chart layout.

Multiple instances: If you run two IOTSIM instances (see Life Hacks), put them in opposite corners. Each Monitor stays separate and readable.

▶Size and ColoursHOW & WHY

Table Size: Normal works for 99% of users. Tiny is useful on laptops with small screens or when running multiple instances. Large is helpful for screen-share presentations or if you have vision constraints.

Background with opacity: Most important visual setting. Fully opaque is easier to read but covers price; semi-transparent preserves chart visibility but strains reading. 60–80% opacity is usually the sweet spot.

Long/Short colours: Make them distinct enough to instantly recognise which column you're reading. The defaults (green longs / red shorts) work for most users. Colour-blind users may prefer blue/orange or yellow/purple.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
The Monitor's visual configuration is purely aesthetic but can dramatically affect usability. A Monitor you can read without disrupting your chart reading is worth more than one with comprehensive data that's constantly in your way.

Tuning-only rows: Max Avg SL Unrealised, Missed PnL, 2nd Chance Win Rate, Killzone/Macro Win Rates — useful while optimising but not needed during active trading. Hide them once the setup is stable.

▶How visuals relate to simulationHOW & WHY

Visual groups do not affect detection. Nothing in this group changes what the simulator finds or how trades behave. These settings control only what's drawn on the chart.

Performance consideration: Drawing dense visuals (many FVGs, OBs, rays) can slow down TradingView on low-end hardware. If you're experiencing sluggishness, consider disabling feature-visibility here. The simulator still uses the underlying structural detection.

Toggle visibility per-feature, not per-category. You might disable FVG boxes but keep OB boxes — depending on which features matter to how you read the chart.

▶Choosing coloursHOW & WHY

Contrast matters more than hue. On dark-themed charts, muted mid-brightness colours read better than saturated neons. On light charts, the opposite.

Use colour to encode meaning. If you use a consistent colour palette across all PD array types (e.g. greens for bullish, reds for bearish, regardless of model), your eye learns to parse the chart faster than if every feature has a unique colour.

Opacity over saturation. A 40% opacity of a fully-saturated colour is easier to read through than a 100% opacity of a desaturated colour — you still see candles behind the box.

💡 NO SIMULATION IMPACT
Visual groups (33–41) never affect detection, trade management, or stats. Change these settings freely to suit your visual preferences — the Insight Monitor numbers are identical regardless.

▶How visuals relate to simulationHOW & WHY

Visual groups do not affect detection. Nothing in this group changes what the simulator finds or how trades behave. These settings control only what's drawn on the chart.

Performance consideration: Drawing dense visuals (many FVGs, OBs, rays) can slow down TradingView on low-end hardware. If you're experiencing sluggishness, consider disabling feature-visibility here. The simulator still uses the underlying structural detection.

Toggle visibility per-feature, not per-category. You might disable FVG boxes but keep OB boxes — depending on which features matter to how you read the chart.

▶Choosing coloursHOW & WHY

Contrast matters more than hue. On dark-themed charts, muted mid-brightness colours read better than saturated neons. On light charts, the opposite.

Use colour to encode meaning. If you use a consistent colour palette across all PD array types (e.g. greens for bullish, reds for bearish, regardless of model), your eye learns to parse the chart faster than if every feature has a unique colour.

Opacity over saturation. A 40% opacity of a fully-saturated colour is easier to read through than a 100% opacity of a desaturated colour — you still see candles behind the box.

💡 NO SIMULATION IMPACT
Visual groups (33–41) never affect detection, trade management, or stats. Change these settings freely to suit your visual preferences — the Insight Monitor numbers are identical regardless.

GROUP 42

Trade Labels & Rays

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 42. TRADE LABELS & RAYS ■

Controls the visual appearance of trade labels, entry/TP/SL lines, and rays on the chart. All customisation is purely visual — nothing here affects detection, trade management, or Monitor statistics.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Detected Label ColourAny colourBase colour for pending-setup labels (🎱).
Active Long ColourAny colourColour for active long trades (🤞🏻).
Active Short ColourAny colourColour for active short trades.
Winning Trade ColourAny colourColour for trades closed at TP (✅).
Losing Trade ColourAny colourColour for trades closed at SL (❌).
Missed ColourAny colourColour for ⛔️ Missed setups.
EOD Close ColourAny colourColour for ⏰ EOD-closed trades.
2nd Chance ColourAny colourColour for re-detected setups.
Entry Line Width1 – 5Thickness of the entry line.
TP Line Width1 – 5Thickness of the TP line.
