Label SizeTiny / Small / Normal / Large / HugeText size for all trade labels.
Label Text ColourAny colourText colour within labels.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Colour-coding strategyHOW & WHY

Distinguish category, not direction. Detected, Active, Win, Loss, Missed, EOD — six outcomes, six distinct colours. Within each category, the direction (Long vs Short) can share a hue but differ in brightness or saturation.

Defaults: Detected = blue (neutral future), Active = green/red (live directional), Win = bright green, Loss = bright red, Missed = grey-red (⛔️), EOD = amber. These are readable for most users; customise when colour-blind or screen-specific needs apply.

The 'all green' trap: Don't make every positive-outcome trade the same green. Your eye needs to distinguish a live active trade from a closed winner at a glance. Use different brightness levels or saturations.

▶Line widths and stylesHOW & WHY

Entry line: Typically the thickest. It's the reference point for both TP and SL so it should dominate.

TP line: Medium thickness, solid. Your profit target — should be clearly visible but not compete with the entry.

SL line: Often made dashed or dotted to visually distinguish it from TP. Some users prefer it bolder (you need to see where your risk is); others prefer it subtler (you don't want to fixate on potential losses).

Rays extending right: All three lines extend to the right of the current bar by default, projecting into the future. The rays visually 'reserve' the zone where the trade will resolve.

▶Label size choicesHOW & WHY

Tiny and Small: Best for multi-trade charts with 20+ concurrent labels. Each label is legible but doesn't dominate.

Normal: The sweet spot for most users. Easy to read at any chart zoom.

Large and Huge: Screen-share presentations, recording videos, or vision-impaired users. Otherwise typically too large.

GROUP 43

Visibility Limits

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 43. VISIBILITY LIMITS ■

Caps on how many trade objects are drawn per category. Preserves TradingView's object limits and keeps chart rendering fast. These limits only affect what's drawn — all stats are still computed in full.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Show PnL/Ticks on LabelsON / OFFMaster toggle for the PnL/Tick value shown inside trade labels. ON shows live PnL on every detected/active/closed label. OFF hides values for a cleaner chart.
Max Detected1 – 500Maximum pending setups drawn at once. Older ones drop off as new ones detect. Default 30.
Max Active1 – 500Maximum active trades drawn at once. Default 30.
Max Closed1 – 2000Maximum closed trades drawn when Show All Closed is ON. Default 100.
Max Missed1 – 500Maximum missed setups drawn when Show Missed is ON. Default 50.
Max Wins1 – 500Max winning trades when Show Wins is ON.
Max Losses1 – 500Max losing trades when Show Losses is ON.
Max 2nd Chance1 – 500Max 2nd Chance setups drawn.
Max Partials1 – 500Max partial-profit lines drawn.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶Why limits existHOW & WHY

TradingView object limit: Pine Script has a ceiling on how many lines, boxes, and labels a single indicator can draw (typically 500 of each). Exceeding the limit causes 'Undefined object limit exceeded' errors.

The simulator tracks everything, but draws what fits. All stats continue to be computed for every trade regardless of visibility — the limits only control drawing. If you hit the limit on a long lookback, your Monitor still shows accurate stats; the chart just draws the N most-recent trades.

Performance consideration: Even well below the hard limit, more drawings = slower chart rendering. If TradingView feels sluggish on your setup, halve the limits — you'll barely notice the visual difference but the performance impact can be dramatic.

▶Recommended valuesHOW & WHY

Live trading configuration (clean chart): Max Detected 10, Max Active 10, Max Closed 0 (hidden anyway), everything else 0. Chart shows only what matters right now.

Analysis configuration: Max Detected 30 (default), Max Active 30, Max Closed 200, Max Wins 100, Max Losses 100. Plenty of context for pattern recognition without overwhelming the chart.

Deep review configuration: Max Closed 500+, Max Missed 300+. Used briefly during weekly reviews, then reset.

⚠️ OBJECT LIMIT ERROR
If TradingView shows 'Undefined object limit exceeded', reduce these visibility caps. The limit is on total objects across all categories — the sum of everything drawn must stay under ~500.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
Stats are independent of visibility. The simulator tracks every trade; these limits only control the chart render. Half your visibility → same stats, faster chart.
g44:
GROUP 44

Custom Date Range

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 44. CUSTOM DATE RANGE ■

Pins the simulation to a fixed date window when Sim Mode (Group 3) is set to Custom Date Range. Useful for reproducible tuning, event-specific testing, and cross-regime comparison.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
From DateAny date-timeStart of the date range when Sim Mode (Group 3) = Custom Date Range. The simulator ignores all bars before this timestamp.
To DateAny date-timeEnd of the date range. The simulator ignores bars after this timestamp.
Custom Time/Day FilterON / OFFMaster toggle for the session/day filter. When ON, trades only fire if both the time window AND the day-of-week boxes for the active day are satisfied.
Time WindowHHMM-HHMMSession window in chart-local time (NOT EST — set chart timezone to EST first if needed). Default 0730-1100 covers NY AM.
Sun / Mon / Tue / Wed / Thu / Fri / SatON / OFFPer-day toggles. All ON by default. Turn OFF Sun/Sat for futures/stocks; turn OFF Mon to skip Monday gap behaviour, etc.
Deep dive · click any setting
▶When to use Custom Date RangeHOW & WHY

Event-specific testing: Cover an exact earnings week, an FOMC announcement day, a holiday session, or a known trend regime. Pin the window so every rerun uses identical bars.

Reproducible configuration development: When tuning a setup, a rolling Lookback Period changes under your feet — yesterday's stats aren't today's stats even with identical settings. A fixed date range locks the dataset so you can compare apples to apples.

Historical regime comparison: Run the same configuration across three Custom Date Ranges — Q1, Q2, Q3 of a year — and see whether your setup's edge is regime-dependent.

▶What Custom Date Range doesn't doHOW & WHY

It doesn't extend beyond TradingView's chart data. If your chart only shows 6 months of 5-minute bars, your From Date can't reach further back than that.

It doesn't use wall-clock time for 'now'. The To Date is a fixed timestamp, not a live cursor. Trades after To Date are invisible even if they're happening right now.

It's incompatible with Lookback Period. Sim Mode in Group 3 picks one or the other — not both. If you want a rolling window that always ends 'now', use Lookback.

▶Custom Time/Day FilterHOW & WHY

What it does: A separate filter from the date-range bookends above. Where the date range controls the WINDOW of the backtest, this controls the SESSIONS WITHIN it. When the master toggle is ON, trades only fire if both the time-of-day window AND the day-of-week toggle for the active day are satisfied.

Time Window: Standard TradingView session input. Format HHMM-HHMM. Default 0730-1100 covers the NY AM session including the macro windows around the open. Custom example: 0900-1130 for European-session-only trading. Important: This input uses chart-local time (NOT EST), unlike killzones. Set your chart timezone to EST first if you want session times to match killzone boundaries.

Day toggles: Common configurations — Monday-Friday only (Sun/Sat OFF) for stocks/futures; weekdays without Monday (Mon OFF) if your asset routinely gaps and you want to skip Monday's catch-up bar; Sunday-only crypto (all OFF except Sun) paired with a specific time window.

How this combines with killzones: If both the killzone filter (Group 29) and this custom filter are ON, BOTH must be satisfied for a trade to fire. Killzones are session-based (London, NY AM, etc.); this filter is window-based. They compound — use one or the other, not usually both, unless you have a very specific reason.

When to enable: Forward-testing strategies that target specific weekly schedules (e.g. only NY AM, Tue-Thu) without modifying killzone definitions.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Use Custom Date Range for tuning reproducibility. Use Lookback Period for live tracking. Swap between them as the task demands.

GROUP 45

System & Alerts

INDICATOR SETTINGS PANE → 45. SYSTEM & ALERTS ■

Performance controls and the complete alert system. The system settings tune how TradingView renders the indicator; the alert system lets you bridge simulated detections to real-world notifications and broker webhooks.

SettingOptionsWhat It Does
Log ScaleON / OFFUse logarithmic distance calculations. For very large price ranges (BTC, ETH). Most traders leave OFF.
Performance ModeON / OFFReduces visual update frequency to speed up TradingView. Enable if the chart feels sluggish.
Update Interval1 – 20Bars between visual refreshes when Performance Mode is ON. Default 5. Higher = faster chart, more stale labels.
Freeze Closed TradesON / OFFStops redrawing closed-trade visuals after they close. Saves memory on long lookbacks.
Alert: New DetectionON / OFFFires when a new setup is detected.
Alert: Entry HitON / OFFFires when a detected setup's entry price is filled.
Alert: TP1 HitON / OFFFires when the first partial TP is reached.
Alert: TP2 HitON / OFFFires when TP2 is reached.
Alert: TP3 HitON / OFFFires when TP3 is reached.
Alert: Final TP HitON / OFFFires when the final TP closes the remaining position.
Alert: SL HitON / OFFFires when SL closes the trade.
Alert: TSL UpdateON / OFFFires when a trailing SL moves.
Alert: B.E. TriggeredON / OFFFires when TP1 Secure moves SL to breakeven.
Alert: EOD CloseON / OFFFires when EOD Auto-Close triggers.
Alert TemplateCustom textThe message body sent with every alert. Supports {symbol}, {timeframe}, {direction}, {event}, {price}, {id}.

Alert Template BuilderInteractive

Click placeholder chips to insert them into your template. The compiled output shows what the alert body looks like for a sample live trade.

Alert Template Builder
{symbol} {timeframe} {direction} {event} {price} {id}
NQ1! 5m · LONG · ENTRY_HIT @ 18240.50
Deep dive · click any setting
▶System settings — Log Scale, Performance Mode, Freeze ClosedHOW & WHY

Log Scale: Use logarithmic distance calculations for deviations. Needed when price range is huge relative to tick size — BTC spanning $15k to $100k, ETH from $800 to $4,500. On linear assets (ES, NQ) leave OFF.

Performance Mode: Reduces visual update frequency to every Nth bar rather than every bar. Dramatically improves chart responsiveness on long lookbacks or dense setups. Stats are unaffected — only the rendering cadence changes.

Update Interval (1–20): Sets how many bars between visual refreshes when Performance Mode is ON. Default 5 — every fifth confirmed bar triggers a full visual rebuild. Higher numbers = fewer updates = faster chart but more stale labels between refreshes. Recommended: 1–3 for live trading with mild lag, 5 default for most setups, 10–15 for heavy backtesting.

Freeze Closed Trades (No-Update Closed Trades): Once a trade closes, its visual is frozen — the label and lines don't update further. Closed trades don't change, so re-rendering them every tick is wasted work. ON by default and almost always should stay ON. Saves substantial CPU on long lookbacks.

Performance hierarchy (most → least impactful): 1) Bars To Scan (Group 3) is the single biggest performance lever. 2) Performance Mode + Freeze Closed both ON. 3) Reduce Max Detected/Active/Closed (Group 43). 4) Disable visuals you don't need (Groups 33–41). 5) Increase Update Interval. If chart is unusable, lower Bars To Scan to 1,000 first — most performance complaints disappear at that point.

▶Alert event typesHOW & WHY

New Detection: Fires the instant a setup is created. Get notified before entries fill so you can size in manually.

Entry Hit: Fires when price reaches entry and the simulated order fills. Most commonly-used alert — this is the moment to execute the trade on your live broker.

TP1 / TP2 / TP3 Hit: Partial-profit alerts. Useful for mirroring the simulator's partial-closures on your live position.

Final TP Hit: The full target is reached and the trade closes fully. A win event.

SL Hit: Trade closed at stop loss. Losing event.

TSL Update: Trailing SL moved. Relevant only for Standard/Advanced Trail (Group 11).

B.E. Triggered: TP1 Secure (Group 13) moved the SL to breakeven. Trade is now risk-free.

EOD Close: Session ended; active trade was force-closed.

▶Alert template syntaxHOW & WHY

Placeholders: The template supports six placeholders — {symbol}, {timeframe}, {direction}, {event}, {price}, {id}. Each is replaced with the trade's actual value at alert-firing time.

{symbol}: The ticker (e.g. NQ1!, BTCUSD).

{timeframe}: The chart timeframe (e.g. 5m, 1h).

{direction}: LONG or SHORT.

{event}: The alert event name (NEW_DETECTION, ENTRY_HIT, TP1_HIT, etc.).

{price}: The price at which the event occurred.

{id}: The trade's unique ID (e.g. L-FVG-0417a). Use this to correlate alerts to specific labels on the chart.

Integration templates:

For Discord webhook: {"content": "{symbol} {timeframe} {direction} {event} @ {price}"}

For Telegram bot: {symbol} {timeframe} · {direction} · {event} @ {price} · ID {id}

For broker execution webhooks: Check the broker's payload spec. TradingView replaces placeholders before sending, so your template becomes the exact payload body.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Alerts let you step away from the chart. Enable Entry Hit on your phone and you can work or train while the simulator watches for your setups. When the alert fires, open TradingView and execute — the setup is already sitting there as a Detected label.
🎯 PRO TIP
For fully automated workflows, combine Entry Hit alerts with a broker webhook. Your template becomes the broker's execution payload, and your simulator's detections trigger live trades autonomously. Start with tiny size while verifying the integration works reliably.
⚠️ WATCH OUT
Enabling all alert types simultaneously produces dozens of notifications per day on active setups. Pick the two or three you actually need (Entry Hit + Final TP Hit + SL Hit is typical) and leave the rest OFF.
part2-intro:
PART 2

The Insight Monitor

The Monitor is where every configuration decision shows its results. Part 2 breaks down each of the six Monitor pages, every row, every statistic, and how to interpret them in practice.

Part 2 is the companion to Part 1. Where Part 1 documents what each setting does, Part 2 documents what the settings produce — every stat, every row, every page of the Insight Monitor, interpreted and explained.

The Insight Monitor as a Feedback Loop

The Monitor is not a passive display. It's the feedback mechanism that closes the optimisation loop: you change a setting → the Monitor updates → you read the change → you decide what to adjust next. Without a clear read of the Monitor, optimisation is guesswork.

That means reading the Monitor correctly is the highest-leverage skill in this manual. A misread number leads to the wrong next setting change. A misinterpreted stat leads to confidence in a configuration that isn't actually profitable. Part 2 exists to prevent both.

Six Pages, One Dataset

Every Monitor page — Full Stats, Overview, Day Analysis, Hour Analysis, Recent Trades, Model Leaderboard — draws from the same underlying calculation. Switching pages doesn't recalculate anything; it just changes which view you're seeing.

This matters because each page is optimised for a different question. Full Stats is the diagnostic microscope. Overview is the pre-session briefing. Day and Hour Analysis are timing microscopes. Recent Trades is the streak tracker. Model Leaderboard is the pruning shears.

ChapterPageAnswers the question
AFull StatsHow is this configuration performing on every dimension at once?
BOverviewIs this configuration still broadly healthy?
CDay AnalysisWhich days of the week does this configuration profit on?
DHour AnalysisWhich hours of the day does this configuration profit on?
ERecent TradesWhat's happened in the last N trades?
FModel LeaderboardWhich of my five models is carrying the profitability?

How to Use Part 2

Read Chapter A first. Full Stats is the deepest page — every concept introduced later builds on terminology defined there. The subsequent chapters cover pages with smaller stat sets and reference back to Full Stats for anything in common.

Then skim the chapters for the pages you use most. Most traders live on Overview during trading and Full Stats during tuning. Day Analysis and Hour Analysis become relevant during session-tuning passes. Model Leaderboard becomes relevant during weekly reviews.

💡 BEFORE PART 3
Part 3 (Workflows) assumes you understand every stat on every page. If any of the chapters in Part 2 leave you with lingering questions, resolve them before attempting the workflows — you won't know whether a workflow is working if you can't read the Monitor.
part2-fullstats:
PART 2 · CHAPTER A

Full Stats

The deepest Monitor page. Every metric, split into Long, Short, and Total columns. This chapter covers every row, what it means, how to interpret it, and when it signals a configuration change.

Full Stats is the deepest and most diagnostic of the six Monitor pages. It shows every metric the simulator tracks, split into Long, Short, and Total columns. Every row is toggleable via Group 32 — you can build a compact personalised version of this page by hiding rows you don't use. This chapter documents every row, what it means, how to interpret it, and when to act on it.

How to Read the Three Columns

Each row shows three values: Long, Short, Total. The Long and Short columns isolate the two directions' performance; Total aggregates them. Comparing Long vs Short reveals directional asymmetry in your asset — a key input to decisions about Setup Mirroring (Group 5).

If Long PF is 1.8 and Short PF is 0.7, the asset trends. Take the long edge seriously; rework or disable the short side. If both are within 0.3 of each other, your configuration is directionally balanced — mirroring is likely a reasonable baseline.

Counting Rows

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Detected / Closed / Active / PendingHOW & WHY

Detected: Total number of setups detected within the lookback window. Increases with more permissive models (all five on), shorter Entry Levels, and longer lookbacks.

Closed: Total trades that have reached a terminal state (Win, Loss, Missed, EOD). Always ≤ Detected.

Active: Trades currently open at the most recent bar.

Pending: Detected setups whose entry has not yet filled.

What to watch: Detected / 100 = rough rule-of-thumb for statistical reliability. Under 100 total trades, Profit Factor readings are volatile and shouldn't drive configuration decisions.

▶Win RateHOW & WHY

Definition: Wins ÷ (Wins + Losses). EOD closes and Missed setups are excluded from this ratio.

Typical ranges: 30–45% for high R:R setups (wide targets, tight stops). 50–65% for balanced setups. 65–80% for scalps (tight targets, moderate stops). Any setup above 80% is either noise or using Risk Back partials to secure early.

Don't optimise Win Rate alone. A 90% Win Rate with 0.3 R:R is worse expected value than a 40% Win Rate with 3.0 R:R. Pair Win Rate with Profit Factor and Average R:R for a real read.

▶Final TPs Hit %HOW & WHY

Definition: Percentage of closed winning trades that reached the full final TP (as opposed to closing via trail or breakeven at TP1 Secure).

Low value (under 30%): Your TP is too far. Either enable partials (Group 9), tighten the target method, or apply a 1/2 Targets modifier.

High value (over 70%): Your TP may be too close. Try TP Expansion, extend the array depth, or switch to a wider target method.

Partials interaction: If Partial 1 Secure is ON, any trade that hits TP1 then reverses to BE is counted as a win — but Final TPs Hit only tracks reaching full TP. The gap between Win Rate and Final TPs Hit is the 'saved by TP1 Secure' percentage.

▶TP1 Hits / TP2 Hits / TP3 HitsHOW & WHY

Definition: Percentage of trades whose partial TP levels were reached before SL. Separate rows for each enabled partial.

Reading TP1 Hits: Should be higher than Final TPs Hit since TP1 is closer. A large gap between TP1 Hits and Final TPs Hit (e.g. TP1 65%, Final 25%) means your partial banking is doing most of the profit work — the remaining runners rarely complete.

If TP1 Hits is below Win Rate: Something's wrong with the partial placement (Group 9/10). Partials should almost always hit more often than the final TP.

PnL & Value Rows

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Net / Avg RealisedHOW & WHY

Net Realised: Sum of all closed-trade PnL, in $ or ticks depending on Group 4. Your bottom line on the lookback window.

Avg Realised: Net Realised ÷ Closed Trades. What a 'typical' trade earned. Toggle between Average and Median in Group 3 to see how outlier-dependent this is.

Sign conventions: Positive = profit; negative = loss. Green highlight for positive, red for negative.

▶Max / Avg UnrealisedHOW & WHY

Max Unrealised Profit: The highest in-the-money figure any winning trade reached during its lifetime. Shows your setup's peak capture potential.

Avg Unrealised Profit: Average peak-profit across all winning trades. Useful for calibrating TP distance — if Avg Unrealised is 2× your Avg TP Distance, you're systematically leaving money on the table.

▶Max / Avg DDHOW & WHY

Max DD (per trade): The deepest drawdown any single trade reached while open. A measure of per-trade worst-case pain.

Avg DD: Mean drawdown across all trades. How much 'heat' a typical trade takes before resolving.

High Max DD with high Win Rate = you tolerate heat before win. High Max DD with low Win Rate = you tolerate heat then lose anyway. The first is a natural consequence of wider stops; the second is a sign your stops are structurally wrong.

▶Peak Max Realised / Peak Max DDHOW & WHY

Peak Max Realised: The all-time-high cumulative Realised PnL across the lookback — the highest your running total ever reached.

Peak Max DD: The deepest drawdown from Peak Max Realised to any subsequent trough. This is the number a prop firm evaluator watches — it defines your maximum account-level pain.

Always tracked in USD regardless of Group 4's Global Value setting. Account balance is always fiat.

If Peak Max DD is over 20% of Peak Max Realised: Your strategy has equity-curve volatility that many prop programs won't tolerate. Tighten risk, reduce position size (Group 4), or add filters to remove the losing streaks that caused the drawdown.

▶Open / Pending / Total ValueHOW & WHY

Open Value: Sum of unrealised PnL across currently-active trades.

Pending Value: Projected PnL if all currently-detected-but-unfilled setups hit their TP without partial.

Total Value: Open + Pending + Net Realised. Your 'if-everything-works-out-today' total.

TP1 Rows (When Partials Are Enabled)

Deep dive · click any setting
▶TP1 Total / TP1 AvgHOW & WHY

TP1 Total $: Cumulative dollar value of all TP1 closures across the lookback. The 'secured profit' figure.

TP1 Avg $: Average TP1 secured per trade. Useful for understanding how much profit is banked even on trades that subsequently hit SL after the partial.

Interpretation: If TP1 Total is 60%+ of Net Realised, your partials are doing the heavy lifting. Your setup is a 'scalp then runner' configuration. If TP1 Total is below 20% of Net Realised, partials aren't adding much — consider removing them for cleaner diagnostics.

▶TP1 Max / TP1 Avg UnrealisedHOW & WHY

TP1 Max Unrealised Profit: The highest a winning TP1-hit trade reached on its runner portion.

TP1 Avg Unrealised Profit: Average runner-portion peak. Useful for calibrating TP2/TP3/final TP placement after TP1.

Drawdown & Risk Rows

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Max / Avg SL UnrealisedHOW & WHY

The most diagnostic SL-related row on the Monitor.

Max SL Unrealised Profit: The highest in-the-money profit that any losing trade reached before reversing to hit SL. If this is high, at least one trade went significantly into profit before turning into a full loss.

Avg SL Unrealised: Average for all losing trades. The 'typical peak profit before a loser became a loser'.

High values = actionable signal: Your trades go positive before hitting SL. The fix is either (a) B.E. Trail or Advanced Trail (Group 11) to lock in profit, (b) TP1 Secure with a partial (Group 13), or (c) Reduce your R:R tolerance. Any of these eliminates the specific 'went up, then down to SL' failure mode.

▶Net R:R / Avg R:RHOW & WHY

Net R:R: Gross profit in R-multiples ÷ Gross loss in R-multiples. Should be > 1.0 for a profitable system.

Avg R:R: Mean target-to-stop distance ratio across all trades. A pure measure of how ambitious your targets are relative to your stops.

Difference between them: Net R:R is dollar-weighted by actual outcomes; Avg R:R is configuration-level. Both > 1 = good. Avg R:R > 1 but Net R:R < 1 = ambitious setup that isn't hitting often enough. Avg R:R < 1 but Net R:R > 1 = tight-target setup winning on volume.

▶Profit FactorHOW & WHY

The single most-watched metric on the Monitor.

Definition: Gross Profit ÷ Gross Loss. 1.0 is breakeven before fees. 1.5 is meaningful edge. 2.0+ is exceptional. 3.0+ on a sample above 100 trades is suspicious — check for overfitting, look-ahead bias, or unrealistic modifier stacks.

Reading across lookbacks: If PF is 2.5 on 300 bars but 1.1 on 3,000 bars, your edge is regime-specific — it works right now but didn't historically. If PF is stable 1.6 across 500/1,500/3,000/5,000 bars, the edge is durable.

▶Expectancy (derived)HOW & WHY

Not a named row but worth computing: Win Rate × Avg Win − Loss Rate × Avg Loss. In dollar terms: your expected value per trade.

Positive expectancy: Keep trading. Negative expectancy: stop trading immediately until configuration is fixed.

Timing & Session Rows

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Killzone Win RateHOW & WHY

Definition: Win Rate of trades that occurred during enabled killzone windows (Group 29).

Compare to global Win Rate: If Killzone Win Rate is materially higher (5+ percentage points), enabling the Killzone filter is a high-confidence improvement.

If Killzone Win Rate is lower: Either the killzones you've enabled don't match your asset, or some enabled killzones have a negative edge and are dragging the average down. Check Hour Analysis to see which specific hours perform.

▶Macro Win RateHOW & WHY

Definition: Win Rate of trades that occurred during enabled macro windows (Group 30).

Typical pattern: Macro Win Rate ≥ Killzone Win Rate ≥ Global Win Rate. If not, either your macro selections are off for this asset, or the sample is too small to be trusted.

▶Max / Avg Win Bars and Loss BarsHOW & WHY

Avg Win Bars: Mean number of bars a winning trade stayed open.

Avg Loss Bars: Mean number of bars a losing trade stayed open.

Win < Loss pattern (problematic): If losing trades take longer than winning trades, your stops are too far or your trails are too loose — trades sit underwater waiting to resolve. Consider Advanced Trail or a tighter SL modifier.

Win > Loss pattern (healthy): Winners run; losers hit stops quickly. The 'let profits run, cut losses quickly' mantra in statistical form.

Streak & 2nd Chance Rows

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Top W Streak / Top L Streak / Current StreakHOW & WHY

Top W Streak: The longest consecutive run of winning trades in the lookback.

Top L Streak: The longest consecutive run of losing trades.

Current Streak: Your most recent consecutive outcomes (positive number = active win streak; negative = active loss streak).

Psychological use: If Top L Streak is 8+, psychologically prepare yourself for a rough run. Every profitable system has losing streaks; the question is whether you can trade through them without changing settings out of tilt.

▶2nd Chance Win RateHOW & WHY

Definition: Win Rate among trades that were re-detected after an initial missed fill (2nd Chance mechanic, Group 5).

Decision rule: If 2nd Chance Win Rate ≥ Global Win Rate, leave 2nd Chance ON. If it's materially below, disable — re-detections are diluting your edge.

Too few 2nd Chance trades to judge: Run the lookback higher. 2nd Chance samples are typically 20–30% of base detection count; you need 100+ re-detections to trust the Win Rate reading.

💡 READING PROTOCOL
Read Full Stats in this order every session: (1) Detected total (is the sample big enough?) → (2) Profit Factor (is there edge?) → (3) Peak Max DD (is the drawdown survivable?) → (4) Max Avg SL Unrealised (are stops being tagged?). Those four reads in 30 seconds tell you whether the configuration is in a workable state.
🎯 WORKFLOW LINK
Part 3 Chapter A (The Baseline Workflow) walks you through a complete Full Stats read in the context of tuning a new configuration. Come back to this chapter as a reference whenever a specific row needs interpretation.
part2-overview:
PART 2 · CHAPTER B

Overview

The compact summary view — five metrics, one glance-read. Designed for active trading sessions where you want confirmation the setup is healthy without the 20+ row density of Full Stats.

Overview is the compact, always-on summary view. Five core metrics, presented in a table that takes up a fraction of the chart space Full Stats does. Best used during active trading when you need confirmation that your setup is healthy without the distraction of 20+ rows of detail.

The Five Overview Metrics

RowMeaningHealthy Range
Win RateWins ÷ (Wins + Losses). EOD/Missed excluded.30–70% (setup-dependent)
Net PnLCumulative realised PnL across the lookback.Positive, trending up
Peak Max DDDeepest drawdown from Peak Max Realised.Under 20% of Peak Max Realised
DetectionsTotal setups detected in the lookback.100+ for reliable stats
Profit FactorGross Profit ÷ Gross Loss. The top-level edge metric.1.5+ minimum, 2.0+ strong

When to Use Overview

Deep dive · click any setting
▶During active tradingHOW & WHY

Switch to Overview once your setup is tuned. The five metrics are enough to confirm the system is still working without occupying chart real estate.

Pair with alerts: Let Group 45 alerts notify you of detections/entries while Overview gives you a glance-read of session health. No need to parse Full Stats during live trading.

▶Pre-session checkHOW & WHY

Workflow: Open TradingView. Load your chart. Check Overview. Three-second verdict: Is PF > 1.5? Is Peak Max DD healthy? Has recent performance held? If yes on all three, start trading. If not, switch to Full Stats and diagnose.

The 'still-works' read: If Overview shows the same PF you saw yesterday, the setup is stable. If PF has drifted down meaningfully overnight, something changed — probably the market regime, probably not your configuration.

▶Why Overview is insufficient for optimisationHOW & WHY

Optimisation needs more granularity. Five metrics can confirm healthy vs not-healthy but can't answer why. For that, you need Full Stats — specifically Max Avg SL Unrealised, TP1 Hits, Final TPs Hit, and the Win/Loss Bars ratio.

Switch during tuning passes; switch back when stable. Full Stats → Overview is a natural lifecycle — diagnostic during tuning, minimal during execution.

💡 PRO TIP
Consider using Group 32 to build your own Overview — a hybrid that adds 2–3 of your personal diagnostic favourites (e.g. Max Avg SL Unrealised, 2nd Chance Win Rate) to the default five. You get a compact view tailored to your specific trading style rather than the generic five.
🎯 COMMON PATTERN
Weekly rhythm: Monday morning — Full Stats for full diagnostic. Tuesday–Friday — Overview for live trading. Sunday evening — Model Leaderboard for pruning decisions. Same configuration, different views for different purposes.
part2-dayanalysis:
PART 2 · CHAPTER C

Day Analysis

Day-of-week performance breakdown. Seven rows (Monday–Sunday) showing trade count, Win Rate, and Net PnL per day. Use it to identify which days carry your edge and which drag the aggregate down.

Day Analysis breaks performance down by day of the week. Seven rows — Monday through Sunday — each showing that day's trade count, Win Rate, and Net PnL. The purpose is simple: identify which days the configuration profits on, and which days are dragging the aggregate down.

What the Page Shows

ColumnMeaning
DayMonday–Sunday. Weekends may or may not be populated depending on the asset.
TradesTotal closed trades on that day of the week across the lookback.
Win RateDay-specific Win Rate.
Net PnLDay-specific cumulative realised PnL in $ or ticks.

How to Read It

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Look for consistency, not just positivesHOW & WHY

A day is a real edge if it meets three conditions:

(1) At least 20–30 trades on that day across the lookback (smaller samples are noise). (2) Win Rate clearly above the global Win Rate — 5+ percentage points. (3) Net PnL is positive and meaningful.

A day is a real drag if: 20+ trades on that day with Win Rate clearly below global AND negative Net PnL. Two signals, not one. A day with negative PnL but decent Win Rate might just have had one bad large loss skewing the total.

▶Filtering to profitable daysHOW & WHY

The indicator doesn't filter by day directly. Killzones and Macros filter by time-of-day, not day-of-week. But TradingView's own chart setup lets you limit the chart to specific days via session-specification on the ticker symbol, which effectively filters the indicator too.

Practical use: Day Analysis is primarily diagnostic rather than actionable — it tells you which days not to trade manually, even if the indicator doesn't auto-filter them out.

▶Regime-specific patternsHOW & WHY

Common regime patterns: Tuesday/Wednesday typically carry index-futures edge (mid-week trend continuation). Mondays can be erratic (weekend-gap aftermath). Fridays can show profit-taking choppiness as the weekly close approaches.

Crypto patterns: Weekend trading on crypto is notoriously low-volume and wide-spread. If Saturday/Sunday show consistently poor performance on a crypto chart, enable Killzone filters to avoid those periods even if you've left EOD Auto-Close OFF.

💡 REAL EDGE VS NOISE
Sample size matters more than any other factor when reading Day Analysis. A day with 8 trades showing 100% Win Rate is not a real edge. A day with 80 trades showing 62% Win Rate is. Ignore anything below 20 trades regardless of the Win Rate.
🧪 EXPERIMENT
Run Day Analysis on two different lookbacks — 3,000 bars and 500 bars. If the day-of-week pattern is similar across both, the edge is durable. If it shifts dramatically, the day-of-week pattern is regime-specific and may not continue.
part2-houranalysis:
PART 2 · CHAPTER D

Hour Analysis

Hour-of-day performance breakdown. 24 rows showing trade count, Win Rate, and Net PnL per hour. The most actionable page on the Monitor for session-tuning — it tells you exactly which hours to trade and which to avoid.

Hour Analysis breaks performance down by hour of the day. 24 rows, each showing that hour's trade count, Win Rate, and Net PnL. The most actionable page on the Monitor for session-tuning — it tells you exactly which hours produce edge and which to avoid.

Pairing with Killzones and Macros

Hour Analysis is the diagnostic; Killzones/Macros are the filters. Use the pattern you see here to decide which killzones (Group 29) and macros (Group 30) to enable.

If hours 07–10 show strong edge and hours 12–13 show negative edge, enable NY AM killzone and disable NY Lunch killzone. Hour Analysis confirms the decision is data-driven rather than tradition-driven.

How to Read the 24 Rows

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Finding your edge hoursHOW & WHY

Sort mentally by Net PnL. The top 3–5 hours should account for the majority of your profitability. The bottom 3–5 should account for most of your losses.

A 'hot hour' meets all three: (1) 15+ trades in the lookback (smaller samples are noise). (2) Win Rate at least 5 percentage points above global. (3) Net PnL significantly positive relative to other hours.

A 'cold hour' meets all three: 15+ trades, Win Rate meaningfully below global, Net PnL negative. Either filter it out (killzone exclusion) or understand why it exists.

▶Common market-structure patternsHOW & WHY

US index futures: Strong edge during 07:00–10:00 EST (NY AM) and 13:30–15:45 EST (NY PM). Weak/negative edge during 11:00–13:00 EST (lunch chop). Asian session variable.

Crypto: Varies wildly by asset. BTC shows consistent edge across all hours. Low-cap alts often show edge concentrated in US daytime hours.

FX majors: Strong edge during London open (02:00–05:00 EST) and NY open (07:00–10:00 EST). Quiet overlap periods.

▶Timezone noteHOW & WHY

All times are EST, regardless of your local timezone. The indicator uses EST internally so that killzones/macros work consistently across users.

On your own analysis: Convert hot hours to your local time to decide whether you actually want to trade them. A pattern that says 'edge is at 02:00 EST' requires you to be awake at 02:00 EST on most schedules — that's useful data but might not be practically actionable.

▶Trust the bigger samplesHOW & WHY

Lookback effect: 500 bars on a 5-minute chart = ~42 hours of total market time, which means many hours might only have 2–5 trades. Unreliable.

For Hour Analysis to be actionable, aim for 3,000+ bars. That gives 250+ hours of coverage, producing 10+ trades in most hours and sometimes 30+ in peak sessions. The reading becomes stable.

🎯 WORKFLOW
Pre-tuning Hour Analysis read: 30 trades or more in an hour? Trust the Win Rate. 20–30 trades? Use with caution. Under 20? Needs a longer lookback before drawing conclusions.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
Hour Analysis + Killzone filtering is typically the single highest-leverage optimisation in the manual. Traders routinely see Profit Factor jump from 1.2 to 2.0+ purely by disabling 4–5 unprofitable hours. No other filter approaches that impact.
part2-recenttrades:
PART 2 · CHAPTER E

Recent Trades

Chronological list of the most recent closed trades. The only Monitor page that preserves sequence. Use it for streak awareness, outcome debugging, and tying Monitor stats back to specific chart labels.

Recent Trades is a chronological list of the most recent closed trades. Each row shows the trade's ID, direction, model, entry/exit prices, PnL, and outcome. The purpose is real-time situational awareness — what's happened in the last hour, the last session, the last dozen trades.

What Each Row Shows

ColumnContent
Trade IDUnique identifier (e.g. L-FVG-0417a). Matches the chart label.
DirectionLONG or SHORT.
ModelWhich of the five ICT models detected the setup.
OutcomeWin / Loss / EOD / Missed, with emoji.
RealisedTrade's final realised PnL in $ or ticks.
TP1 $Secured partial value (if partials were enabled).
SessionKillzone/Macro during which the trade was detected.

How to Use It

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Spotting streaks as they formHOW & WHY

Recent Trades is the only Monitor page that shows chronological order. Full Stats aggregates everything into summary statistics; Recent Trades preserves the sequence.

Why that matters: A Win Rate of 55% looks healthy in Full Stats. But if the last 12 trades were 2 wins / 10 losses, you're currently deep in a drawdown — the long-term Win Rate is going to mean-revert but the next-hour experience may be rough.

Use case: Glance at Recent Trades before taking the next live trade. If the last 5 trades were all losses, consider sitting out for an hour to let the streak resolve statistically.

▶Debugging unusual outcomesHOW & WHY

A trade outcome that feels wrong? Find it on Recent Trades, get its ID, and locate the matching label on the chart. You can now see the exact bar-by-bar behaviour and understand why the trade closed where it did.

Common finds: 'Why did that setup become Missed?' → Check the entry price on the label; likely the TP was swept before price retraced. 'Why did TP1 hit but final TP didn't?' → The trail activated prematurely.

The Trade ID is the thread. Everything about a trade — Monitor row, chart label, alert message — is linked by it.

▶Using it with chart filtersHOW & WHY

Pair with Group 2's Quick Switches: Enable Show Losses, Show Wins, Show 2nd Chance. Now Recent Trades' text rows correspond visually to chart labels — you can see where each recent outcome occurred structurally.

Pattern recognition: If the last 5 losses all occurred at the same HTF resistance zone, that's a structural insight that raw Monitor stats would never reveal. Use Recent Trades to notice the pattern; use Full Stats to measure it.

⚠️ COUNTER-INTUITIVE NOTE
Recent Trades shows the tail of a distribution — it's naturally noisier than Full Stats. Don't make configuration changes based on Recent Trades alone. Use it for situational awareness, then verify any suspicions against Full Stats before acting.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
Recent Trades is the page to open during a perceived 'cold streak'. Count the last 10 trade outcomes. If the ratio matches Full Stats' Win Rate, you're in normal variance. If it's significantly worse, something's changed — check Day Analysis and Hour Analysis for possible regime shifts.
part2-modelleaderboard:
PART 2 · CHAPTER F

Model Leaderboard

Ranks all ten model/direction combinations by Profit Factor. The pruning tool — use it to decide which models are carrying your edge and which to disable. Pruning decisions made here compound, since removing a losing model stops its losses from dragging down the aggregate.

Model Leaderboard ranks all ten model/direction combinations — the five ICT models (Displacement, FVG, i-FVG, OB, Breaker) × two directions (Long, Short) — by Profit Factor. It's the page you consult when deciding which models to keep, prune, or weight differently. Pruning decisions made here compound: disable a losing model and its poor performance no longer drags the aggregate stats down.

What Each Row Shows

ColumnContent
Rank1–10, highest Profit Factor first.
ModelOne of the five ICT models.
DirectionLong or Short.
TradesTotal detected + closed trades for this model/direction.
Win RateModel-specific Win Rate.
Net PnLModel-specific cumulative realised PnL.
Profit FactorModel-specific PF — the ranking key.

How to Use the Leaderboard

Deep dive · click any setting
▶The pruning decisionHOW & WHY

Not every model works on every asset. A Displacement model might carry 60% of your edge on NQ while the OB model loses money. Disabling OB-Long and OB-Short (Groups 6 and 17) removes them from detection — the losing trades stop happening and the aggregate PF climbs.

The pruning rule: Disable any model/direction combination whose Profit Factor is below 1.0 on a sample of 30+ trades. A losing model with a small sample is noise; a losing model with a meaningful sample is a real drag.

But: Don't prune a losing model whose losses were concentrated in a clearly-identifiable regime (e.g. 'it lost money during a week-long sideways chop but won during the prior trend'). That's regime-specific, not model-specific. Use Custom Date Range (Group 44) to verify.

▶Reading the balanceHOW & WHY

A single model carrying 80% of Net PnL = concentration risk. If that model's edge collapses (regime shift, market evolution), your entire system collapses with it. Look for configurations where 3–4 models contribute meaningfully — diversification.

But a single model with 80% of PnL might be the right answer if the rest are slightly losing. If Displacement-Long contributes +$5,000 while OB-Short contributes −$200 and i-FVG-Long contributes +$200, the smart move is: keep Displacement-Long, disable OB-Short, consider whether i-FVG-Long is meaningful.

Ratio to watch: Second-best model's PF / Best model's PF. If it's above 0.6, you have diversified edge. Below 0.3, you're over-concentrated.

▶When to pruneHOW & WHY

Don't prune too early. A model with 15 trades and PF 0.8 might just be having a bad run. Wait for 30+ trades before deciding.

Prune in layers, not all at once. Remove one model/direction. Rerun. Check Full Stats PF. Did it improve by more than the share the model contributed? If yes, the pruning is net-positive. If no, reinstate the model and prune a different one.

Prune on multiple lookbacks. If OB-Short has PF 0.7 on 5,000 bars AND 0.8 on 1,000 bars AND 0.6 on 300 bars, it's consistently losing — prune with confidence. If it's 0.7 / 1.2 / 1.5, the recent performance is strong even if historical is weak — don't prune.

▶Disabling a model vs zeroing its sizeHOW & WHY

The Leaderboard ranks what exists. Disabling a model via Group 6/17 removes it from detection entirely — it stops appearing on the Leaderboard.

Alternative: Leave the model enabled but set Partial Count to zero for it, or set a Min R:R filter that its typical setups can't meet. This keeps the model trackable in Leaderboard but effectively muted.

Use the alternative when: You want to monitor whether the model's performance changes without acting on it. If a disabled model starts producing profitable setups again in the future, you won't know — because it's not running at all.

🎯 WEEKLY RHYTHM
Check Model Leaderboard every Sunday evening. Note changes in rank. If a model climbs or drops three ranks in a week, dig deeper with Full Stats and Day/Hour Analysis. Persistent rank instability suggests overfitting somewhere in the configuration.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
Profit comes from your best 2–3 models, not from your worst 5. Most successful long-term configurations end up pruned to 2–4 active model/direction combinations. All ten active is either (a) a beginner configuration not yet optimised, or (b) a balanced edge across the full ICT stack — which is rare but powerful when real.
⚠️ WATCH OUT
The Leaderboard's Profit Factor reading depends on sample size. A model with 12 trades at PF 3.0 is not genuinely 3× better than a model with 300 trades at PF 1.5. Always check the Trades column before acting on rank.
part3-intro:
PART 3

Workflows

Systematic procedures for tuning, diagnosing, and maintaining configurations. Each workflow is a proven sequence that isolates one variable at a time, reads the Monitor's response, and progresses on evidence. Part 1 gives you the settings; Part 2 gives you the reads; Part 3 gives you the sequences.

Part 1 documents what each setting does. Part 2 documents how to read the Monitor's response. Part 3 ties both together with what to do, in what order: complete workflows from a blank configuration to a profitable one, and from a broken configuration to a diagnosed one.

The Five Workflows

ChapterWorkflowWhen You'd Run It
AThe Baseline WorkflowFirst session with the indicator on a new asset.
BDirection TuningWhen Long and Short performance diverge and mirroring isn't working.
CFilter Layering OrderWhen you want to add quality filters without over-restricting.
DModel Pruning via LeaderboardWeekly / monthly review of which models are carrying edge.
ETroubleshooting PF Below 1When Profit Factor has collapsed and you don't know why.

Why Workflows Matter

The indicator has effectively infinite configurations. Random exploration rarely finds the profitable ones; systematic exploration does. Each workflow is a proven sequence that isolates one variable at a time, reads the Monitor's response, and progresses based on evidence.

Workflows are opinions, not rules. Every experienced trader develops personal variations. Treat these as starting templates — once you understand why each step happens, you can adjust the order or substitute steps based on your style.

Universal Workflow Principles

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Change one variable at a timeHOW & WHY

The most important rule. If you change two settings simultaneously and the Monitor improves, you don't know which change caused the improvement.

How to enforce: Write down your current configuration. Change one setting. Record the Monitor's new state. Decide whether to keep the change. If keep, update your written config and change the next setting.

▶Respect sample sizeHOW & WHY

Every interpretation assumes sufficient data. Under 100 trades total, Profit Factor readings are volatile. A single outlier can swing PF by 0.5+.

Rule of thumb: 100+ trades for broad reads. 30+ trades for model/direction reads. 15+ trades for hour-of-day reads. Below these, extend the lookback first.

▶Bracket, don't creepHOW & WHY

When tuning a continuous setting (Entry Level, Bars To Scan, Min R:R), test extremes before middle values. Bars To Scan: try 5,000 and 500 before trying 1,500. The endpoints tell you the direction of the effect; middle values fine-tune.

Why it's faster: Creeping values (500 → 600 → 700 → 800) may take 20 tests to find a peak. Bracketing (5,000 → 1,500 → 500 → 750) finds the same peak in 4.

▶Separate tuning from tradingHOW & WHY

Don't tune during a live trade. The urge to fiddle while a position is open will cost you. Lock the configuration at the start of the session; review and tune between sessions.

Why: Reading live Monitor during an active trade is a different cognitive task than optimisation. Conflating them produces erratic decisions driven by the current trade's emotional state.

💡 BEFORE YOU START
Every workflow assumes you've already configured Group 4 (Account & Risk) correctly for your asset. If your Monitor PnL doesn't roughly match what you'd expect in dollars, stop and fix Group 4 first. See Group 4's chapter for the asset-class workflow reference.
part3-a-baseline:
PART 3 · CHAPTER A

The Baseline Workflow

From a blank configuration on a new asset to a working baseline with measurable edge. The first workflow you run when opening a new chart. Seven steps, one variable at a time, Monitor-driven decisions at every stage.

The Baseline Workflow takes you from a freshly-loaded indicator on a new asset to a working configuration with measurable edge. It's the first workflow you run when opening a new chart, and the reference you return to when starting over from scratch.

Before You Start

✅ PREREQUISITES
Group 4 is configured correctly for the asset. Group 4's calculator output matches your expected per-trade dollar risk. Auto-Detect matches your asset class (ON for CME futures, OFF for crypto/equities/FX). The chart has loaded at least 5,000 bars of history.

The Seven-Step Workflow

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Step 1 — Set the Lookback baselineHOW & WHY

Action: Group 3 → Sim Mode = Lookback Period → Bars To Scan = 5,000.

Why: The maximum lookback gives you the broadest baseline. If your edge survives at 5,000 bars, it survives across multiple regimes. We'll drop it later to check current-regime adaptation.

Expect: 200–1,000 detections depending on chart timeframe and enabled models. Monitor stats may take a second to populate.

▶Step 2 — Enable Mirroring, all models, default entryHOW & WHY

Actions:

• Group 5 → Setup Mirroring = Shorts Mirror Longs.

• Group 6 → All five models ON. Entry Level = 0.34 (the indicator's default; front-runs the 0.5 mid-point).

• Group 11 → SL Method = Setup Auto Calc.

• Group 7 → Target Method = Auto Calc.

• Everything else at default.

Why: Mirroring halves your configuration work. Default ICT settings are a well-studied starting point. Auto Calc on both target and SL lets the simulator's structural logic do the work — you just observe the result.

Read the Monitor: Switch to Full Stats. Note Win Rate, Profit Factor, Detections, Peak Max DD. Write these down. This is your baseline.

▶Step 3 — Establish baseline profitabilityHOW & WHY

Question: Is Profit Factor above 1.0 on the baseline?

If yes (PF ≥ 1.0): You have viable baseline edge. Proceed to Step 4.

If no (PF < 1.0): The default configuration is unprofitable on this asset. Three likely causes and their fixes:

• Wrong asset class: Re-verify Group 4. Most PF < 0.5 readings are misconfigured Margin/Leverage.

• Wrong chart timeframe: The ICT defaults are calibrated for 5-minute to 1-hour charts. On 1-minute or Daily, the assumptions break down. Try switching to 5-minute.

• Wrong market for ICT: Some low-liquidity assets (exotic FX crosses, tiny-cap equities) don't respect ICT structure. Move to a different asset.

▶Step 4 — Refine the Entry LevelHOW & WHY

Action: Hold everything else constant. Sweep Entry Level: 0.0 → 0.25 → 0.34 → 0.5 → 0.618 → 0.786. Record Profit Factor and Detections at each.

Why: Entry Level is the highest-leverage single setting. It affects fill rate, R:R, Missed PnL, and Win Rate simultaneously. Finding the asset's retracement character is step 1 of meaningful tuning.

Result: Typically one level clearly peaks Profit Factor. Lock in that Entry Level.

If multiple levels produce similar PF: Pick the one with the highest Detection count — you're getting the same edge from more sample, which is more reliable.

▶Step 5 — Refine the Target MethodHOW & WHY

Action: With Entry Level locked, cycle Target Method: Auto Calc → Fixed Devs 2.0 → Swing H/L → PD Array Matrix (Depth 1) → PD Array Matrix (Depth 3).

Read the Monitor at each: Profit Factor, Final TPs Hit, Avg R:R.

Decision rules:

• If Final TPs Hit < 30%: Target too far. Pick a tighter method (Fixed Devs 1.5, or PD Array Depth 1).

• If Final TPs Hit > 70% with Avg R:R < 1.5: Target too close. Pick a farther method (PD Array Depth 3, HTF targets).

• If Profit Factor consistently peaks on one method: Lock it in.

▶Step 6 — Refine the SL MethodHOW & WHY

Action: With Entry and Target locked, cycle SL Method: Setup Auto Calc → Adjustable R (0.33) → PD Array Matrix → Advanced Trail (Trigger 0.5, Trail 0.3).

Read the Monitor at each: Max Avg SL Unrealised, Peak Max DD, Win Rate.

Decision rules:

• If Max Avg SL Unrealised > 50% of Avg TP distance: Trades are going into profit before being stopped. Enable Advanced Trail or TP1 Secure.

• If Peak Max DD > 25% of Peak Max Realised: Stops are too loose. Use a structural SL (PD Array Matrix) or add SL Buffer OFF if it's currently ON.

• If Win Rate drops sharply on a method: That method's SL is being tagged by noise. Revert.

▶Step 7 — Verify on a shorter lookbackHOW & WHY

Action: Drop Bars To Scan to 1,000. Record Profit Factor. Drop to 500. Record. Drop to 300. Record.

Why: A configuration that works on 5,000 bars but collapses on 500 bars is historically profitable but currently not working. You want both.

Acceptable pattern: PF stable or slightly higher at shorter lookbacks = current regime is favourable. Use this configuration.

Warning pattern: PF collapses at 500 bars = the historical edge has evaporated in the current regime. Don't trade this configuration live. Either wait for the regime to shift back, or rerun the workflow on different settings to find a regime-matched edge.

Expected Time Investment

First full workflow: 45–90 minutes. Each Monitor refresh after a setting change takes a few seconds. The 7 steps typically require 15–25 setting changes total.

Retuning on a familiar asset: 10–20 minutes. You'll recognise which steps are already optimised and which need attention.

💡 THE BASELINE WORKFLOW IS REPEATABLE
Rerun this workflow weekly on live-traded assets. A configuration that was optimal six weeks ago may not be optimal today. The workflow is fast once familiar — treat it as maintenance.
🎯 AFTER THE BASELINE
Once you have a working baseline, move to Part 3 Chapter B (Direction Tuning) if Long and Short are diverging, or Chapter C (Filter Layering) if the baseline is profitable but you want to raise quality at the cost of detections.
part3-b-directiontuning:
PART 3 · CHAPTER B

Direction Tuning

When Long and Short performance have diverged, mirroring becomes a liability. This workflow breaks the mirror, tunes each direction independently, and decides whether to keep a weaker direction or disable it entirely.

Direction Tuning is the workflow you run after the Baseline Workflow (Chapter A) reveals that Long and Short performance have diverged. Most assets are not symmetric — a mirrored configuration is a reasonable starting point, not the final answer. This chapter walks you through breaking the mirror and optimising each direction independently.

When to Run This Workflow

📊 DIVERGENCE SIGNALS
Run Direction Tuning when any of these are true:
• Long Profit Factor is more than 0.5 away from Short Profit Factor.
• Long Net PnL and Short Net PnL have opposite signs (one profitable, one losing).
• Model Leaderboard (Part 2 Chapter F) shows the top 5 slots are all one direction.
If none of these apply, your asset is symmetric — stay mirrored, skip this workflow.

The Six-Step Direction Tuning Workflow

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Step 1 — Break the mirrorHOW & WHY

Action: Group 5 → Setup Mirroring = None (Independent).

What happens immediately: Nothing visible. Groups 17–27 were copying from Groups 6–16; now they use their own values, which were previously ignored but still configured. The result on the Monitor should be identical.

If the Monitor changes after breaking mirror: Your Short-side settings (Groups 17–27) are different from Long-side (Groups 6–16) because you set them manually at some point. Either reset them to match, or investigate why they differ.

▶Step 2 — Identify the weaker directionHOW & WHY

Look at Full Stats: Which column has the lower Profit Factor? That's the weaker direction. All subsequent steps tune the weaker direction while keeping the stronger unchanged.

Common pattern: On trending futures, Long is usually the stronger direction. On crypto, it varies by asset. On FX, depends heavily on which pair.

▶Step 3 — Check the weaker direction's Entry LevelHOW & WHY

Action: On the weaker direction only, sweep Entry Level like you did in Chapter A Step 4. Use values 0.25, 0.34, 0.5, 0.618, 0.786.

Why separately: The optimal Entry Level can differ by direction. On trending instruments, the long-side entry is typically deeper (0.5–0.618) while the short-side is shallower (0.25–0.34) because short setups need to capture brief pullbacks in the dominant uptrend.

Lock in: The Entry Level that peaks the weaker direction's PF.

▶Step 4 — Check the weaker direction's Target MethodHOW & WHY

Action: Same as Chapter A Step 5, but only for the weaker direction (Group 18 Targets if shorts are weaker; Group 7 if longs are).

Why it might differ: Short targets on an uptrending asset are constrained by structural support zones that are closer than Long targets' resistance zones. Auto Calc may underperform on Shorts; Swing H/L or PD Array Matrix Depth 1 may win.

▶Step 5 — Check the weaker direction's SL MethodHOW & WHY

Action: Same as Chapter A Step 6 but for the weaker direction only.

Common finding: The weaker direction often benefits from B.E. Trail or Advanced Trail where the stronger direction uses static SLs. This is because the weaker direction is fighting the dominant trend — trailing aggressively locks in early profit before the trend reasserts.

▶Step 6 — Re-evaluate whether to disable the weaker directionHOW & WHY

After Steps 3–5: What's the weaker direction's PF now?

• PF > 1.3: Keep both directions enabled. You've found the asymmetric edge.

• PF between 1.0 and 1.3: Marginal. Keep it if your dollar risk per trade is small; disable if you're running size. A small-edge direction contributes noise at scale.

• PF < 1.0 even after tuning: The weaker direction doesn't have edge on this asset. Group 5 → Trade Direction = Long Only (or Short Only, if longs were the problem). You've just removed a drag on the system.

Read-Back Verification

After Direction Tuning, run the Part 2 Chapter A Full Stats read again and compare:

Before TuningAfter TuningInterpretation
Total PF 1.2, Long 1.6, Short 0.8Total PF 1.8, Long 1.6, Short 1.9Fixed the short direction. System now balanced.
Total PF 1.2, Long 1.6, Short 0.8Total PF 1.8, Short disabledCouldn't fix shorts. Removing them was correct.
Total PF 1.2, Long 1.6, Short 0.8Total PF 1.25, no real changeTuning didn't help. Accept the asymmetry, consider disabling the weak side anyway.
⚠️ DON'T RE-ENABLE MIRRORING
Once you've broken the mirror and tuned each direction independently, don't re-enable mirroring later. You'll silently overwrite one direction's tuning with the other's. If you need to reset, reset both sides manually.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
Asymmetric assets reward asymmetric configuration. The mirror is a starting heuristic; once you have 200+ trades of evidence showing divergent Long/Short performance, independent tuning almost always outperforms mirrored tuning.
part3-c-filterlayering:
PART 3 · CHAPTER C

Filter Layering Order

Adding quality filters without over-restricting. Layered correctly, each filter has a specific job — preserving expected value, restricting to institutional-flow windows, aligning with trend, requiring structural confirmation, demanding quality. This workflow walks you through the right sequence.

After a baseline is profitable, the next natural question is 'can I make it better?' Filters are the answer — but layering them correctly is a skill. Added in the wrong order, they over-restrict, leave you with no trades, and produce misleading Profit Factor readings from tiny samples. This chapter walks through the correct layering sequence.

The Filter Stack

Every filter in the indicator reduces detection count. The goal is to remove bad detections while keeping good detections. Layered correctly, each filter has a specific job:

LayerFilterJob
1Min R:R Filter (Group 15/26)Preserve expected value. Never take a trade with unfavourable risk-reward.
2Time filters (Killzones, Macros)Trade only when institutional flow is active.
3Trend filter (SMA + direction)Trade only with the prevailing trend.
4Structural filter (MSS/BOS)Trade only after structural confirmation.
5Quality filter (Divergence, HTF Scout)Trade only high-probability setups.
6Dollar/Tick floor (Group 28)Trade only economically-meaningful setups.

The Layering Workflow

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Prerequisite — profitable baselineHOW & WHY

Do NOT run this workflow on an unprofitable baseline. Filters can't rescue a configuration with no edge; they can only refine one that already works.

Verify first: Part 3 Chapter A is complete. Baseline PF is > 1.3. Detection count is > 200.

▶Step 1 — Add Min R:R Filter firstHOW & WHY

Action: Group 15 → Min R:R Filter = 1.0. (If short-side tuned independently, Group 26 → same.)

Why first: It's the safest filter. It guarantees every trade has favourable expected value. Enabling it should never worsen Profit Factor — it only removes the trades that would have degraded it.

Read the Monitor: Detections should drop 10–20%. PF should rise slightly. If PF drops, the baseline was relying on sub-R:R trades for its edge — unstable.

Tighten: If Detections and PF both improve further, try 1.5. Keep tightening until the Detection count drops more than Profit Factor rises (diminishing returns).

▶Step 2 — Add time filtersHOW & WHY

Action: Consult Hour Analysis (Part 2 Chapter D). Identify the 3–4 hours with clearly positive edge on your asset. Enable the killzone(s) that contain those hours (Group 29).

Example: Hour Analysis shows edge during 07:00–10:00 EST. Enable NY AM killzone. Leave all other killzones off.

Read the Monitor: Detection count drops sharply (often 50%+). Profit Factor should rise — sometimes dramatically. If PF drops, your hour selection was wrong; return to Hour Analysis and recheck.

Macro layering: If killzone filtering improved PF, try also enabling macros within that killzone. Macros produce another tier of refinement.

▶Step 3 — Consider a trend filterHOW & WHY

Action: Group 15 → SMA Filter ON, SMA Length 200.

Why optional: On clearly trending assets (US index futures), a trend filter adds 0.2–0.5 PF. On range-bound assets (spot crypto in consolidation, FX crosses), it can destroy edge.

Read the Monitor: If PF improves meaningfully and Detections stay above 100, keep it. If PF drops or detections collapse to under 50, remove it.

▶Step 4 — Structural filter (conditional)HOW & WHY

Action: Group 15 → MSS/BOS Filter ON.

Why conditional: Requires structural breaks in the trade's direction before accepting a setup. Works best on 5m–1h charts with clean structure. Less useful on low timeframes (1m) where MSS/BOS signals are noisy.

Typical impact: Cuts detections another 30%. Should lift PF if the structural logic matches your asset's behaviour.

▶Step 5 — Quality filter (conditional)HOW & WHY

Action: Group 15 → Bullish Divergence ON (or Group 5 → Filter Trades By HTF ON).

Why last: Quality filters are the most restrictive. Adding them too early when baseline PF is marginal can leave you with 30 trades and misleading stats.

Verify samples: After enabling, Detection count must still be at least 100. If it drops below, the filter is too strict for this asset or lookback. Tighten only if you can maintain sample.

▶Step 6 — Verify the stack at multiple lookbacksHOW & WHY

Action: With all layered filters on, test Bars To Scan at 5,000, 1,500, 500.

Healthy pattern: PF stable or improving at shorter lookbacks. Regime-adapted edge.

Unhealthy pattern: PF excellent at 5,000 bars but collapses at 500 bars. You've overfitted — removing filters may actually help current performance.

When to Remove Filters

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Filter removal signalsHOW & WHY

Remove a filter when:

• Detection count falls below 50 on your normal lookback.

• PF was higher without the filter (check by disabling temporarily).

• PF is excellent but the filter's rule was arbitrary ('I added it because someone suggested it' rather than 'I added it because the Monitor told me it helped').

Remove in reverse order of addition. The last filter added is the most likely to be marginal.

⚠️ OVERFITTING WARNING
Five filters that each improved PF by 0.1 might combine to produce a PF jump of 0.8. But the combined stack may be overfit — removing any one could drop PF back by 0.4. Always test the combined stack at multiple lookbacks before committing it to live trading.
💡 KEY INSIGHT
The minimum number of filters that achieve target PF is better than the maximum number. A 2-filter configuration with PF 1.6 on 400 trades is more robust than a 5-filter configuration with PF 2.0 on 80 trades. Prefer sample size and simplicity.
part3-d-modelpruning:
PART 3 · CHAPTER D

Model Pruning via Leaderboard

Most configurations don't benefit from all five ICT models. Model Pruning identifies underperformers and disables them, compounding aggregate Profit Factor improvements. Weekly rhythm, not a one-time fix.

Most configurations don't benefit from all five ICT models equally. On any given asset, one or two models carry the bulk of the edge while the others contribute noise. Model Pruning is the workflow that identifies and disables underperforming models, compounding the aggregate Profit Factor improvement.

When to Run This Workflow

📅 REGULAR CADENCE
Weekly: Check Model Leaderboard on Sunday evening. Prune if clearly warranted.
After a regime shift: News events, earnings seasons, volatility regime changes can flip which models perform. Re-run pruning whenever you suspect the environment has changed.
When adding a new asset: The first weekly check should include a full pruning pass.

The Pruning Workflow

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Step 1 — Set adequate sample sizeHOW & WHY

Action: Group 3 → Bars To Scan = 5,000 (or the largest lookback with meaningful data).

Why: Pruning decisions need at least 30 trades per model/direction to be statistically meaningful. A typical well-tuned setup with five models might produce 150–300 trades per 5,000 bars, split across 10 slots — so expect 15–30 per slot.

If sample is still too small: The asset may not produce enough setups for model-level pruning to be meaningful. Skip this workflow for now; rerun when you have more data.

▶Step 2 — Read the LeaderboardHOW & WHY

Action: Group 1 → Select Monitor = Model Leaderboard.

Note the following:

• The top 3 ranks — these are your edge carriers.

• The bottom 3 ranks — these are your drag candidates.

• The middle 4 ranks — neutral, decide case-by-case.

Flag candidates for disabling: Any model/direction with PF < 1.0 AND at least 30 trades. PF < 0.8 with 30+ trades is a near-certain disable.

▶Step 3 — Disable one flagged model at a timeHOW & WHY

Action: Turn off the flagged model in Group 6 (for longs) or Group 17 (for shorts).

Important: Disable ONE at a time, not all at once. The aggregate Monitor needs to recompute and you need to verify each disable is net-positive.

Read: After disabling, check Full Stats total PF. Did it go up by more than the contribution the model made? If yes, the disable is confirmed net-positive. If no, re-enable — the model was a smaller drag than you thought.

▶Step 4 — Check multi-lookback consistencyHOW & WHY

Before disabling permanently: Verify the model's underperformance across multiple lookbacks.

Action: With the model still enabled, cycle Bars To Scan through 5,000 / 2,000 / 500. Record the model's Leaderboard PF at each.

Consistent underperformer: PF < 1.0 at all three lookbacks = safe to disable. The weakness is durable.

Regime-sensitive: PF < 0.8 at 5,000 but > 1.5 at 500 bars = the model WAS weak historically but is currently strong. DON'T disable — you'd be cutting a current edge based on stale data.

▶Step 5 — Iterate and re-rankHOW & WHY

After pruning one model, rerun. Re-read the Leaderboard. The ranks have shifted. Models that were 'middle of the pack' may now be clear losers or clear winners.

Rule of thumb: Each pruning pass typically removes 1–2 models. A third prune is rare. More than three consecutive prunes suggests the baseline was overfit or the asset is unstable.

▶Step 6 — Watch for lone-model concentrationHOW & WHY

Concentration risk: After pruning, check whether one model accounts for more than 60% of Net Realised.

If yes: You've built a concentrated configuration. Fine if that model's edge is stable, risky if it's regime-sensitive. Consider keeping a secondary model active even at marginal PF to provide diversification.

If no: Healthy diversification across 2–3 models. The pruning was clean.

Re-Enabling Pruned Models

Deep dive · click any setting
▶When to bring a pruned model backHOW & WHY

Markets evolve. A model that underperformed six months ago may outperform today. Build a habit of checking disabled models periodically.

How: Temporarily re-enable a pruned model on a 500-bar lookback. If its Leaderboard PF is clearly above 1.0 on recent data, reintegrate it. If not, keep it disabled.

Cadence: Monthly check of disabled models. Takes 2 minutes. Occasionally catches a regime shift early.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Pruning compounds. Removing a model with PF 0.8 doesn't just remove 10% of losing trades — it removes those losers from Win Rate, from PF, from DD calculations, from every aggregate stat. A single correct prune often lifts total PF by 0.3–0.5.
🧪 EXPERIMENT
Test the aggregate effect: run full Baseline Workflow with all 5 models active, record PF. Then prune the two worst models. Record PF again. The delta is your model-level edge — for most assets, it's 30–50% of the Profit Factor gain above baseline.
part3-e-troubleshooting:
PART 3 · CHAPTER E

Troubleshooting PF Below 1

The rescue workflow. You had a profitable configuration; something broke. This chapter is the systematic diagnostic — seven checks in order, each isolating a common cause. Work through them before changing anything.

This workflow is the rescue procedure. You return to a configuration you previously had running profitably, look at the Monitor, and Profit Factor is under 1. Something has changed. This chapter is the systematic diagnostic to find what and fix it.

First, Stop

⚠️ DON'T CHANGE ANYTHING YET
Resist the urge to start fiddling. Most PF-collapse events are caused by a small number of specific factors. Random setting changes almost always make it worse. Work through this diagnostic before touching anything.

The Diagnostic Tree

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Check 1 — Is the sample still meaningful?HOW & WHY

Look at Detection count. If it's under 50, the low PF may be statistical noise, not a real edge loss.

Fix: Extend Bars To Scan until Detection count is over 200. Does PF recover above 1? If yes, the 'collapse' was a small-sample artefact and the underlying edge is intact.

If still low PF with 200+ detections: It's a real issue. Continue to Check 2.

▶Check 2 — Did the lookback window just change?HOW & WHY

Context: If you're using Lookback Period mode (Group 3), the window moves forward as new bars print. A recent high-impact event (news, session close, or a single bad trade) can temporarily depress PF until the window moves past it.

Action: Switch to Custom Date Range (Group 44) and set a window that excludes the last 50 bars. If PF recovers, the recent bar-count was the cause. The edge is intact; the current regime is producing a temporary drawdown.

If still low PF with Custom Date Range excluding recent bars: The degradation is older. Continue to Check 3.

▶Check 3 — Did you accidentally change a setting?HOW & WHY

Common setting-drift causes: Clicking OK on a TradingView chart preset that reset the indicator's defaults. Duplicating a chart and losing custom settings. Updating the indicator version and having defaults re-applied.

Action: Do a full walk-through of your 5 most-important settings:

• Group 3 → Bars To Scan — still at your expected value?

• Group 5 → Setup Mirroring — still set correctly?

• Group 6/17 → Entry Level — still your optimised value?

• Group 11/22 → SL Method — still the one you tuned?

• Group 13/24 → TP1 Secure — still ON if your edge relied on it?

If anything's different from your written config: Revert and re-check PF.

▶Check 4 — Did the asset's regime shift?HOW & WHY

Run multi-lookback comparison: Record PF at Bars To Scan = 5,000 / 1,500 / 500 / 200.

Pattern: 5k = 1.8, 1.5k = 1.5, 500 = 1.1, 200 = 0.7. Regime shift. Your configuration worked historically; it doesn't work in the current regime. Two options:

(1) Wait for the regime to shift back (hours to weeks). Monitor the shorter-lookback PF daily. When it recovers, resume trading.

(2) Re-run Baseline Workflow (Chapter A) on the 500-bar window to find a regime-matched configuration. This is what pro traders do — adapt rather than wait.

▶Check 5 — Is one model poisoning the stats?HOW & WHY

Switch to Model Leaderboard. Is any single model showing a very low PF (< 0.5) on a meaningful sample?

Action: Disable that model temporarily. Does aggregate PF recover? If yes, that model is currently broken and Chapter D (Pruning) is your next workflow.

▶Check 6 — Is Max Avg SL Unrealised high?HOW & WHY

If yes: Trades are going positive before being stopped. The fix is B.E. Trail, Advanced Trail, or adding SL Buffer (Group 14).

This is the most common single fix. If Max Avg SL Unrealised has climbed since your last known-good configuration, that's almost certainly the cause.

▶Check 7 — Stats look right but LIVE PnL doesn't match?HOW & WHY

If PF is fine on the Monitor but your broker account isn't growing: The issue isn't configuration — it's execution.

Check: (1) Slippage — are your real fills systematically worse than the simulator assumes? (2) Fees — not modelled by the simulator at all. (3) Skipped trades — are you only taking some of the detected setups? The simulator assumes 100% participation.

Fix: Model slippage and fees mentally. If the simulator says PF 1.8 and each trade pays $2 in fees on an average $15 profit, your real PF is ~1.6 — still profitable but significantly reduced.

When to Give Up and Rebuild

Some PF collapses are irrecoverable with the existing configuration. If:

• PF is under 0.8 across every lookback from 300 to 5,000 bars.

• Pruning any single model doesn't recover it.

• Regime-specific reconfiguration (Check 4) doesn't recover it.

…the asset has evolved past your configuration. Archive the old settings, run Chapter A (Baseline Workflow) from scratch on the current data. It's faster than endless tweaking.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Most PF collapses are caused by one of three things: small sample artefact, regime shift, or a single model going bad. Checks 1, 4, and 5 catch 80% of cases. The other four checks handle the long tail.
📝 KEEP A CONFIG JOURNAL
Write down your configuration whenever PF is healthy. Every setting you'd struggle to remember exactly. When PF collapses, the journal lets you diff current settings against the last known-good state instantly. Five minutes of note-taking saves hours of diagnosis.
part4-intro:

Real-world questions, common confusions, error messages, and frequently-asked questions. Organised by category. The search box is your friend here — every question-relevant phrase is indexed.

Part 4 exists for the questions that don't fit neatly into a single settings group or Monitor chapter. Real-world issues, common confusions, error messages, and frequently-asked questions — organised by category so you can find an answer fast.

The Six Part 4 Chapters

ChapterContains
