part4-a-flowcharts:
PART 4 · CHAPTER A

Diagnostic Flowcharts

Visual decision trees for the most common scenarios. Each flowchart starts with a symptom and ends with a specific fix. Follow the branches one at a time.

Visual decision trees for the most common scenarios. Each flowchart starts with a symptom and ends with a specific fix. Useful when you have a feeling something's wrong but aren't sure where to start.

Flowchart 1 — 'My Monitor shows numbers I don't expect'

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Follow the branchesHOW & WHY

Start: Are the PnL numbers roughly the right order of magnitude for my actual account size?

• No, way too big/small: Group 4 misconfiguration. Check Auto-Detect, Margin, Leverage, Contracts. See Group 4 chapter's asset-class workflow reference.

• Yes, PnL looks right: Next check. Does Profit Factor look right for what you've seen on chart?

  • No, PF dramatically different: Lookback may be picking up a regime you didn't intend. Check Bars To Scan.

  • Yes, PF looks reasonable: Next check. Do Long/Short columns make sense given the chart's trend?

    • Asymmetric when you expected symmetric: Setup Mirroring may be off. Check Group 5.

    • Symmetric when you expected asymmetric: Mirroring may be ON when you want it OFF. Check Group 5.

Flowchart 2 — 'Nothing is being detected'

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Follow the branchesHOW & WHY

Start: Detection count is 0 or very low.

• Are any models enabled? Check Group 6 and 17 — at least one model per direction must be ON.

• Is Trade Direction restricted? Group 5 → Trade Direction. If Long Only, you won't see short detections even with short models enabled.

• Are Killzones/Macros filtering out everything? Temporarily disable Group 29 and 30 filters. If detections appear, one or more enabled windows didn't contain detectable setups.

• Is Filter Trades By HTF restricting? Group 5 → Filter Trades By HTF. Try OFF temporarily.

• Is Custom Date Range empty? Group 3 + 44. If Date Range dates are outside chart history, no bars are scanned.

Flowchart 3 — 'Chart is sluggish or shows object-limit errors'

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Follow the branchesHOW & WHY

Start: TradingView feels slow or 'Undefined object limit exceeded' appears.

• How many bars are enabled? Group 3 → Bars To Scan. Over 3,000 bars dramatically increases load.

• What's enabled in Group 2? Show All Closed + Show Wins + Show Losses together produces the densest chart visuals. Disable all three; enable only Show Detected + Show Active.

• Visibility limits too high? Group 43 → Max Closed, Max Missed. Halve them. Usually resolves object-limit errors.

• Performance Mode OFF? Group 45 → Performance Mode ON reduces visual update frequency.

• Freeze Closed Trades OFF? Group 45 → Freeze Closed Trades ON prevents closed-trade redraws.

Flowchart 4 — 'My alert didn't fire'

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Follow the branchesHOW & WHY

Start: Expected alert didn't arrive.

• Is the correct alert type enabled? Group 45. New Detection, Entry Hit, TP1 Hit are all separate toggles. Verify the one you wanted is ON.

• Is the TradingView alert set to fire "Once per bar close" or "Once per bar"? For most trade events, "Once per bar" is what you want — "Once per bar close" delays by up to one full bar.

• Is the alert configured in TradingView at all? Enabling toggles in Group 45 activates the simulator's internal alert logic. You still need to create a TradingView alert with the 'any alert() function call' condition pointing at the IOTSIM indicator.

• Did the alert expire? TradingView alerts have expiration dates. Free-tier alerts last ~2 months; renew periodically.

Flowchart 5 — 'Profit Factor varies wildly when I change lookback'

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Follow the branchesHOW & WHY

Start: PF at 5,000 bars is 1.8 but 500 bars shows 0.9 (or vice versa).

• Are both samples large enough? Each lookback needs 100+ detections. Below that, differences are statistical noise, not regime shift.

• Is the 500-bar window during a known event? FOMC week, earnings week, holiday session. Use Custom Date Range (Group 44) to exclude or focus.

• Does the difference persist on rerun? Some platforms have occasional calculation glitches. Close/reopen the indicator, retest.

• If difference is real and persistent: Regime shift. See Part 3 Chapter E — rebuild for current regime.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Most flowchart failures come from trying three fixes at once. Follow the branches one at a time. Test after each change. If the symptom persists, revert and try the next branch. Isolation is faster than parallel experimentation.
part4-b-errors:
PART 4 · CHAPTER B

Error Messages & Warnings

Every error, warning, and unusual chart emoji the indicator can produce, with plain-English explanation and fix. Use search to find a specific message instantly.

Every error, warning, or unusual chart emoji the indicator produces, with plain-English explanation and fix. Use the search box to find a specific message or emoji instantly.

TradingView-Level Errors

Deep dive · click any setting
▶"Undefined object limit exceeded"HOW & WHY

What it means: TradingView's hard ceiling on drawn objects (lines, boxes, labels) has been reached. The indicator stopped drawing further trades to avoid a crash.

Impact on stats: None. The simulator still tracks every trade; only chart rendering is capped.

Fix: Reduce Group 43 visibility limits. Typical cause is Max Closed set too high (500+) on a long lookback. Set Max Closed to 100–200. Also consider: disable Show All Closed (Group 2) and use Show Wins + Show Losses for category-specific views.

▶"Script must not use more than..." (memory / time)HOW & WHY

What it means: Pine Script computation limits hit. Common on very long lookbacks (5,000+) combined with all 5 models + all filters + many visible trades.

Fix: (1) Reduce Bars To Scan to 3,000. (2) Disable unused models. (3) Enable Group 45 → Performance Mode and Freeze Closed Trades.

▶"Error loading indicator" or blank chartHOW & WHY

What it means: Indicator failed to initialise. Usually a TradingView-side issue rather than configuration.

Fix: Refresh the browser. If persistent, remove and re-add the indicator to the chart. If still persistent, check TradingView status page for known outages.

Indicator-Level Warnings

Deep dive · click any setting
▶"No data in lookback window"HOW & WHY

What it means: Sim Mode = Custom Date Range but the configured From/To dates don't overlap any bars on the chart.

Fix: Group 44 → adjust dates to fall within your chart's loaded history. Or switch Sim Mode back to Lookback Period.

▶"Sample size too small for reliable stats"HOW & WHY

What it means: Under 30 closed trades. The Monitor will show numbers but the statistical confidence is low.

Fix: Increase Bars To Scan or enable more models/relax filters. The warning disappears above 30 trades but meaningful stats require 100+.

▶"Mirror mode overriding manual settings"HOW & WHY

What it means: You've set Setup Mirroring to copy one direction to another AND manually configured the target direction. Your manual settings are being ignored.

Fix: Either (a) accept the mirror and ignore your manual short-side settings, or (b) set Mirroring to None and keep your manual config.

Trade Label Emojis Explained

EmojiStateMeaning
🎱DetectedPending setup — waiting for entry fill
🤞🏻ActiveEntry filled — trade live
✅WinClosed at final TP
❌LossClosed at SL
⏰EOD CloseForce-closed at session end
⛔️MissedTP was hit before entry filled
🎛️TP ModifiedTarget modifier applied (Group 13)
🎚️SL ModifiedSL modifier applied (Group 14)
🧢Dev CappedDeviation multiplier rounded/capped
💰TP Hard CapTP truncated by dollar/tick cap (Group 28)
🛑Circuit BreakerDetection halted by daily loss limit (Group 28)
🪞MirroredSetup copied from the other direction (Group 5)

Common Unexpected Behaviours

Deep dive · click any setting
▶"I changed Group 17 but nothing happened"HOW & WHY

Why: Setup Mirroring is active (Group 5). Changes to Group 17 are silently ignored because shorts are being copied from longs.

Fix: Group 5 → Setup Mirroring = None, then retest your Group 17 change.

▶"TP1 Secure made my Win Rate look worse"HOW & WHY

Why: TP1 Secure trades that hit TP1 then retrace to BE are counted as neither win nor loss in some stat calculations — they're breakeven-closes. This can appear to reduce Win Rate while improving Net PnL and Profit Factor.

Not a bug: It's the expected behaviour. Check Net PnL and Profit Factor instead — those tell the real story.

▶"My TP1 amount doesn't match the partial size"HOW & WHY

Why: Size Method = Risk Back (Group 9). TP1 partial size is calculated dynamically to secure the specified multiple of risk, not as a simple percentage of position.

If this isn't what you want: Size Method = Equal Size instead. Each partial closes an equal fraction of position.

▶"My PF went way up, did I find something?"HOW & WHY

Check sample size. A PF jump from 1.5 to 2.5 on a stable sample is meaningful. On a sample below 50 trades, it's probably noise.

Check lookback consistency. If the jump only appears on one specific lookback value, it's regime-specific. Test at 3 different lookbacks before believing it.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Most 'errors' aren't indicator bugs — they're misconfigurations. The indicator's internal logic is deterministic; unexpected results almost always trace back to a setting you didn't mean to set. Start diagnosis by walking through your 5 core settings before suspecting a deeper issue.
part4-c-faq-general:
PART 4 · CHAPTER C

FAQ — General Usage

Broad questions about what the indicator is, what it does, which contexts it works in, and how it fits into a trading workflow.

Broad questions about the indicator — what it is, how it works at a high level, and how it fits into a trading workflow.

General Questions

Deep dive · click any setting
▶What is IOTSIM in one sentence?HOW & WHY

A TradingView indicator that detects potential ICT-based trade setups on historical data, simulates what would have happened if each were taken, and presents the cumulative results as statistics on an on-chart Insight Monitor.

▶Does it place real trades on my broker?HOW & WHY

No. IOTSIM is a simulator, not an execution engine. It doesn't connect to any broker or exchange; it only analyses what would have happened hypothetically. Real trades require manual execution (or an external webhook pipeline using the alert system — see Group 45).

▶Does it analyse live data or historical data?HOW & WHY

Both, simultaneously. Historical detections from the lookback window drive the statistics. As new bars arrive in real time, the simulator evaluates them for new setups, fills, TPs, SLs, and EOD closures just like it would for historical bars.

▶Which timeframes does it work best on?HOW & WHY

Designed primarily for 5-minute to 1-hour charts. The ICT methodology the indicator implements was calibrated for these timeframes. On 1-minute, structural noise increases and some models become unreliable. On 4-hour+, sample size per lookback drops substantially — you need very long lookbacks to get meaningful stats.

That said: The indicator runs on any timeframe TradingView supports. Results quality varies; validate with the Insight Monitor before committing to any non-default timeframe.

▶What asset classes work?HOW & WHY

CME futures (ES, NQ, CL, GC, etc.) are the canonical target. Crypto (BTC, ETH, majors) works well with correct Group 4 configuration. Equities and FX work with manual Margin/Leverage setup. Any asset with sufficient liquidity and clean price action can be simulated — the results quality reflects the underlying market quality.

▶Can I run multiple IOTSIM instances on one chart?HOW & WHY

Yes. Add the indicator multiple times. Each instance has independent configuration and its own Insight Monitor. Common use: one instance tuned for Longs Only, another for Shorts Only, on the same chart — cleaner separation than a single mirrored configuration.

Watch for: Object limits combine across all instances. If you run three indicators each with 200 visible closed trades, you're drawing 600 — likely to hit TradingView's ceiling.

▶How does IOTSIM differ from TradingView's built-in Strategy Tester?HOW & WHY

Strategy Tester is position-based: takes the next trade, reports PnL. Conceptually simple but built for traditional indicators (crossovers, oscillators).

IOTSIM is setup-based: detects every qualifying ICT setup simultaneously, tracks all of them independently, and reports aggregate statistics. It's designed for structural methodologies where multiple setups often coexist.

Use IOTSIM when: your methodology involves multiple concurrent setups, partial profits, dynamic target/SL logic, or wants to compare model performance.

▶I don't know ICT — can I still use this?HOW & WHY

Yes, but with caveats. The indicator runs without you understanding the underlying methodology. You can read the Insight Monitor and configure settings without knowing what an OB is.

But: You'll be fumbling in the dark when tuning. Not knowing why a setup type works or fails makes it hard to know which settings to try. The Foundations chapter is the minimum viable ICT primer — read it.

▶Do indicator updates break my saved configurations?HOW & WHY

Usually not, but occasionally. Major version upgrades may add settings or change defaults. TradingView preserves your existing settings where possible but any newly-added ones take the new default.

After every update: Verify your 5 core settings (Bars To Scan, Entry Level, Target Method, SL Method, TP1 Secure) match what you had before. Most updates don't touch these, but a quick check costs nothing.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
The indicator is a tool, not a trading strategy. It doesn't tell you which trades to take. It tells you how a hypothetical strategy would have performed on historical data. Your job is to interpret the results and decide what to do with them.
part4-d-faq-config:
PART 4 · CHAPTER D

FAQ — Configuration & Filters

Specific questions about settings, filters, and how groups interact. Organised by the decision area — setup, target/SL, filters, account configuration.

Specific questions about configuring settings, combining filters, and understanding how groups interact.

Setup & Entry Configuration

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Which model should I start with?HOW & WHY

Start with all five enabled. Run the Baseline Workflow. Then use Model Leaderboard (Part 2 Chapter F) to identify which two or three models actually produce edge on your asset — those become your working set.

Don't pre-decide based on reading about ICT. What works theoretically doesn't always work empirically. The Leaderboard is the source of truth.

▶What's the right Entry Level?HOW & WHY

Start at 0.34 — the indicator's default. This level front-runs the 0.5 mid-point of FVGs (Consequent Encroachment) and OBs (Mean Threshold), trading a small amount of theoretical R:R for a meaningful improvement in fill rate. Then sweep 0.25, 0.5, 0.618, 0.786 and pick the value that peaks Profit Factor on your asset. See Part 3 Chapter A Step 4 for the full sweep procedure.

Note on ICT OTE: The classical ICT Optimal Trade Entry is 0.618. If you want strict ICT methodology by the book, set Entry Level to 0.618. The default of 0.34 is the indicator's choice for fill-rate balance, not a redefinition of OTE.

Common asset patterns: Index futures often peak at 0.34–0.5. Crypto often peaks at 0.5–0.618 (deeper retracements due to higher volatility). FX often peaks at 0.25–0.34 (tighter price action).

▶Should I use Mirroring or configure both sides independently?HOW & WHY

Start mirrored. Halves your configuration work. Run the Baseline Workflow. If Full Stats shows Long and Short PFs within 0.3 of each other, stay mirrored.

Break the mirror when: Long/Short PF diverge by 0.5+, OR one direction has negative Net PnL while the other is positive. See Part 3 Chapter B.

Target & SL Configuration

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Which Target Method is best?HOW & WHY

Auto Calc is the right default because it scales with candle momentum. Switch when you have a specific reason — see Group 7's Target Method drawer for when each method wins.

Decision shortcut: Your main question is whether targets should be momentum-adaptive (Auto Calc, Fixed Devs) or structure-seeking (Swing H/L, PD Array Matrix). Answer that first; the specific method comes easily after.

▶Which SL Method is best?HOW & WHY

Setup Auto Calc is the default and works on most configurations. Two reasons to change:

(1) Max Avg SL Unrealised is high → switch to B.E. Trail or Advanced Trail.

(2) You want structural SLs → switch to PD Array Matrix (configure the arrays in Group 12).

▶Should I add SL Buffer?HOW & WHY

Only if Max Avg SL Unrealised is problematic. SL Buffer widens your stops, which lowers R:R on winners. The trade is only worth it when noise-tagging is demonstrably costing you more than the R:R loss.

Test: Enable SL Buffer. Does Win Rate rise by more than R:R drops? If yes, keep. If no, disable.

▶When should I enable Partials?HOW & WHY

Almost always enable them, paired with TP1 Secure (Group 13). The combination transforms your outcome distribution — breakeven-or-better becomes far more common than full-loss.

Exception: During diagnostic tuning. Temporarily disable partials to see the raw performance of your entry/target/SL. Re-enable after tuning.

Filter Questions

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Which filters should I enable?HOW & WHY

The minimum number that achieve your target Profit Factor. More filters isn't better. See Part 3 Chapter C for the correct layering order.

Universal recommendation: Min R:R Filter at 1.0. The safest filter — never worsens a valid configuration.

▶Killzones vs Macros — which?HOW & WHY

Killzones first. They're broader and easier to tune. Check Hour Analysis to identify profitable hours, enable only the killzones that contain them.

Macros as a second layer. Inside a profitable killzone, macros can further concentrate edge. Test by adding them on top of your killzone configuration; keep if PF improves, remove if not.

▶Is Filter Trades By HTF worth enabling?HOW & WHY

On trending assets, usually yes. Pair with a significantly higher HTF (5m chart → Daily HTF Scout). The filter enforces alignment with macro bias.

On range-bound assets, usually no. HTF alignment becomes ambiguous in consolidation, and the filter can produce very few detections.

▶Can I stack every filter?HOW & WHY

Technically yes, but you shouldn't. Stacking all filters often leaves you with under 30 trades, which produces unreliable PF readings. Sample size always matters.

Rule of thumb: After adding all filters, Detection count should still be 100+. If it's below, remove filters in reverse order of addition.

Account & Asset Configuration

Deep dive · click any setting
▶How do I configure Group 4 for my asset?HOW & WHY

CME futures (ES, NQ, CL): Auto-Detect ON, Margin 1.0, Leverage 1.0, Contracts = number of contracts. Done.

Crypto spot: Auto-Detect OFF, Margin = your investment in dollars, Leverage 1.0, Contracts 1.0.

Crypto isolated margin: Auto-Detect OFF, Margin = collateral, Leverage = your exchange's leverage, Contracts 1.0.

Equities: Auto-Detect OFF, Margin = per-trade dollar allocation, Leverage 1.0 (cash) or match broker (margin), Contracts 1.0.

See Group 4's chapter for full asset-class reference with examples.

▶My dollar PnL doesn't match what I'd expect in my brokerHOW & WHY

90% of the time, Group 4 is misconfigured. Verify all four values: Auto-Detect setting matches your asset class, Margin matches your actual per-trade collateral, Leverage matches your exchange, Contracts matches the number of contracts/units you'd trade.

Sanity check with the Calculator: Group 4's built-in Leverage/Margin Calculator shows exactly how Buying Power is derived. If the calculator's Buying Power doesn't match your actual broker's buying power, the inputs are wrong.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Most configuration questions have the same answer: start with defaults, tune one variable at a time, let the Monitor tell you what's working. Pre-deciding 'I want SL Method to be Advanced Trail because I read it's better' almost always produces worse results than discovering the right method empirically on your specific asset.
part4-e-faq-stats:
PART 4 · CHAPTER E

FAQ — Statistics & Interpretation

How to read Monitor values correctly. Each question isolates a specific interpretation risk or common misread — answered with the sample-size, comparison, and context considerations you need.

How to read Monitor values correctly, what specific numbers mean, and common misinterpretations to avoid.

Reading Individual Stats

Deep dive · click any setting
▶What Win Rate should I aim for?HOW & WHY

Depends on R:R. 40% Win Rate with 3.0 R:R has positive expectancy. 65% Win Rate with 0.6 R:R has negative expectancy. Don't optimise Win Rate alone.

Healthy combinations:

• Scalping: 65–75% Win Rate with 0.8–1.5 R:R.

• Balanced: 45–60% Win Rate with 1.5–2.5 R:R.

• High R:R: 30–45% Win Rate with 2.5–4.0 R:R.

All three produce Profit Factor > 1.5 when Win Rate and R:R are paired correctly.

▶What Profit Factor should I aim for?HOW & WHY

Minimum acceptable: 1.5. This is the floor for a genuinely profitable system after accounting for real-world slippage and fees.

Good: 1.8–2.2. Most well-tuned configurations end up here.

Exceptional: 2.5+ with a sample above 200 trades, stable across lookbacks. Rare but real.

Suspicious: 3.0+. Check for overfitting, look-ahead bias, or unrealistic modifier stacks. Often a small-sample artefact.

▶How big a sample do I need?HOW & WHY

Minimum for any meaningful read: 100 closed trades.

For model-level decisions (Leaderboard): 30+ trades per model.

For hour-level decisions (Hour Analysis): 15+ trades per hour.

For day-level decisions (Day Analysis): 20+ trades per day.

Below these thresholds, variance dominates signal.

▶Is a big Peak Max DD bad?HOW & WHY

Relative to Peak Max Realised, not absolute. $500 Max DD on $5,000 peak is 10% — healthy. $500 Max DD on $1,000 peak is 50% — brutal.

Rule of thumb: Under 20% of peak is comfortable. 20–30% is tolerable for aggressive systems. Over 30% means position size is too large for the strategy's volatility profile.

For prop firms: Under 10% is typically required. Many prop accounts have hard 4–5% daily DD limits.

▶How do I interpret TP1 Total vs Net PnL?HOW & WHY

TP1 Total / Net PnL ratio: What fraction of your profits come from partials.

• Under 20%: Partials aren't adding much. Core setup profitable on its own.

• 20–50%: Healthy contribution from partials without over-dependence.

• Over 50%: Configuration heavily dependent on partials. Core setup may be marginal. Try disabling partials temporarily to verify.

Comparing Stats Across States

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Comparing two configurationsHOW & WHY

Check the same five metrics: Profit Factor, Win Rate, Detections, Peak Max DD, Max Avg SL Unrealised.

One is better when: PF is higher AND sample size is comparable AND Max Avg SL Unrealised isn't dramatically worse.

Watch for: A configuration with higher PF but fewer detections. Fewer detections = smaller sample = less reliable PF. Prefer higher PF with sample > 100 over higher PF with sample < 50.

▶Comparing across lookbacksHOW & WHY

Stable PF across 5,000 / 1,500 / 500 bars: Durable edge. Trade with confidence.

PF declining as lookback shortens: Edge was historical, is now weakening. Pause trading; investigate regime.

PF rising as lookback shortens: Edge is currently strong. Ride it while the regime persists; tighten risk in case it reverts.

Erratic (high-low-high): Sample size issue. Lengthen the shortest lookback until all three samples are above 100.

Common Misinterpretations

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Don't optimise Win Rate aloneHOW & WHY

A configuration that's 90% win rate with 0.2 R:R has a 2% negative edge per trade. You win 9 out of 10 small wins, lose 1 big loss, net negative.

Always check PF and R:R together. Win Rate in isolation is misleading.

▶The 'one big winner' trapHOW & WHY

A setup with PF 2.5 and Avg Win = 10× Avg Loss looks great — until you check Median Win, which is 1.5× Avg Loss.

What that reveals: The good stats are carried by a few outlier wins. Typical trades are marginal. When outliers stop occurring, the edge evaporates.

Toggle Avg/Median: Group 3 → Avg/Median to Median. If stats get much worse, the system is outlier-dependent — a risk to understand but not necessarily a reason to avoid.

▶Killzone Win Rate can mislead if sample is tinyHOW & WHY

Killzone Win Rate 100% sounds great. If that killzone had 4 trades in the lookback, it's meaningless.

Check trade count per killzone. The Monitor shows totals somewhere — require 30+ trades before trusting a Killzone-specific Win Rate reading.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
Every Monitor stat is conditional on sample size. Before concluding anything, ask: how many trades produced this number? Under 30, it's probably noise. Over 100, it's probably signal. In between, use cautiously.
part4-f-faq-technical:
PART 4 · CHAPTER F

FAQ — Performance & Technical

Performance-related questions, TradingView integration, data-calculation behaviour, and versioning. The technical answers that don't fit cleanly elsewhere.

Performance-related questions — speed, TradingView limits, caching, and technical details about how the indicator handles heavy configurations.

Performance

Deep dive · click any setting
▶The chart feels sluggishHOW & WHY

Quick fixes, in order of impact:

(1) Group 45 → Performance Mode ON. Reduces visual update frequency.

(2) Group 45 → Freeze Closed Trades ON. Closed trades stop being redrawn.

(3) Group 3 → Bars To Scan reduced. 5,000 → 2,000 halves the compute load.

(4) Group 43 → Max Closed reduced. 500 → 100 reduces the chart's object count.

(5) Group 2 → Show All Closed OFF. Closed-trade rendering is the biggest chart-load source.

Most sluggishness resolves with (1) + (2) + (5) alone.

▶How do I avoid the object limit error?HOW & WHY

TradingView caps total drawn objects (lines, boxes, labels) at around 500. Group 43's visibility limits control the simulator's share.

Keep these totals under 400 to avoid hitting the ceiling:

Max Detected 30 + Max Active 30 + Max Closed 100 + Max Missed 50 + Max Wins 50 + Max Losses 50 + Max 2nd Chance 30 + Max Partials 50 = 390.

Adjust individual limits up or down based on which categories you actually care about.

▶Multi-instance performanceHOW & WHY

Two instances on one chart doubles compute load. If single instance was fine but two instances lag, reduce both their Bars To Scan and enable Performance Mode on both.

Object limits combine. If each instance has Max Closed 200, total drawn objects = 400 (plus active, pending, etc.) — quickly hits the ceiling. Halve visibility limits when running multi-instance.

TradingView Integration

Deep dive · click any setting
▶How do alerts integrate with TradingView?HOW & WHY

Group 45's alert toggles activate the indicator's internal alert-firing logic. Each toggle enables the simulator to call `alert()` at the specified event.

TradingView alerts are what actually deliver notifications. Create a TradingView alert with:

• Condition: 'Any alert() function call'.

• Scope: the IOTSIM indicator.

• Trigger: 'Once per bar' (not 'Once per bar close' — that delays events by one full bar).

• Expiration: renew periodically (TradingView alerts expire on free tier).

Message template: What you configure in Group 45 becomes the alert body.

▶Using alerts with broker webhooksHOW & WHY

TradingView alert → Webhook URL → Broker execution. The alert message body is posted to your configured URL.

Format the Group 45 template to match your broker's payload spec. Example for a broker expecting JSON:

{"symbol": "{symbol}", "side": "{direction}", "price": "{price}", "event": "{event}"}

Test with tiny size first. Webhook-execution bugs are expensive if your configuration fires dozens of alerts a day.

▶Why does Detection count differ from what I see on chart?HOW & WHY

The chart visualises only recent setups (capped by Group 43). The Insight Monitor aggregates every setup in the Lookback Period.

If chart shows 30 detections but Monitor says 200: Normal behaviour. 200 were detected; 30 most recent are visible.

Data & Calculation Behaviour

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Does the indicator use 'future' bars?HOW & WHY

No look-ahead bias. Every calculation uses only bars at or before the current evaluation point. The indicator's confirmation rules (Swing Strength, etc.) inherently require N bars after a candidate to confirm — those N future bars are part of the confirmation window but no later bars are consulted.

What this means practically: A swing high at bar X isn't confirmed until bar X+N (where N = Swing Strength). Before bar X+N, the simulator doesn't 'know' about the swing — it only sees it when it actually becomes structurally valid.

▶What about the real-time (live) bar?HOW & WHY

Live bar calculations are provisional. Price on the current bar updates constantly; detections and trade states recompute with each tick.

Once the bar closes: The state is final. Subsequent bars never look back at the closed bar's state and change it.

Implication: A detection that appears mid-bar may disappear if the bar closes in a way that invalidates the setup. This is normal; wait for bar close before acting on a live detection.

▶Does the indicator repaint?HOW & WHY

Historical results don't repaint — they're final.

Real-time detections can provisionally update as the current bar develops. Once the bar closes, its state is locked in.

Trade labels don't move backwards. An active trade's entry, TP, and SL are fixed at detection time; they can't shift after the fact.

▶What timezone does the indicator use?HOW & WHY

EST, always. Killzones and macros (Groups 29 and 30) reference EST fixed windows. This ensures consistency across users worldwide.

TradingView's chart timezone is independent. You can display your chart in any timezone without affecting the indicator's internal EST-based calculations.

DST adjustments: Handled automatically — the indicator tracks EDT during daylight saving, EST during standard time.

Versioning & Updates

Deep dive · click any setting
▶How do I know which version I have?HOW & WHY

TradingView shows the indicator's name and version in the top-left corner of the chart when the indicator is loaded. Hover the name to see the full version string.

Updates are published by the author to TradingView's indicator library. If your indicator shows an 'update available' notification, review the changelog before accepting — major updates may add or change settings.

▶Backing up configurationsHOW & WHY

TradingView has no built-in indicator-config backup. Your only copy is the one in the chart's indicator settings.

Manual backup protocol: Screenshot the settings pane or keep a text file listing your 10 most important settings. Update it whenever you finalise a new configuration.

Multiple saves: Save each profitable configuration as a separate TradingView chart layout. 'NQ 5m config A', 'NQ 5m config B'. You can switch between them in one click.

💡 KEY INSIGHT
The indicator is deterministic — given identical settings, data, and market state, it produces identical results. If something seems non-deterministic, it's almost always a real-time-bar effect (live bar updates tick-by-tick) or a TradingView-side rendering issue, not the indicator.
appendix-emoji-key:
APPENDIX

Emoji Key

Every emoji the indicator uses on chart labels, in the Monitor, and in alerts — in one reference page. Use this when decoding an unfamiliar label on a label or alert message.

Trade Lifecycle — State Emojis

EmojiStateWhen It Appears
🎱DetectedA setup has been found and is waiting for entry to fill. Magic eight ball — asking the future.
🤞🏻ActiveEntry has filled; the trade is live. Fingers crossed.
✅WinThe final TP closed the trade in profit.
❌LossThe SL closed the trade.
⏰EOD CloseThe session ended and EOD Auto-Close force-closed the trade.
⛔️MissedThe TP was reached before the entry filled. The setup became a missed opportunity.
🎯Target HitA partial TP (TP1/TP2/TP3) reached its mark.

Configuration — Modifier Emojis

EmojiMeaningIndicator Trigger
🎛️TP ModifiedAny Group 13 target modifier is active for this trade (Round Down, Front Run, 1/2 Targets, 3/4 Targets, TP Expansion).
🎚️SL ModifiedAny Group 14 SL modifier is active (SL Buffer, Extra SL Buffer, 1/2 SL, 3/4 SL, SL Expansion).
🧢Dev RoundedThe deviation multiplier was rounded or capped by Min/Max Tgt Devs or by Increaser (Group 7/28).
💰TP Hard CappedThe target was truncated by the TP Hard Cap ceiling in Group 28.
🛑Circuit BreakerDetection was halted because the Daily Circuit Breaker's loss limit was hit. Setup appears on chart but is excluded from active calculations.
🪞Mirror ActiveThe setup was generated from the other direction via Setup Mirroring (Group 5).

Where Each Emoji Shows Up

Deep dive · click any setting
▶On chart trade labelsHOW & WHY

Main emoji — reflects the current state (🎱 / 🤞🏻 / ✅ / ❌ / ⏰ / ⛔️). Positioned at the start of the label.

Modifier emojis — appended to the label when active. 🎛️ next to TP-related labels, 🎚️ next to SL-related labels. Multiple modifiers show multiple emojis.

Special-state emojis — 💰, 🧢, 🛑, 🪞 appear alongside the main state emoji when the corresponding condition applies.

▶On the Insight MonitorHOW & WHY

Recent Trades page: Each row in Recent Trades shows its lifecycle emoji next to the trade ID.

Model Leaderboard: Direction indicated by L (Long) or S (Short) rather than emoji.

▶In alert messagesHOW & WHY

When a state transition fires, the new state's emoji appears in the alert body (e.g. "✅ NQ1! LONG @ 18400 ID L-FVG-0417a"). This lets you parse the alert type at a glance in a phone notification without reading the full body.

💡 CUSTOMISING
All the emojis above are baked in and not customisable. What is customisable: the colours of labels and lines (Group 42), the sizes of labels, which label categories appear on chart (Group 2), and the Monitor's colours (Group 31).
appendix-base-setups:
APPENDIX

Base Setups

Proven starting configurations for each major asset class. Copy these into a fresh chart to establish a sensible baseline; then run the Part 3 Workflows to tune for your specific asset. These are scaffolding — they get you to 'working' fast but are not intended as final configurations.

CME Index Futures (ES, NQ, YM, RTY)

Deep dive · click any setting
▶SettingsHOW & WHY

Chart: 5-minute or 15-minute timeframe.

Group 3 — Engine:

• Sim Mode: Lookback Period. Bars To Scan: 5,000. Avg/Median: Average. Trades To Track: 5,000.

Group 4 — Account:

• Global Value: Dollars. Auto-Detect: ON. Contracts: 1 (or your trading size). Margin: 1.0. Leverage: 1.0.

Group 5 — Global:

appendix-insight-optimisation:
APPENDIX

Insight Monitor Optimisation

The X-ray view of your configuration. Each Full Stats row is a symptom; each symptom has a specific set of dials that respond to it. This appendix is the diagnostic recipe book — 24 rows, 24 one-page diagnoses. Use as a quick reference when the Monitor shows a number you want to move.

Deep dive · click any setting
▶1 · Detected / ClosedHOW & WHY

What it represents: The number of setups the engine 'saw' (Detected) vs the number of trades actually executed and finished (Closed).

The edge: A measure of filter aggression. If you detect 5,000 trades but only 100 close, your entry levels are too deep a retrace or you have thousands of pending positions. If you close 4,900 out of 5,000, entries and/or TP levels are too close to price — you're likely making small gains per position with little room for error.

To increase closed trades: Move entries closer to price action. Lower Entry Level (Group 6/17), shorten Partial Count, reduce Tgt Array Depth.

To reduce detected noise: Combine filters to fine-tune the engine. For scalping: enable all models, disable all modifiers except dollar/tick filters (Group 28) to control risk and profit with precision.

▶2 · Win RateHOW & WHY

What it represents: The percentage of closed trades that secured profit.

The edge: Your psychological armor. But remember: a 40% Win Rate with massive winners often produces higher Profit Factor and better R-returns than a 90% Win Rate with tiny winners. Don't optimise Win Rate alone.

If Win Rate is low: Increase SL Array Depth in the PD Array Matrix (Group 12) to hide stops behind deeper, safer institutional levels. Alternatively use modifiers like 1/2 or 3/4 SL (Group 14) to bring net negative PnL down or take risk off the table earlier.

▶3 · Net / Avg RealisedHOW & WHY

What it represents: The total bottom-line profit (Net) and average cash value of every closed trade (Avg).

The edge: Your true expectancy. Tells you exactly how much the account grows on average every time the algorithm pulls the trigger.

If average realised is too low: You may be on too small a timeframe for your expected returns, or capping your upside. Uncheck TP Hard Cap (Group 28), or switch your Target Method from Fixed Devs to PD Array Matrix (Group 7) to let trades run to the next liquidity pool.

▶4 · Max / Avg Unrealised ProfitHOW & WHY

What it represents: Peak in-the-money value each winning trade reached during its lifetime (Max), and the mean across all wins (Avg).

The edge: The gap between your Avg Unrealised and Avg Realised is how much profit you're systematically leaving on the table.

If the gap is large: Enable 'Move SL to B.E. after TP1' (Group 13's TP1 Secure) to lock in profit, or use a Dollar TP Cap set to a realistic target to force the system to secure floating profit before the market reverses.

▶5 · Single Max / Avg DDHOW & WHY

What it represents: How much 'pain' (negative floating PnL) a trade endured before eventually closing.

The edge: If average drawdown is too high you need to optimise stop losses — but don't place them too tight or you'll be wicked out of the larger positive-PnL trades.

How to reduce: Try 1/2 SL or 3/4 SL modifiers. Turn on Extra SL Buffer in modifiers (Group 14). Increase PD Array Depth for SLs (Group 11) to hide behind deeper arrays. Or change entry to a deeper retrace level like 0.618–0.786 to enter the market at a safer, deeper price.

▶6 · Peak Max Realised PnLHOW & WHY

What it represents: The highest peak your account balance ever reached across the lookback.

The edge: The all-time high. Compared against Peak Max DD, it defines system stability.

To push it higher: Work on Profit Factor — tighter losses, broader winners. See rows 2, 3, and 23.

▶7 · Peak Max DDHOW & WHY

What it represents: The deepest structural canyon your realised balance fell into from its highest peak.

The edge: Defines system stability. A system that peaks at $10k but routinely suffers $8k drawdowns is too volatile to trade with real money. Check both Total and per-direction — is one direction compromising your performance?

To smooth the equity curve: Use the Daily Circuit Breaker (Group 28) to halt trading after a bad streak. Activate Directional Partials (Near) combined with 'Move SL to BE at TP1/2/3' to constantly bank cash.

▶8 · TP1 Hits / TotalHOW & WHY

What it represents: How many times your first partial profit target (TP1) was struck, and the total cash secured.

The edge: The backbone of profit-banking strategies. TP1 converts 'hope trades' into 'paid trades'.

If TP1 is rarely hitting: Change Partial Profits Split Method to PD Arrays (Near) combined with Early Offset to force the engine to take money off the table sooner.

▶9 · TP1 Max / AvgHOW & WHY

What it represents: The highest single payout from a TP1 hit, and the average.

The edge: If your average TP1 payout is tiny, the partial isn't worth the volume you're sacrificing on the remainder.

How to fix: Reduce the number of partials. Or change Split Method to PD Arrays (Grid) with Partial Size = Equal Size. This divides your final TP destination by partial count and snaps each fraction to the nearest PD array.

▶10 · Final TPs HitHOW & WHY

What it represents: Percentage of trades that survived all the way to their final Take Profit.

The edge: Grades the realism of your targets. If this is very low, you're dreaming, not trading.

How to increase: Use the 'Reducer' feature on Max Tgt Devs (Group 7) if using Auto Calc — incrementally reducing the cap raises hit rate. Or reduce Target Array Depth from 5 down to 1 or 2, forcing the algorithm to target the absolute closest FVG or Order Block rather than a distant swing point.

▶11 · 2nd Chance Win RateHOW & WHY

What it represents: Setups where TP was hit before your entry filled, but price eventually returned, triggered your entry, and played out.

The edge: Tells you if 'used' setups are toxic. Contrary to popular belief, a target sweep doesn't always kill the setup — price often revisits entry levels to offer another bite.

If the Win Rate here is terrible: Disable 2nd Chance Setups entirely in Group 5. If it's markedly better than global Win Rate, embrace it.

▶12 · SL HitsHOW & WHY

What it represents: The raw percentage of trades that hit SL.

The edge: Pure defensive tracking.

If SL hits skew heavily toward one direction: The market may be trending in the opposite direction. To verify, go into the underperforming direction's SL Method and switch to Setup Auto Calc to strictly bind stops to their entry models.

▶13 · Max / Avg SL UnrealisedHOW & WHY

What it represents: When a trade ultimately hit SL, how much profit did it see first before turning around and killing you?

The edge: The metric of poor trade management. A trade should never go deep into positive PnL and then close fully negative.

How to fix: Activate Advanced Trail SL (Group 11). Set Trail R Trigger to 0.5 and Trail R to 0.1. If the trade gets halfway to target, the stop aggressively trails price, preventing the full loss.

▶14 · Missed EntriesHOW & WHY

What it represents: Trades where the algorithm correctly predicted direction and hit the target, but your entry was never triggered.

The edge: Tracks how often you're left on the sidelines because your entry requirements are too strict.

How to reduce: Change Entry Method from OB Candle Range to Disp. Candle Range (Group 16). Or use the Entry Level options to bring entries closer to price (reduce from 0.5 toward 0.34 — the indicator's default — or 0.25).

▶15 · Missed PnLHOW & WHY

What it represents: The hypothetical dollar amount you missed out on from untriggered entries.

The edge: Highlights the physical cash cost of being a perfectionist. A massive number here is the ultimate wake-up call.

How to fix: Try Front Run (+/- 0.1425) entry option to guarantee fills — just be mindful of the drawdowns that come with it.

▶16 · Open / PendingHOW & WHY

What it represents: How many trades are live right now plus the number waiting for entry fills.

The edge: Your active order-book footprint.

If too many pending trades clog the engine: Enable EOD Auto-Close (Group 5) to wipe the slate each day, or tighten Killzones (Group 29) to reduce the window in which new pending trades can form.

▶17 · Total Value OpenHOW & WHY

What it represents: Total floating dollar value (positive or negative) of all live trades.

The edge: Your live risk exposure.

If terrifyingly high: You may be on too high a timeframe with trades taking too long to resolve. Or entries are too close to price with targets set at over-optimistic distances. Move entries further away (higher Fib level) and bring stop losses and/or take profits closer.

▶18 · Max / Avg Win BarsHOW & WHY

What it represents: How many candles (time) it takes for a winning trade to hit target.

The edge: Capital velocity. If winners take 40 bars to play out, your capital is locked up and exposed to macro shifts for a long time.

How to accelerate: If trades are taking too long, you're trading in sub-optimal market conditions. Enable Killzone and/or Macro Time Filters (Groups 29, 30) to force the algorithm to only trade during key times.

▶19 · Max / Avg Loss BarsHOW & WHY

What it represents: How many candles it takes for a losing trade to hit SL.

The edge: If losing trades take 2 bars while winners take 40, your entries are getting crushed immediately when you're wrong.

How to survive early volatility: Switch SL Method to PD Array Matrix (Group 11) and select deep nested arrays to hide stops behind stronger structure. Or add SL Buffer (Group 14) to push the stop beyond the initial noise zone.

▶20 · Killzone Win RateHOW & WHY

What it represents: Your success rate specifically inside ICT-defined high-volume session windows.

The edge: Time is more important than price. Proves whether your strategy capitalises on major session volume injections.

If 30% globally but 65% in Killzones: Enable Killzone Filter (Group 29). The engine will permanently ignore the rest of the day. Instant PF boost.

▶21 · Macro Win RateHOW & WHY

What it represents: Success rate specifically inside the 20-minute ICT Macro windows.

The edge: Macros are algorithmically-timed volatility spikes. High Win Rates here mean your model thrives on fast, aggressive displacement.

For rapid scalp-like trades: Disable Killzones and exclusively enable Macro Time Filters (Group 30). You'll trade fewer but faster setups.

▶22 · Net / Avg R:RHOW & WHY

What it represents: The true mathematical multiplier of your system, ignoring dollar amounts (e.g. risking 1R to make 3R).

The edge: R:R is the great equaliser. You can trade with $10 or $10,000 — the R:R proves if the math works. Net R:R is the accumulated total across all trades.

To floor your R:R: Enable the Min R:R Filter (Group 15/26) and set it to at least 1. The engine will automatically reject any trade where the target isn't at least 1× larger than the stop.

▶23 · Profit FactorHOW & WHY

What it represents: Gross Profit divided by Gross Loss.

The edge: The holy grail. Anything above 1.0 makes money. Anything above 2.0–3.0 is an absolute money-making monster.

How to boost it: Cut losers faster. Turn on 1/2 SL or 3/4 SL modifiers (Group 14) to slash the financial impact of your losing trades. Or increase Min R:R Filter to 1.5+ to only take trades with strong expected value.

▶24 · Top / Last W and L StreaksHOW & WHY

What it represents: Your longest winning and losing streaks.

The edge: Mental and dollar capital preservation. If the data says your max losing streak is 6, you won't panic when you lose 4 in a row live. Check long and short separately — if either has a zero next to the last streak, the streak is still ongoing.

How to protect against destruction streaks: Use the Daily Circuit Breaker (Group 28). Set it to halt trading after a specific dollar loss to physically prevent long losing streaks from destroying the account in a single day.

How to use this appendix in practice

Open the Insight Monitor → Full Stats. Scan the rows. Pick the single worst-performing stat relative to your expectations. Find its recipe above. Apply the suggested dial change. Rerun. Repeat.

One recipe at a time. Applying three recipes simultaneously makes it impossible to tell which caused the improvement. Discipline: one change, observe, decide.

💡 THE BIG INSIGHT
The data does not lie. Stop guessing what works. Use the Full Stats Insight Monitor to diagnose the exact weakness in your system, tweak the corresponding dials in the UI, and engineer your own customised edge.
appendix-life-hacks:
APPENDIX

Life Hacks

Workflow shortcuts, creative uses, and power-user techniques that go beyond setting-by-setting documentation. These are the tricks experienced IOTSIM users develop over time to extract more insight, save time, and get more out of the indicator's flexibility. None are required — but any of them can meaningfully improve your workflow.

Deep dive · click any setting
▶Running Multiple IOTSIM Instances on One ChartHOW & WHY

TradingView lets you add the same indicator multiple times. Each instance runs independently with its own settings — opening up comparison workflows that aren't possible with a single instance.

Dollars vs Ticks side-by-side: Add two instances. Set one to Global Value = Dollars, one to Ticks. Hide the trade visuals on one (Group 42 → Show All Position Visuals OFF) and keep only the Monitor visible. You now see performance in both units simultaneously — dollar PnL on one monitor, tick performance on the other — without toggling.

A/B testing configurations: The most powerful use. Instance A = your current config. Instance B = one change you want to test (different entry level, different target method, partials on vs off). Both process the same price data simultaneously → perfect apples-to-apples comparison on the same chart.

Readability tips: Hide trade visuals on one instance (Group 42). Position the two Monitors in different corners (Group 31). Use different Monitor pages on each — Full Stats on one, Model Leaderboard on the other.

Modifying specific setups once a baseline exists: Once you've found a profitable baseline with one instance, add a second to experiment aggressively — trying different SL methods, extreme entry levels, or new filters — while the first instance stays untouched as your control.

⚠️ OBJECT LIMIT
Running multiple instances multiplies the chart's object count. Halve visibility limits (Group 43) on each when running multi-instance to avoid TradingView's object-limit error.
▶Saved Chart Layouts for Different Asset ClassesHOW & WHY

Different asset classes need fundamentally different Group 4 configurations, and often benefit from different entry levels, targets, and filters. Save dedicated TradingView chart layouts for each.

LayoutKey Configuration
CryptoAuto-Detect OFF, Margin/Leverage per exchange, Entry Level 0.5, EOD Auto-Close OFF, Killzones adjusted for preferred crypto sessions.
FuturesAuto-Detect ON, Margin 1.0, Leverage 1.0, Entry Level 0.34 (the indicator's default; 0.618 for strict ICT OTE), EOD Auto-Close ON, standard ICT Killzones.
ForexAuto-Detect OFF, Margin/Leverage per broker, appropriate session times for the pairs you trade.
CommoditiesAuto-Detect ON (CME products), Entry Level 0.382–0.5, wider SL buffers for commodity volatility.
EquitiesAuto-Detect OFF, Margin = per-trade allocation, Leverage 1.0 for cash accounts (or match broker for margin).

Multiple layouts per asset class: You can even save multiple within one class — 'Crypto Scalp' with tight targets and 'Crypto Swing' with deeper targets and higher timeframes. Switching asset contexts becomes a one-click operation.

▶Split-Screen Layouts on the Same AssetHOW & WHY

TradingView's multi-chart layouts let you view the same asset across multiple timeframes simultaneously. Save a split-screen layout — e.g. 5-minute chart alongside 1-hour chart — each with its own IOTSIM instance.

Multi-timeframe analysis: Lower timeframe shows granular setups and precise entry levels; higher timeframe shows broader structural context. Are HTF IPDA levels supporting the direction? Is there an HTF FVG/OB that your lower-TF entry sits within? The Monitor on each tells you independently whether that timeframe's configuration is performing.

Same asset, different lookbacks: One panel at 5,000-bar lookback (long-term baseline), another at 300 bars (current conditions). Both perspectives simultaneously without toggling.

▶Shorts Mirror Longs for Fast Initial OptimisationHOW & WHY

First thing to do on a new asset: Enable 'Shorts Mirror Longs' in Group 5. This halves the number of settings you need to configure — every Long Setup change is automatically applied to shorts.

Why it's faster: You can rapidly iterate through entry levels, target methods, and SL approaches on the long side while seeing the combined Total column update in real time for both directions. One variable change, one Monitor update.

Once profitable: Turn mirroring off. Check Long vs Short columns. If both are profitable mirrored, you may not need to change anything. If one underperforms, configure it independently via Groups 17–27.

Key insight: Mirroring lets you optimise one set of 11 groups instead of two sets of 11 during exploration. Once broad strokes are right, fine-tune each direction separately.

▶Disable Partials to See True Target PerformanceHOW & WHY

TP1 Secure + partials is excellent risk management — but it distorts the Monitor's view of how your targets are actually performing. Trades that would have hit full TP close at breakeven after TP1; trades that would have hit SL also close at breakeven.

For an unfiltered view: Temporarily disable partials entirely (Group 9 → Enable Partials OFF). Removes TP1/TP2/TP3 from the equation; every trade runs to its full conclusion — final TP, SL, or EOD.

What the resulting stats tell you: Win Rate reflects how often full TP is reached without a safety net. Net PnL reflects true R:R of entry-to-TP-to-SL. Max/Avg DD shows real drawdown exposure without BE protection.

Diagnostic use, not a recommendation: Ask "Is my core setup profitable on its own, or am I entirely dependent on TP1 Secure?" If profitable without partials, adding them back only improves it. If unprofitable, you're masking a fundamental problem — fix entry/target/SL first.

Alternative — PD Arrays (Grid): If you don't want to disable partials, try switching Split Method to Grid. Distributes partial levels more evenly across structural features rather than clustering near entry. Less aggressive partial strategy; captures intermediate profit without moving SL to breakeven as quickly.

▶Lookback Bracketing — Finding Your Optimal WindowHOW & WHY

Instead of guessing 1,000 vs 3,000 bars, bracket systematically:

Start at 5,000. Record PF. Drop to 2,500 → record. Then 1,000 → 500 → 300 → 200. Plot mentally — you're looking for the range where PF stabilises or peaks.

Patterns:

• PF improves as lookback drops: Your setup performs better in current conditions than historical — regime shift in your favour.

• PF degrades as lookback drops: Setup was optimised for conditions that no longer exist.

Sweet spot: Lowest lookback that still provides 100+ trades (statistically meaningful) while reflecting current conditions. Pair with split-screen layouts to monitor both long-term and short-term simultaneously.

▶Timeframe Shifting for Quick Local Trend ReadingHOW & WHY

Instinct: Lower the lookback to see recent setups. Problem: lower lookback means fewer trades = every stat becomes less reliable. A PF calculated from 15 trades is far less trustworthy than one from 300.

Better approach: Lower the timeframe instead. Drop to 30 seconds or lower while keeping Lookback at maximum (5,000 bars). On a 30s chart, 5,000 bars = ~40 hours of trading — large sample and recent data.

Read the Monitor: Whichever direction (Long vs Short column) shows higher PF and Win Rate on this low timeframe is the direction favoured in very recent market conditions. Data-driven local trend bias without touching your primary lookback.

Multi-timeframe confluence: Also check one or two higher timeframes. If 5m chart shows longs dominating and 1h agrees, multi-timeframe confluence supports a long bias. If they disagree — low TF favours longs, high TF clearly favours shorts — the conflict itself is valuable: high probability of local reversal.

Combine with split-screen: Save a layout with a 30-second chart alongside your primary trading TF, each with its own IOTSIM. The 30s panel becomes a real-time local-trend gauge; the primary panel is your actual trading setup.

▶Model Leaderboard Elimination StrategyHOW & WHY

Rather than finding the best model, eliminate the worst:

Start with all five models enabled. Check Leaderboard. Identify the worst-performing model/direction — lowest PF. Disable just that one. Recheck Leaderboard. Disable the next worst. Continue until disabling another model causes overall PF to drop.

Why elimination is more reliable than picking winners: Removing a consistently losing model improves total stats even if remaining models haven't changed. The point where removing another hurts rather than helps is your optimal model set.

Cadence: Run monthly. Markets evolve — a model that was winning last quarter may be losing this one. The elimination workflow catches shifts without requiring deep analysis.

▶The Pre-Session Settings SnapshotHOW & WHY

Before each trading session, turn the Monitor ON, switch to Overview. Take a mental (or physical) snapshot of the four headline numbers: Win Rate, Net PnL, Peak Max DD, Detection count.

At session end: Check the same numbers. If Win Rate or PF has meaningfully deteriorated compared to your snapshot, the session's conditions may not suit your setup.

Across multiple sessions: If deterioration persists, lower the lookback and check whether your configuration needs adjusting for the current regime.

Why it works: 30 seconds of pre-session snapshot gives you an early warning system for regime changes. Most traders only realise their setup has stopped working after significant losses. The snapshot catches degradation early.

▶Custom Date Range for Event-Specific TestingHOW & WHY

Before a major economic event — FOMC, CPI, NFP, earnings — test against history: Switch Sim Mode to Custom Date Range (Group 44). Set dates to cover previous instances of the same event.

Example: Date range = last three FOMC announcement days. If your setup's PF is terrible during those periods, you have data-driven justification for sitting out (or tightening with additional filters and smaller targets) during the upcoming event.

If it thrives during those periods: You know your setup handles the volatility well — you can trade the event with confidence.

▶The One-at-a-Time Filter RuleHOW & WHY

Never add multiple filters simultaneously. Enable one — SMA, MSS/BOS, Divergence, Min R:R — check the Monitor, record PF and trade count. Disable. Enable a different one. Compare independently.

Only after independent testing should you combine. When you do, add one at a time and check after each. If adding a second filter improves PF but drops trade count below 50, you're probably overfitting.

Goal: The minimum number of filters that maximise PF with a trade count you're comfortable with. More filters is not inherently better — each reduces sample size, and at some point you're optimising for noise rather than signal.

▶The 60-Second Diagnostic ChecklistHOW & WHY

When something looks wrong, run this checklist before changing any settings:

1. Group 4: Is Auto-Detect correct for your asset class? Are Margin/Leverage/Contracts producing realistic dollar values? Misconfigured Group 4 = 90% of 'my PnL looks wrong' issues.

2. Lookback: Did you recently change it? 5,000 vs 200 bars are completely different datasets. Make sure you're looking at the window you intend.

3. Trade Direction: Is it Long and Short, or accidentally Long Only / Short Only from a prior analysis?

4. Mirroring: On when you think off, or vice versa? If you've been configuring shorts but mirroring is copying longs, your short changes aren't active.

5. Killzones/Macros: Enabled when you didn't intend? An active Killzone excluding the current session means zero new detections during that session.

6. Reload the chart: Pine Script recalculates from scratch on reload. Suspicious numbers often resolve with a simple reload.

▶Selecting Setups Using Deviation PrecisionHOW & WHY

The hidden signal on every TP label: When multiple setups are firing in the same direction and you need to decide which to take — or when you're in a trade and new setups appear with higher TPs — the deviation multiplier contains signal most traders overlook.

Why precise deviations matter: The 'Round Deviation Down to 0.25' and 'Round Deviation Down to 1.0' options exist in Group 13 because precise values carry significance.

Technique: Disable all deviation rounding filters (you want raw, unfiltered multipliers). Scan TP labels on your detected and active setups. Look for exact whole numbers or exact 0.25 multiples: 2.0×, 3.0×, 5.0×, 1.75×, 2.25×, 2.75×, 5.5×.

Why these win: For the displacement ratio to land on a clean number without rounding, the relationship between the displacement candle's momentum and the preceding candle's range had to be precise. Precision in underlying price action tends to correlate with cleaner, more structured moves.

When to apply: When multiple setups compete for your attention, the one with 3.0× deviation is generally a stronger candidate than the one with 2.73×. Not a guarantee; a meaningful tiebreaker.

▶Navigating Multiple Setups in a Price LegHOW & WHY

Rule of thumb: Avoid taking the fifth setup or higher within a single directional price leg. By then, the probability you're near the end of the leg is significantly higher than the probability of continuation.

Why: Price legs in ICT methodology are structured moves with finite energy. Each successive displacement depletes remaining momentum.

Gauge missed entry probability: Disable 2nd Chance Setups (Group 5). Enable the Missed Entries row on the Monitor (Group 32). Check Missed Entry percentage for your intended direction. If 10 setups in a leg and missed % is ~10%+, at least one will fail to trigger its entry.

Counterintuitive insight: The first setup in a local leg is the highest-probability of all setups to be left behind as a Missed. Actually a positive signal — it means the displacement was strong enough that price moved too quickly for the entry to fill. Genuine institutional flow, not noise.

♾️ THE PHILOSOPHY
The indicator is infinitely customisable by design. These hacks exist because the product of enough small workflow optimisations is dramatically more time, insight, and profitability than the settings themselves could ever deliver in isolation. Pick a hack. Try it for a week. Add another.

REFERENCE

Glossary

Alphabetical reference of every term used in the manual. Use this when a term in a chapter isn't immediately familiar, or to confirm your understanding of a specific concept.

A

TermDefinition
Active TradeA detected setup whose entry has filled and is currently being tracked against TP/SL/trail. Marked 🤞🏻.
Adjustable RA Group 11 SL Method where the stop distance is a configurable fraction of the target distance. R:R 0.33 = 3:1 reward-to-risk.
Advanced TrailA Group 11 trailing-SL method that waits for the trade to reach a trigger profit (in R) before trailing, then continuously trails at the configured Trail R:R distance.
Asian Session19:00–02:00 EST killzone. Range-building session; often produces liquidity pools that London Open sweeps.
Auto CalcDefault Target and SL method that uses the detected model's structural boundaries and displacement momentum to position TP and SL automatically.
Auto-Detect Point ValueGroup 4 setting that reads the exchange's native point value. ON for CME futures, OFF for crypto/equities/FX.
Average Realised PnLNet Realised PnL ÷ Closed Trades. What a typical trade earned. Toggle between Average and Median via Group 3.
Avg R:RAverage ratio of target distance to stop distance across all trades. Pure measure of how ambitious your targets are relative to your stops.

B

TermDefinition
B.E. TrailGroup 11 SL method that waits for the trade to reach a trigger profit (in R), then moves the SL to breakeven and stops there (no further trailing).
Bars To ScanGroup 3 setting controlling how many bars the Lookback Period scans. The most impactful single setting on the Monitor.
BOS (Break of Structure)Price breaks a swing high in an uptrend (or swing low in a downtrend). Continuation signal.
Breaker BlockAn Order Block that has been displaced through in the opposite direction. A failed OB that now acts as support/resistance in the inverted role.
BSL (Buy-Side Liquidity)Equal highs on the chart — multiple swing highs at nearly the same price. Resting stop orders above the level make it a liquidity pool.
Buying PowerMargin × Leverage. The total dollar value of the position the simulator assumes you could open.

C

TermDefinition
Circuit Breaker (Daily)Group 28 safety feature that halts new detections after cumulative daily loss hits the limit. Active trades continue; new setups marked 🛑. Resets 18:00 EST.
Closed TradeA trade that has reached a terminal state — Win, Loss, EOD Close, or Missed.
Contracts / QtyGroup 4 setting for the number of units traded. Futures: contract count. Crypto: typically 1.0 with Margin holding the dollar value.
Custom Date RangeGroup 3 Sim Mode that scans between two fixed dates set in Group 44. Alternative to the rolling Lookback Period.

D

TermDefinition
Detected SetupA setup the simulator has identified but whose entry hasn't filled yet. Marked 🎱.
Deviation MultiplierHow far the TP sits from entry, expressed as a multiple of the displacement candle's body size. Auto Calc derives this automatically; Fixed Devs lets you set it manually.
Direction (Trade Direction)Group 5 setting — Both / Long / Short. Restricts which directions the simulator will detect setups for.
DisplacementA strong directional candle where the close exceeds the prior candle's high (bullish) or low (bearish) with meaningful body-to-body momentum. The seed pattern for all ICT structure.
Double TP SweepWhen 2nd Chance is ON and a re-detected setup also has its TP swept before the entry fills. The setup is permanently retired. Marked ⛔️ Double TP Sweep.
Drawdown (DD)The negative PnL a trade experiences while open (Single Max/Avg DD) or the fall from Peak Max Realised (Peak Max DD).

E

TermDefinition
Early OffsetGroup 9 Split Method that shifts partials closer to entry by a configurable percentage (0–50%). Captures profit faster.
Entry LevelGroup 6 setting for where within the detected model's range the entry sits. Expressed as a Fibonacci-style decimal 0.0 (near edge) to 1.0 (far edge).
EOD Auto-CloseGroup 5 setting that force-closes all active trades at session end. Closures marked ⏰. Essential for session-based markets; disable for 24/7 markets like crypto.
EquilibriumThe 0.5 Entry Level — mid-range retracement.
ExpectancyMathematical expected value per trade: (Win Rate × Avg Win) − (Loss Rate × Avg Loss). Must be positive for a profitable system.

F

TermDefinition
Fair Value Gap (FVG)A three-candle pattern with an imbalance gap between candle 1's high and candle 3's low (bullish), formed after a displacement. Represents unfilled price delivery.
Filter Trades By HTFGroup 5 setting that restricts detections to those aligned with the HTF Scout's most recent unmitigated FVG or OB bias.
Final TPThe outermost TP level — the full target of a trade. Distinct from TP1/TP2/TP3 which are partial levels.
Final TPs Hit %Percentage of winning trades that reached the full final TP (as opposed to closing via trail or breakeven-at-TP1).
Fixed DevsGroup 7 Target Method where the TP distance is a user-specified multiple of the displacement candle's body size.
Freeze Closed TradesGroup 45 performance setting that stops redrawing closed-trade visuals. Saves memory on long lookbacks.
Front RunEntry or TP option that places the order outside the PD array range (±0.1425 beyond the edge). Higher fill rate at the cost of edge.

G

TermDefinition
Global ValueGroup 4 setting — Dollars or Ticks. Switches all PnL calculations between fiat currency and pure chart distance.
Gross Profit / Gross LossSum of all winning trade PnL (Profit) and sum of all losing trade PnL (Loss, as absolute value). Profit Factor = Gross Profit ÷ Gross Loss.

H

TermDefinition
HTF IPDAHigher Timeframe Interbank Price Delivery Algorithm. The 20/40/60/80/100-period highs and lows from a configured higher timeframe. Macro reference levels.
HTF ScoutThe indicator's sandboxed higher-timeframe scanner. Tracks the most recent unmitigated HTF FVG and HTF OB independently of the main engine.

I

TermDefinition
i-FVG (Inversion FVG)An FVG that has been violated — price closed through the full gap in the opposite direction. The invalidated original is now treated as an inverted entry zone.
IPDA TimeframeGroup 5 setting controlling which HTF is used for the IPDA projection. Daily is standard for futures.

K

TermDefinition
KillzoneAn ICT-defined high-activity session window. Group 29 lets you restrict detections to enabled killzones. All times EST.

L

TermDefinition
LeverageGroup 4 multiplier applied to Margin to produce Buying Power. 1.0 for futures and spot; match exchange for crypto margin.
Liquidity SweepWhen price takes out a prior swing level, triggering resting stop orders. A frequent institutional manoeuvre before the 'real' move.
London Close10:00–12:00 EST killzone. Post-NY-open reversal zone.
London Session02:00–05:00 EST killzone. First major session; often sets daily range.
Long OnlyTrade Direction = Long Only (Group 5). Disables short detections entirely.
Lookback PeriodGroup 3 Sim Mode that scans the most recent N bars (controlled by Bars To Scan). Creates a rolling window that moves with new bars.

M

TermDefinition
MacroA narrow 20–30 minute window within a killzone where specific algorithmic institutional events concentrate flow. Group 30.
MarginGroup 4 setting for your collateral amount. Combined with Leverage to produce Buying Power.
Max Avg SL UnrealisedThe highest peak-profit any losing trade reached before reversing to SL. Key diagnostic for stop placement quality.
Max DDThe deepest drawdown a single trade reached before closing (or cumulative drop from Peak Max Realised).
MedianMiddle value when results are sorted. Resistant to outliers. Alternative to Average in Group 3's Avg/Median setting.
Min R:R FilterGroup 15/26 filter that rejects setups below a minimum risk-to-reward ratio. The cleanest expected-value-preservation filter.
MirroringGroup 5 setting that copies one direction's full configuration to the other. None / Shorts Mirror Longs / Longs Mirror Shorts.
Missed EntryA detected setup where TP was reached before entry filled. Marked ⛔️ Missed. Counted separately from Win/Loss.
ModelOne of the five ICT entry models: Displacement, FVG, i-FVG, Order Block, Breaker Block. Group 6/17 selects which are enabled.
Model LeaderboardGroup 1 Monitor page that ranks all ten model/direction combinations by Profit Factor. The primary tool for pruning decisions.
MSS (Market Structure Shift)Price breaks a swing in the opposite direction of the prevailing trend. Reversal signal.

N

TermDefinition
2nd ChanceA Group 5 mechanic where a setup whose TP was hit before entry filled is automatically re-detected if the same structural pattern reappears. See also: Double TP Sweep.
Net PnLCumulative realised profit/loss across the lookback. The bottom line.
Net R:RGross Profit (in R) ÷ Gross Loss (in R). Dollar-weighted, reflecting actual outcomes rather than configuration.
NY AM07:00–10:00 EST killzone. The highest-volume session; where most ICT methodology is calibrated.
NY Lunch12:00–13:00 EST. Typically low volume; most traders disable this killzone.
NY PM13:00–16:00 EST. Second NY session; volume picks up into close.

O

TermDefinition
OB Candle RangeGroup 16 setting — Wicks or Bodies. Whether the OB zone uses the candle's full wick range (wider, more fills) or just body (tighter, better R:R).
OB Liquidity SweepGroup 16 filter requiring the OB candle to have swept a prior swing before the OB qualifies.
Optimal Trade Entry (OTE)ICT methodology's classical OTE level — the 0.618 retracement. Available in the Entry Level dropdown as a one-click preset. The indicator's default of 0.34 is a different, fill-rate-optimised level that front-runs the 0.5 mid-point (Consequent Encroachment of FVG, Mean Threshold of OB). Both are valid choices; the trader picks which suits their style.
Order Block (OB)The candle immediately before a displacement. Bullish OB = last down-close before a bullish displacement. Bearish OB = last up-close before a bearish displacement.

P

TermDefinition
Partial ProfitA fraction of position closed at an intermediate level between entry and final TP. Configured in Group 9/20.
PD ArrayPremium and Discount Array — ICT umbrella term for any structural feature where price delivers between states. FVG, i-FVG, OB, Breaker, Wickless, Tailless, Swing H/L, BSL/SSL, HTF IPDA, HTF FVG, HTF OB.
PD Array Matrix (SL)Group 11 SL Method that places SL behind the nearest (or Nth) unmitigated PD array of the types enabled in Group 12.
PD Array Matrix (Target)Group 7 Target Method that places TP at the nearest (or Nth nearest) unmitigated PD array of the types enabled in Group 8.
Peak Max DDThe deepest drawdown from the all-time-high cumulative realised balance. The account-level pain metric. Always tracked in USD.
Peak Max RealisedThe all-time-high cumulative realised balance across the lookback. Always tracked in USD.
Performance ModeGroup 45 setting that reduces visual update frequency to speed up TradingView on heavy configurations.
Profit Factor (PF)Gross Profit ÷ Gross Loss. The top-level edge metric. 1.0 = breakeven; 1.5+ = meaningful edge; 2.0+ = strong.

R

TermDefinition
R:R (Risk-to-Reward)Ratio of target distance to stop distance. 3:1 R:R means the target is 3× as far from entry as the stop.
Recent TradesGroup 1 Monitor page showing a chronological list of the most recent closed trades. The only page that preserves sequence.
Risk BackGroup 9 Size Method that sizes TP1 to secure a specific multiple of trade risk. 100% = recover breakeven on risk at TP1.

S

TermDefinition
Session Auto-CloseSee EOD Auto-Close.
Setup Auto CalcGroup 11 default SL Method. Places SL based on the detected model's structural boundary.
SL BufferGroup 14 modifier adding 50% of base risk as a cushion beyond the calculated SL. Protects against noise wicks.
SSL (Sell-Side Liquidity)Equal lows — mirror of BSL. Resting stop orders below make it a liquidity pool.
Standard TrailGroup 11 SL Method that trails price at the configured distance from the moment the trade opens.
Swing H/LTarget or SL method that uses the nearest confirmed swing high (for longs' target / shorts' stop) or swing low. Requires Swing Strength calibration.
Swing StrengthGroup 5 setting — number of bars left and right required to confirm a pivot as a swing. Affects BOS, MSS, Swing-based targets/stops, and HTF IPDA calculations.

T

TermDefinition
Tailless CandleA candle with no (or minimal) lower wick. Signals undelivered sell-side liquidity — the opposite of wickless.
Target Array DepthGroup 7 setting controlling which array is targeted for structural methods. Depth 1 = nearest; Depth 5 = skip four, target the fifth.
Target MethodGroup 7 setting for how TP is calculated. Auto Calc / Fixed Devs / Dollar $ / Ticks / Wickless / Swing H/L / HTF / PD Array Matrix.
TP ExpansionGroup 13 modifier multiplying TP distance by 1.5× / 2× / 3× / 4× / 5×. Ambitious target extension.
TP Hard CapGroup 28 absolute ceiling on TP distance in dollars or ticks. Prevents unrealistic targets. Capped trades marked 💰.
TP1 Secure (B.E. SL)Group 13 modifier that moves the SL to breakeven when TP1 is reached. Transforms the outcome distribution of the system.
Trade DirectionSee Direction.
Trade IDUnique identifier assigned to each setup (e.g. L-FVG-0417a). Appears on chart labels, in Recent Trades, and in alert messages — the thread that ties all references to a trade together.
Trades To TrackGroup 3 cap on how many trades can be held in memory at once. Default 5,000.
Trail R TriggerGroup 11 setting for trailing SL methods. The profit threshold (in R-multiples) before trailing activates.
Trail R:RGroup 11 setting. The distance the trailing SL maintains from price, as a fraction of 1R.

U

TermDefinition
Unrealised PnLThe current floating profit/loss of an active trade (not yet closed).

W

TermDefinition
Wickless CandleA candle with no (or minimal) upper wick. Signals undelivered buy-side liquidity; price is often drawn to revisit the wick-absent high.
Win RateWins ÷ (Wins + Losses). EOD closes and Missed setups are excluded from the ratio.
